description:
Testability re-review of the 2a2ba65 ADR-018 evolve pass identified 20 gaps. Filling them in a follow-up evolve pass with user defaults on design decisions.

Items covered:
A. Grants ≤ role rule table (strict interpretation)
B. Audit retention on tenant drop (configurable per tenant, default archive 7y)
C. Op-to-HTTP-method mapping table (schema + db create/drop = admin)
D. JWT failure mode → status table (aud mismatch = 403)
E. Auto-bootstrap concurrency (SQL unique constraint + ON CONFLICT)
F. Tailscale auto-provision race (tx + retry pattern)
G. Grants v1 JSON Schema (concrete, inline in ADR-018)
H. aud single-value rejection of array form
I. Signing key rotation vulnerability callout
J. 4-6 week estimate removal from ADR
K. Observability envelope for auth failures
L. --no-auth + tenant URL behavior (in-memory per-URL routing)
M. Pre-release API stability banner in Vision and README
N. Authority-order fix: walk ADR-018 up into PRD §10 + §18 and Vision
O. L1 invariants INV-017 through INV-022
P. L3 properties PROP-009 through PROP-011
Q. L6 contract test additions for new routes
R. Negative scenario matrix SCN-014
S. Default-tenant bootstrap test scenario
T. Audit attribution stability test

All design decisions use defaults presented in the previous review turn. User approved with 'Go with your defaults and perform the spec evolution and commit.'

description:
Wave 2, prerequisite bead. Unblocks the two skipped playwright tests in tests/e2e/tenant-admin.spec.ts (credential issue + tenant member add) and closes the admin-UX hole where a human cannot create a user row without OIDC federation.

## Problem

Today the only path that creates a row in the `users` table is `upsert_user_identity` (called by OIDC federation / JWT verification). There is no REST endpoint that provisions a bare user, so:

- POST /control/tenants/{id}/credentials fails with `not_a_tenant_member` unless the target_user is already a member, which requires the user to already exist in `users`.
- PUT /control/tenants/{id}/members/{user_id} fails with FOREIGN KEY constraint because tenant_users.user_id → users.id.

Both are blocked on the same missing capability: a way to insert a `users` row via HTTP.

## Endpoints to add

Under `/control/` prefix in crates/axon-server/src/control_plane_routes.rs:

1. **POST /control/users** — create a bare user row.
   - Request body: `{ "display_name": "Alice", "email": "alice@example.com" | null }`
   - Response 201: `{ "id": "<uuid>", "display_name": "Alice", "email": "...", "created_at_ms": 123 }`
   - Requires deployment-admin.
   - Generates a fresh UserId via `UserId::generate()`.
   - Stores via a new storage adapter method or via raw SQL path (see note below).

2. **GET /control/users/list** — list full user rows.
   - (Note: GET /control/users is already taken by the role-assignment listing. Use a new path, not a query param.)
   - Response: `{ "users": [{ id, display_name, email, created_at_ms, suspended_at_ms }, ...] }`
   - Requires deployment-admin.
   - Backed by a new StorageAdapter method like `list_users() -> Vec<User>`.

3. **DELETE /control/users/{id}** — soft-delete via `suspended_at_ms` (or hard-delete, caller's choice; soft is safer).
   - Not in the same path namespace as `/control/users/{login}` (role assignments) since that's keyed on login string, not UUID. Keep them separate — document the distinction in the route comments.

## Storage layer changes

`crates/axon-storage/src/adapter.rs`: add trait methods
  - `fn create_user(&self, id: &UserId, display_name: &str, email: Option<&str>) -> Result<User, AxonError>;`
  - `fn list_users(&self) -> Result<Vec<User>, AxonError>;`
  - `fn suspend_user(&self, id: &UserId) -> Result<bool, AxonError>;`

Implement in sqlite.rs (insert into `users` table), memory.rs, and postgres.rs. See the existing `upsert_user_identity` for how `users` rows are inserted — the schema is `(id, display_name, email, created_at_ms, suspended_at_ms)`.

## Tests

New integration test file `crates/axon-server/tests/control_users_provision_test.rs` mirroring control_databases_test.rs pattern (MemoryStorageAdapter + ControlPlaneState + TestServer):
- POST /control/users creates a row, returns 201 with id
- GET /control/users/list returns the created user
- POST with duplicate display_name — accepted (no uniqueness constraint)
- DELETE sets suspended_at_ms
- Non-admin gets 403

## Out of scope (next bead)

- UI wiring (wave2-users-provision-ui bead)
- Email verification or invite flows
- Tenant-scoped user listing (this is deployment-wide)
- Renaming the existing role-assignments endpoint for clarity (would break compat)

## Files to touch

- crates/axon-server/src/control_plane_routes.rs — new handlers + route registration
- crates/axon-storage/src/adapter.rs — trait methods
- crates/axon-storage/src/sqlite.rs — sqlite impl
- crates/axon-storage/src/memory.rs — memory impl
- crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs — postgres impl
- crates/axon-server/tests/control_users_provision_test.rs — NEW

## Verification

- cargo test --workspace green
- cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings clean

description:
Clean-break migration from the legacy X-Axon-Database header + unprefixed routes to /tenants/:t/databases/:d/ path-based routing. This is the biggest bead in the ADR-018 rollout. Scope it tight and follow the plan below.

## Scope — exactly what to do, in order

### Phase 1: audit the surface (5 min)

Read these files first to see the current state:
- crates/axon-server/src/gateway.rs — 5947 lines; contains the data-plane router builder at line ~2718. This is the main target.
- crates/axon-server/src/gateway.rs line 609 — resolve_handler function that reads X-Axon-Database
- crates/axon-api/src/request.rs — the request struct that surfaces X-Axon-Database
- crates/axon-server/tests/api_contract.rs — existing integration tests against the data plane
- crates/axon-server/tests/graphql_mutations.rs — GraphQL tests (probably minimal URL usage; the tenant/database nest is added in D4)

### Phase 2: rewrite the router (the heavy lifting)

In crates/axon-server/src/gateway.rs, find the data_plane_routes() (or equivalent) function that returns the axum Router containing all /entities/*, /collections/*, /links/*, /transactions, /audit/*, /snapshot, /traverse/*, etc. routes.

Wrap the entire router inside a parent router that nests it under /tenants/:tenant/databases/:database/:

    pub fn data_plane_routes(state: AppState) -> Router {
        let inner = Router::new()
            .route("/entities/:collection/:id", post(create_entity))
            // ... all existing routes unchanged
            .route("/transactions", post(commit_transaction))
            // etc.
            .with_state(state);

        Router::new().nest("/tenants/:tenant/databases/:database", inner)
    }

The inner router's handlers stay exactly the same — they read entities/collections/etc. from URL params as before. What changes is the PARENT NEST that forces the prefix.

### Phase 3: resolve_handler uses path params, not header

Find the function that currently reads X-Axon-Database:

    fn resolve_handler(headers: &HeaderMap, ...) -> TenantHandler {
        let db_name = headers.get("X-Axon-Database")...
        ...
    }

Replace it with a middleware layer (or an axum extractor) that reads the :tenant and :database path params installed by the nest above. The D1 path_router_layer already extracts these into ResolvedPath — reuse it:

    use crate::path_router::ResolvedPath;

    async fn resolve_handler_from_path(
        Extension(path): Extension<ResolvedPath>,
        state: Extension<AppState>,
    ) -> TenantHandler {
        // look up (path.tenant, path.database) via state.database_router (from D2)
        // or fall back to legacy TenantRouter with path.database as the slug
    }

Actually, for this bead the simpler move is: keep the existing TenantHandler dispatch mechanism, but change resolve_handler to read the :database axum path param (via axum::extract::Path) instead of the header. We don't NEED the DatabaseRouter integration yet — that's more surgery and risks the bead.

Concretely: turn resolve_handler into:

    pub async fn resolve_handler_path(
        Path((_tenant, database)): Path<(String, String)>,
        State(state): State<AppState>,
    ) -> TenantHandler {
        // Use the existing TenantRouter::get_or_create_any with `database` as the slug.
        // Tenant IS used for future isolation but for this bead we just pass through.
    }

Each data-plane handler that currently does `let handler = resolve_handler(&headers, ...)` changes to `let handler = resolve_handler_path(path, state).await` and takes the tenant/database from axum Path.

If that's too much handler rewriting (there are ~40 handlers), the alternative is a SINGLE middleware layer that extracts (tenant, database) once and installs the resolved TenantHandler into request extensions — all downstream handlers read the handler from extensions instead of re-resolving. This is the preferred approach: less churn.

### Phase 4: delete the header path

After every handler reads (tenant, database) from the path, delete:
- The X-Axon-Database header read in gateway.rs:609 region
- The header constant if any
- Any reference in crates/axon-api/src/request.rs

After deletion, `git grep -rn X-Axon-Database crates/ sdk/` MUST return zero lines.

### Phase 5: rewrite the existing tests

crates/axon-server/tests/api_contract.rs — find every request that hits an unprefixed path like POST /entities/foo/bar and rewrite to POST /tenants/default/databases/default/entities/foo/bar. Also remove any X-Axon-Database header set calls.

crates/axon-server/tests/graphql_mutations.rs — find the /graphql path and leave it for D4 (GraphQL path nesting is D4's job; this bead only handles the REST path migration).

crates/axon-server/tests/lifecycle_test.rs, snapshot_test.rs, mcp_contract.rs — check and update if they hit unprefixed paths.

Use sed or similar batch replace for the common case: prepend `/tenants/default/databases/default` to every hardcoded path that starts with `/entities/`, `/collections/`, `/links`, `/traverse/`, `/audit/`, `/snapshot`, `/transactions`, `/collections/*/query`, `/collections/*/schema`.

### Phase 6: add the L6 golden route contract test

crates/axon-server/tests/l6_route_contract_test.rs (NEW):

    //! L6 contract: every data-plane route is prefixed with /tenants/:t/databases/:d/

    use axum::Router;

    #[test]
    fn every_data_plane_route_has_tenant_database_prefix() {
        // Build the router exactly as gateway.rs does, then walk its routes.
        // axum doesn't expose the route inventory directly, so instead:
        // 1. Compile a list of known reserved prefixes: /health, /metrics, /ui, /control, /favicon.ico
        // 2. For every path in a handcrafted "expected routes" list, assert the path starts with either /tenants/ or one of the reserved prefixes.
        // 3. Use an integration test that hits each known path without auth and asserts it returns 401/403/404 (never 200) for unprefixed paths.
    }

Alternatively, if axum exposes a route listing in the test environment, walk it programmatically. If not, maintain a golden list of expected paths and assert the list matches what the router exposes.

Separately add a literal grep-based test at compile time:

    #[test]
    fn no_x_axon_database_references() {
        let output = std::process::Command::new("git")
            .args(["grep", "-l", "X-Axon-Database", "crates/", "sdk/"])
            .output()
            .expect("git grep");
        let matches = String::from_utf8_lossy(&output.stdout);
        assert!(
            matches.trim().is_empty(),
            "X-Axon-Database still referenced in:\n{}",
            matches
        );
    }

This is a quick L6-like contract gate.

### Phase 7: SCN-011 cross-tenant isolation scenario

crates/axon-server/tests/scn_011_cross_tenant_isolation_test.rs (NEW):

Set up the server in --no-auth mode for simplicity. Hit:
1. POST /tenants/acme/databases/orders/collections/orders/create → create collection
2. POST /tenants/acme/databases/orders/entities/orders/order-1 → create entity
3. GET /tenants/acme/databases/orders/entities/orders/order-1 → reads back (200)
4. GET /tenants/beta/databases/orders/entities/orders/order-1 → must return 404 (different tenant, same database name)
5. GET /tenants/acme/databases/customers/entities/orders/order-1 → must return 404 (different database)

This proves the per-URL namespace (combined with E2's no-auth synthesized identity) is fully isolated.

## Out of scope — defer to follow-ups

- GraphQL path nesting → D4
- SDK rewriting to use /tenants/:t/databases/:d/ → G1
- UI rewriting → H1
- Any changes to control-plane routes (stay at /control/*)
- Any changes to /health, /metrics, /ui, /favicon.ico

## Context you must know

- D1 landed path_router_layer + ResolvedPath — reuse
- D2 landed DatabaseRouter (not used in this bead's handlers; legacy TenantRouter stays for now)
- B1-B4 + C1-C3 + E1-E2 + F1 are all landed — do NOT touch them
- gateway.rs:2718 is the route builder where the nest happens
- axon-api/src/request.rs has the request type that reads the header
- Existing tests are the authoritative reference for what must keep working — after your rewrite, every existing test must still pass (just with new paths)

## Files to touch (high traffic — be surgical)

- crates/axon-server/src/gateway.rs — ROUTER REWRITE (nest under /tenants/:t/databases/:d/), delete header read, add path-param resolver
- crates/axon-api/src/request.rs — remove X-Axon-Database references
- crates/axon-server/tests/api_contract.rs — batch path rewrite
- crates/axon-server/tests/lifecycle_test.rs — batch path rewrite
- crates/axon-server/tests/snapshot_test.rs — batch path rewrite
- crates/axon-server/tests/mcp_contract.rs — batch path rewrite if needed
- crates/axon-server/tests/l6_route_contract_test.rs — NEW
- crates/axon-server/tests/scn_011_cross_tenant_isolation_test.rs — NEW

## Files NOT to touch

- crates/axon-server/src/tenant_router.rs — legacy
- crates/axon-server/src/database_router.rs — D2 primitive, not wired yet
- crates/axon-server/src/auth.rs — legacy Tailscale path
- crates/axon-server/src/auth_pipeline.rs — B1
- crates/axon-server/tests/graphql_mutations.rs — D4's job
- crates/axon-server/tests/auth_pipeline_test.rs + auth_pipeline_integration_test.rs + auth_rejection_matrix.rs + control_tenants_test.rs + control_databases_test.rs + control_credentials_test.rs + federation_test.rs + path_router_test.rs + bootstrap_test.rs + no_auth_test.rs + database_router_test.rs + tenant_users_test.rs + attribution_test.rs — these all must continue to pass unchanged
- crates/axon-storage/* — no storage changes
- crates/axon-core/* — no type changes

## Verification

- git grep -rn X-Axon-Database crates/ sdk/ → zero lines
- cargo test --workspace (every test, including the rewritten ones and the new L6/SCN-011)
- cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings clean

## If you hit a blocker

If the router rewrite reveals a non-trivial tangling you cannot unwind in one pass (e.g., a circular dep introduced by the nest), write no_changes_rationale.txt explaining exactly what blocked and split the work into a targeted follow-up bead. Do NOT commit a half-migrated state.

description:
Extend audit entries with the ADR-018 attribution tuple: {user_id, tenant_id, jti, auth_method}. Additive and backward-compatible: the existing `actor: String` field stays untouched so legacy callers keep working.

The tenant-delete archive path is SCOPED OUT — it requires a cold-storage subsystem that doesn't exist yet. This bead does the immutable in-log attribution + tests that prove attribution survives user/credential mutations. A follow-up bead can wire the archive path when cold storage is specced.

## Scope

### 1. AuditAttribution struct in axon-audit/src/entry.rs

Add alongside AuditEntry:

    /// Attribution tuple carried on every audit entry that was authenticated
    /// via the ADR-018 pipeline. Captures the stable identity AND the credential
    /// used to authenticate, so post-hoc forensic queries can reconstruct who
    /// did what even after renames, suspensions, or revocations.
    ///
    /// Field semantics (ADR-018 Implementation Notes):
    ///
    /// - `user_id` — stable UUID of the User record. Does NOT change across
    ///   display_name/email edits.
    /// - `tenant_id` — the tenant the action took place within. Stable UUID.
    /// - `jti` — the JWT credential ID that authenticated this request, when
    ///   the auth path was JWT. `None` for Tailscale whois synthetic claims
    ///   and for --no-auth mode.
    /// - `auth_method` — which path produced the identity: "jwt", "tailscale",
    ///   or "no-auth". Stable string matched by the observability envelope.
    #[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq, Serialize, Deserialize)]
    pub struct AuditAttribution {
        pub user_id: String,       // serialized UserId/Uuid
        pub tenant_id: String,     // serialized TenantId/Uuid
        #[serde(skip_serializing_if = "Option::is_none")]
        pub jti: Option<String>,
        pub auth_method: String,
    }

Do NOT pull UserId/TenantId from axon-core into axon-audit — axon-audit currently has no dependency on axon-core (except for CollectionId/EntityId from axon-core::id). Adding a heavy dep would be scope creep. Store as strings; the middleware serializes UUIDs to strings on its side.

### 2. Extend AuditEntry with an optional attribution field

    #[serde(skip_serializing_if = "Option::is_none")]
    pub attribution: Option<AuditAttribution>,

The existing `actor: String` field stays — legacy callers (--no-auth mode, Tailscale whois, test harnesses) still work. When attribution IS present, it is the authoritative post-hoc identity; the `actor` field is then a human-readable display string (like the user's email at write time, which may drift).

Update AuditEntry::new to accept Option<AuditAttribution> as a new parameter — that breaks callers. **Instead**, add a builder method:

    impl AuditEntry {
        pub fn with_attribution(mut self, attribution: AuditAttribution) -> Self {
            self.attribution = Some(attribution);
            self
        }
    }

All existing callers continue to work. New middleware-aware callers chain `.with_attribution(...)` after `AuditEntry::new(...)`.

### 3. Immutability proof

Audit entries are already append-only in axon-audit (they live in an AuditLog that never mutates). Add a unit test that:
- Creates an AuditEntry with attribution
- Serializes it
- Deserializes it
- Asserts every attribution field round-trips byte-identical
- Then creates a second entry with the same attribution (simulating what the middleware would do) and asserts both entries carry independent-but-equal attribution (no shared mutation).

### 4. Test: attribution survives user mutation

Integration test in crates/axon-audit/tests/attribution_test.rs (NEW):

- write_entry_with_attribution — build an AuditEntry with AuditAttribution { user_id: "uuid-alice", tenant_id: "uuid-acme", jti: Some("uuid-cred-123"), auth_method: "jwt" }. Append to a test AuditLog. Query back by ID. Assert attribution is intact.

- attribution_persists_after_rename_simulation — since axon-audit has no direct link to the users table, simulate the "rename" by mutating nothing on the entry itself. Re-query and assert the attribution is still intact. This proves the entry is physically immutable.

- attribution_persists_after_revocation_simulation — same pattern, with jti still pointing at a credential that would later be revoked. The audit entry still has the jti.

### 5. INV-022 primitive axon-sim workload (OUT OF SCOPE — file follow-up)

The full INV-022 workload — which mutates users, revokes credentials, and asserts attribution stays intact across those mutations end-to-end — requires a full middleware-wired server. That's not this bead. File a follow-up bead after D3 lands.

## Out of scope

- Cold storage for tenant delete archive path (requires a new subsystem)
- INV-022 axon-sim workload (requires D3)
- Modifying the legacy actor field behavior
- Wiring attribution into the middleware (that's the B1/D-phase integration, to be done when the full request pipeline lands)
- axon-core dep: do NOT add it to axon-audit; attribution fields are stringly-typed at this layer

## Context

- Existing AuditEntry in crates/axon-audit/src/entry.rs has `actor: String` (legacy) + `metadata: HashMap`
- axon-audit has no dependency on axon-core (except for CollectionId/EntityId imports); do not add axon-core::auth::UserId here
- The middleware (B1/D3) will be responsible for constructing AuditAttribution and calling .with_attribution() when B1's verify pipeline or B3's federation produces a ResolvedIdentity

## Files to touch

- crates/axon-audit/src/entry.rs — add AuditAttribution + attribution field on AuditEntry + with_attribution builder
- crates/axon-audit/src/lib.rs — re-export AuditAttribution
- crates/axon-audit/tests/attribution_test.rs — NEW

## Files NOT to touch

- crates/axon-audit/src/log.rs — the log's append/query semantics don't need to change; attribution is inline on the entry
- crates/axon-audit/src/cdc.rs — CDC stream should naturally pick up the new optional field via serde
- crates/axon-core/* — no changes
- crates/axon-storage/* — audit storage is in axon-audit, not axon-storage
- crates/axon-server/* — wiring comes later

## Verification

- cargo test -p axon-audit (existing tests must still pass — additive schema)
- cargo test -p axon-audit --test attribution_test (new tests)
- cargo test --workspace (no regressions elsewhere)
- cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings clean

Phase 2 of axon-41f48f99 (closed Phase 1 surfaced backend-filtered group totals only). Add an inline expandable target-data preview to the Links tab that renders neighbor entity fields through the shared redaction primitive established by axon-c3895a14.

Required design:

2. Render the target-entity payload (already returned by traverseLinks GraphQL query as edges[].node.data) through redactValue() with the target collection's redactedFields list, then JsonTree (which already marks [redacted] placeholders with data-testid='redacted-field').
3. Cover with a policy-enforcement.spec.ts test that asserts the contractor's view of an invoice neighbor has commercial_terms and amount_cents redacted in the inline preview (sensitive value never appears in DOM).

acceptance: policy-enforcement.spec.ts 'contractor list surfaces policy-filtered totalCount and policy version' passes against the live axon-server (no GraphQL response mock). Root cause is identified and either fixed in the implicated layer (backend totalCount, UI binding, or fixture seeding) or — if it turns out to be a fixture-only timing issue — the test is hardened with a deterministic await on the seeded rows being visible.
description:
The policy-enforcement.spec.ts test 'contractor list surfaces
policy-filtered totalCount and policy version' (introduced by
axon-3fdfeb33) is failing on master. Reproduced on commit 1ff682c by
running the test against a clean baseline (no other in-flight changes):

  AXON_E2E_STORAGE=sqlite scripts/test-ui-e2e-docker.sh \
      --grep 'contractor list surfaces policy-filtered totalCount'

Failure (Playwright):

  > 174 |    expect(total).toBe(2);
        |                  ^
  Expected: 2
  Received: 0

The test seeds the SCN-017 fixture (two invoices, both with
assigned_contractor_id == SCN017_SUBJECTS.contractor) and asserts the
contractor sees the two assigned rows reflected in the
`entity-list-total-count` pill. Other tests in the same file that drive
the same fixture as the contractor — e.g. 'contractor sees redacted
commercial_terms across list, detail, and audit views' — DO render rows
for the contractor and pass, so it is not a wholesale identity-loading
or seeding issue. The discrepancy is specific to either:

  (a) the GraphQL totalCount path on the entities connection failing to
      apply the contractor-reads-assigned-invoices read rule (returning 0
      when row scans correctly return 2), or
  (b) the UI's `entity-list-total-count` rendering binding to the wrong
      source (e.g. raw collection entity_count rather than the
      GraphQL-filtered totalCount from the entities connection), or
  (c) a fixture-timing race where the totalCount read fires before the
      entity rows are visible to the index/storage adapter, but the
      sibling tests do not race because they act on row-level reads.

Investigation should start by capturing the GraphQL request the UI sends
on this route and the response's `totalCount` value (both with and
without the contractor x-axon-actor header), then walking down whichever
side mis-reports.

Files:
- ui/tests/e2e/policy-enforcement.spec.ts:190 (line in current master
  after axon-a525ef55 landed; was line 149 in axon-3fdfeb33)
- ui/src/routes/tenants/[tenant]/databases/[database]/collections/[name]/+page.svelte
  (entity-list-total-count testid)
- crates/axon-graphql/src/dynamic.rs (entities connection totalCount)

acceptance:
- [ ] JWT middleware installed in data-plane router as optional layer (runs when Authorization header present)
- [ ] JwtIssuer constructed at startup in serve.rs (dev: random key with warning)
- [ ] AuditAttribution populated on every audit write when ResolvedIdentity is present
- [ ] gRPC service.rs migrated from x-axon-database to composite x-axon-tenant-database
- [ ] gRPC service.rs gained optional JWT verify path
- [ ] cutover_jwt_test.rs integration test with real JWT end-to-end
- [ ] scripts/cutover-smoke.sh script runs end-to-end against dev server
- [ ] cargo test --workspace green
- [ ] cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings clean
- [ ] Legacy auth paths (--no-auth, --guest-role, Tailscale) still work unchanged
description:
Finish wiring the ADR-018 auth stack into the real request pipeline. After the smoke test on commit e83cf02, D3 already landed more than expected: `resolve_tenant_handler` composes `{tenant}:{database}` into the TenantRouter slug, giving real per-pair physical isolation. What remains is the three things that are *built but not installed*.

## What is already live (confirmed by smoke test)

- Path-based routing: /tenants/:t/databases/:d/... routed to per-pair SQLite files via composite slug
- GraphQL under the nested path
- Unprefixed REST + /graphql return 404
- HttpAxonClient in the SDK + the UI use the new paths
- Legacy X-Axon-Database header is gone from REST

## What is still NOT installed (the gap this bead closes)

### 1. JWT pipeline not enforced

B1 landed `auth_pipeline::jwt_verify_layer` + `JwtIssuer`. The real gateway router in `crates/axon-server/src/gateway.rs` does NOT install this layer. The server currently runs in legacy auth mode (Tailscale or --no-auth). Install the layer as an OPTIONAL middleware: if the request has an Authorization header, run the JWT pipeline; if not, fall back to legacy auth.

Wire point: in `build_router_with_auth` (or wherever the data-plane nested router is constructed), add `.layer(from_fn_with_state(state.clone(), jwt_verify_layer))` before the tenant_handler middleware. The layer needs access to a `JwtIssuer` and the `StorageAdapter`. Add them to the AppState struct.

Create the JwtIssuer at server startup in `serve.rs`. Read the signing key from an env var `AXON_JWT_KEY` (base64 HMAC secret); if unset, generate a random 32-byte key at startup and log a warning (dev mode). Production key management is a future concern.

The legacy auth path (crates/axon-server/src/auth.rs Identity + Role) still works for requests without an Authorization header — the JWT layer is a no-op pass-through when the header is absent. This preserves --no-auth, --guest-role, and Tailscale whois behavior exactly as today.

### 2. Audit attribution not populated

F1 added `AuditAttribution` as an optional field on `AuditEntry` plus the `with_attribution()` builder. No handler currently calls it. In the JWT middleware (step 1), after `ResolvedIdentity` is installed into the request extension, also expose a `current_attribution()` helper on AppState that the audit write sites can call.

Find every `AuditEntry::new(...)` call site in `crates/axon-server/src/gateway.rs` (there are probably 10-20) and chain `.with_attribution(current_attribution(&request))` when a ResolvedIdentity is present. When absent (legacy auth), leave the attribution as None — the existing `actor: String` field still works.

### 3. gRPC service.rs still reads x-axon-database

`crates/axon-server/src/service.rs:26` has `const AXON_DATABASE_HEADER: &str = "x-axon-database"` and `grpc_current_database` reads it from metadata. The gRPC service is entirely separate from the REST gateway and D3 did not touch it. Replace this header read with path-based extraction... except gRPC doesn't have URL paths in the same way. gRPC calls use service + method names, not paths. The database has to come from either:

(a) A metadata field (still a header-equivalent), OR
(b) A new field on every request proto

(a) is pragmatic. Rename the metadata header from `x-axon-database` to `x-axon-tenant-database` and carry a composite value `"{tenant}:{database}"`. This matches the REST composite slug. The gRPC service parses it and dispatches via the same `get_or_create_any` path.

Also add `x-axon-authorization` (or reuse the standard `authorization` metadata key) for JWT credentials. Run the JWT verify pipeline in the gRPC `check_auth` step before dispatching to the handler.

For this bead, the gRPC change is small:
- Rename the metadata constant
- Update `grpc_current_database` to parse the composite form
- Update the Rust + TS gRPC clients to send the composite form
- Add JWT auth path in `check_auth`

### 4. DatabaseRouter NOT swapped in (deferred — not needed right now)

D2 landed `DatabaseRouter` as a new primitive, but the gateway still uses `TenantRouter::get_or_create_any(slug)`. The smoke test confirmed this composite slug works correctly. Replacing TenantRouter with DatabaseRouter is a refactor that doesn't change behavior — defer it to a later cleanup bead. Not in this bead's scope.

## Scope summary

This bead does exactly three things:

1. Install B1's JWT middleware in the data-plane router, optional path (Authorization header present)
2. Populate F1's AuditAttribution at every audit write site when ResolvedIdentity is present
3. Migrate gRPC service.rs from x-axon-database header to the composite slug + optional JWT metadata

## Tests for this bead

### Integration test: crates/axon-server/tests/cutover_jwt_test.rs (NEW)

- create a JwtIssuer with a known key
- mint a JWT for a default user with grants on (default, default)
- start the in-process test server harness that gateway_test.rs uses
- send a request with Authorization: Bearer <jwt> to /tenants/default/databases/default/collections
- assert 200 (the JWT pipeline accepted + the handler saw ResolvedIdentity)

### Integration test: attribution populated

- send an entity-create request with a JWT
- query the audit log
- assert the audit entry has `attribution.user_id` set to the JWT sub

### gRPC test: composite slug

- send a CreateEntity gRPC request with metadata `x-axon-tenant-database: acme:orders`
- assert routing to the acme:orders database (check the storage file exists)

### Manual smoke test

Start the server, issue a JWT via `/control/tenants/default/credentials`, use the HttpAxonClient with the JWT, create an entity, verify the audit log has attribution. Write a short shell script under `scripts/cutover-smoke.sh` that does this end-to-end.

## Out of scope

- DatabaseRouter replacement — deferred
- OIDC federation — follow-up
- Signing key rotation — future ADR
- Removing legacy Identity/Role types from auth.rs — they still serve the --no-auth and Tailscale paths

## Files to touch

- crates/axon-server/src/gateway.rs — install JWT middleware, thread AuditAttribution into audit writes (10-20 sites)
- crates/axon-server/src/serve.rs — construct JwtIssuer at startup
- crates/axon-server/src/service.rs — rename header constant, parse composite, add JWT path
- crates/axon-server/tests/cutover_jwt_test.rs — NEW
- scripts/cutover-smoke.sh — NEW
- Possibly sdk/typescript/src/client.ts — update gRPC metadata key if touching gRPC SDK surface

## Files NOT to touch

- crates/axon-server/src/auth_pipeline.rs — B1 is canonical, just consume it
- crates/axon-server/src/auth.rs — legacy Tailscale path, out of scope for removal
- crates/axon-storage/* — no storage changes
- crates/axon-core/* — no type changes

## Verification

- cargo test -p axon-server (all prior tests green + new cutover_jwt_test)
- cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings clean
- scripts/cutover-smoke.sh runs end-to-end against a dev server

acceptance: Either: typed createXxx mutations return an already_exists error code when the id is already assigned in the same collection (matches commitTransaction op:create behavior); OR upsert is documented as intentional in the GraphQL schema with a createOrFail variant available for strict semantics. Nexiq's contract suite (tests/contract/axon-contract.spec.ts) duplicate-rejection assertions can be re-enabled and pass.
description:
<context-digest>
Filed from nexiq-d3f00d3f / nexiq-26b1c35d on 2026-04-29. The Nexiq contract suite
discovered that live Axon (v0.2.6, postgres-backed) accepts a second typed
createXxx GraphQL mutation with the same id and silently overwrites the entity's
data. Reproduction:

  curl -X POST .../tenants/:t/databases/:d/graphql -d '{
    "query": "mutation { createAccounts(id: \"X\", input: { name: \"A\", industry: \"...\" }) { entity { id version name } } }"
  }'
  -> { entity: { id: "X", version: 1, name: "A" } }

  curl -X POST .../graphql -d '{
    "query": "mutation { createAccounts(id: \"X\", input: { name: \"B\", industry: \"...\" }) { entity { id version name } } }"
  }'
  -> { entity: { id: "X", version: 1, name: "B" } }   <-- silent upsert, no error

The same shape on commitTransaction with op:create returns AlreadyExists, so the
duplicate-rejection semantics exist in the storage layer but the typed GraphQL
createXxx mutations bypass them.

Nexiq downstream impact: clients that intend create-only semantics (and would
expect AlreadyExistsError to flag a programmer/UI error) get silent overwrites
through the typed path. Nexiq's SPA already routes around this by writing
through commitTransaction, but the contract suite documenting the typed surface
had to t.skip() 10 duplicate-rejection assertions until this is resolved.
</context-digest>

Two paths:

A) Make typed createXxx return already_exists when the id is already present
   in the same collection. Mirror the storage-layer behavior. This is the
   principle of least surprise: 'create' should mean create-or-error, not
   upsert.

B) If upsert-on-create is actually intended, document it loudly in the
   GraphQL schema description fields and add a separate createOrFail variant
   for callers that want strict semantics. Nexiq would migrate to the strict
   variant.

A is preferred — matches the rest of the API and the storage layer.
title: axon-server: typed createXxx mutations should reject duplicate IDs (currently upsert)

acceptance: A GraphQL parity suite runs without human intervention against a live in-memory server and covers create/read/update/patch/delete, filtered list/sort/pagination/count, generic entity/entities, collection metadata, aggregations, link create/delete, relationship fields forward/reverse, linkCandidates, neighbors, lifecycle transition/validTransitions, auditLog, commitTransaction/idempotency, subscriptions, auth/RBAC, tenant/database isolation, CORS-relevant headers, and error shape mapping. Tests assert real postconditions, not only HTTP status or schema shape: persisted state, version increments, audit rows/transaction ids, relationship results, subscription payloads, idempotent replay behavior, and GraphQL error extensions. At least one full UI canary workflow creates an entity, queries and filters it, updates with OCC, verifies audit/version, creates a relationship, observes subscription output, deletes it, and verifies final state. At least one bulk E2E commits mixed create/update/link operations across collections, verifies all persisted state, replays with the same idempotency key, and verifies cache/replay semantics. Empty-database root queries return valid empty responses. description:
Add a reusable axum middleware layer that extracts (tenant, database) from the URL path /tenants/{tenant}/databases/{database}/..., validates the identifiers, and installs an extension the downstream handlers and the JWT pipeline (B1) can read. This bead does NOT wire the middleware into the main router — that is D3's job. This bead builds the primitive + its tests. The primitive is a pure function + a thin axum layer wrapper.

## Scope — exactly what to add

### 1. New module: crates/axon-server/src/path_router.rs

Three pieces:

(a) A pure extractor function:

    pub fn extract_tenant_database(path: &str) -> Option<(String, String)> {
        // Match /tenants/{tenant}/databases/{database}(/...)?
        // Return (tenant, database) for data-plane paths
        // Return None for unprefixed paths, /health, /ui/*, /control/*
    }

This MUST be pure (takes &str, returns Option<(String, String)>), MUST validate both segments against the identifier rule:
- 1-63 characters
- ASCII only: [a-zA-Z0-9_-]
- Cannot start with a digit
- Cannot be empty

Reserved prefixes that bypass extraction (return None cleanly, no error):
- /health
- /metrics (for future Prometheus scrape)
- /ui (and /ui/*)
- /control (and /control/*)
- /favicon.ico
- /robots.txt

Any other path that does not start with /tenants/ returns None. Paths that start with /tenants/ but fail the identifier rule ALSO return None — invalid identifiers are treated as 'not a data-plane route' and fall through, so the caller can emit a 404. (Do not emit 400 here; the middleware layer decides whether to 404, and 400 on bad identifiers is too chatty for the common 'wrong URL' case.)

(b) A ResolvedPath struct that goes into the request extension:

    #[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq)]
    pub struct ResolvedPath {
        pub tenant: String,
        pub database: String,
    }

(c) The axum middleware layer as an async function:

    pub async fn path_router_layer(
        mut req: axum::http::Request<axum::body::Body>,
        next: axum::middleware::Next,
    ) -> axum::response::Response {
        let path = req.uri().path().to_string();
        if let Some((tenant, database)) = extract_tenant_database(&path) {
            req.extensions_mut().insert(ResolvedPath { tenant, database });
        }
        next.run(req).await
    }

The layer ALWAYS calls next — it does NOT decide 404s. Routing decisions (e.g., 'unrecognized /tenants/* path') are the router's job at the route table level. This is deliberate: the middleware's only job is to populate the extension when it can, and to leave it unpopulated when it cannot.

### 2. Re-export from crates/axon-server/src/lib.rs

pub mod path_router; and re-export path_router::{extract_tenant_database, path_router_layer, ResolvedPath}.

### 3. Tests: crates/axon-server/tests/path_router_test.rs

Unit tests on the pure extractor (NOT the middleware yet — that's easier to unit-test as a pure function):

#### Happy path
- extract_tenant_database("/tenants/acme/databases/orders") -> Some(("acme", "orders"))
- extract_tenant_database("/tenants/acme/databases/orders/collections/c/entities/e") -> Some(("acme", "orders"))
- extract_tenant_database("/tenants/acme/databases/orders/graphql") -> Some(("acme", "orders"))

#### Reserved prefixes
- extract_tenant_database("/health") -> None
- extract_tenant_database("/metrics") -> None
- extract_tenant_database("/ui/collections") -> None
- extract_tenant_database("/control/tenants") -> None
- extract_tenant_database("/favicon.ico") -> None

#### Malformed
- extract_tenant_database("/") -> None
- extract_tenant_database("/tenants") -> None (no tenant segment)
- extract_tenant_database("/tenants/") -> None
- extract_tenant_database("/tenants/acme") -> None (no /databases/ segment)
- extract_tenant_database("/tenants/acme/databases") -> None
- extract_tenant_database("/tenants/acme/databases/") -> None
- extract_tenant_database("/tenants/acme/orders") -> None (missing 'databases' literal)

#### Invalid identifiers
- extract_tenant_database("/tenants/3bad/databases/orders") -> None (leading digit)
- extract_tenant_database("/tenants/acme/databases/or ders") -> None (space)
- extract_tenant_database("/tenants/acme/databases/or..ders") -> None (dot)
- extract_tenant_database("/tenants//databases/orders") -> None (empty tenant)
- extract_tenant_database("/tenants/a-very-long-tenant-name-that-exceeds-sixty-three-characters-total/databases/orders") -> None (>63 chars)

#### Middleware integration
One integration test that builds a minimal axum router with the layer installed, hits /tenants/acme/databases/orders/ping, and asserts the handler sees ResolvedPath in extensions.

### 4. PROP-010 path routing determinism property test

In the same test file, add a proptest that generates random URL segments and asserts:
- If segment validates as an identifier by the rule above, extract_tenant_database("/tenants/{t}/databases/{d}/x") returns Some((t, d)).
- If segment does NOT validate, extract_tenant_database returns None.
- The function is referentially transparent: same input -> same output (automatic from being a pure function, but the test should explicitly assert it to make PROP-010 visible in the suite).

Use proptest strategies: prop_oneof![valid_identifier(), invalid_identifier()] where valid_identifier uses any::<String>().prop_filter(...) or a regex-based generator. 1000 iterations minimum.

### 5. INV-017 deferred (out of scope for this bead)

INV-017 is a cross-cutting axon-sim workload that requires multiple tenants with materialized storage, middleware wired into a real router, and entities written to different (tenant, database) tuples. It cannot be implemented until D3 (remove-legacy-routes) and D2 (database-router) land and a full request pipeline exists. File a follow-up bead after D3.

## Context you must know

### Already landed
- A1 schema: tenants, databases-per-tenant tables exist but this bead does NOT touch them. It just parses the URL.
- A2 types: no types needed here; ResolvedPath is local to path_router.rs.
- B1 middleware: the JWT verify pipeline reads a request extension to know which tenant/database the request targets. Currently it does its own path parsing inside verify_request. After D1 lands, the JWT pipeline can read ResolvedPath from the extension instead. B1's current inline parsing stays — refactoring B1 to consume ResolvedPath is D3's job, not D1's.

### Why no router wiring
D1 is a primitive. D2 (database-router) uses (tenant, database) to pick a storage adapter. D3 (remove-legacy-routes) wires everything into the real router and removes the old paths. Keeping D1 pure + testable in isolation is deliberate — the axum router surgery belongs to D3 where all the moving parts land together.

## Files to touch

- crates/axon-server/src/path_router.rs — NEW
- crates/axon-server/src/lib.rs — add 'pub mod path_router;'
- crates/axon-server/tests/path_router_test.rs — NEW
- Possibly crates/axon-server/Cargo.toml [dev-dependencies] — add proptest if not already present (it likely is — axon-core already uses it via B4)

## Files NOT to touch

- crates/axon-server/src/auth_pipeline.rs — B1 keeps its inline path parsing until D3
- crates/axon-server/src/gateway.rs — that's the main router, D3's territory
- crates/axon-server/src/auth.rs — legacy Tailscale, unrelated
- Anything in axon-storage or axon-core

## Verification

    cargo test -p axon-server --test path_router_test  # must pass ~20 unit tests + 1 integration + PROP-010
    cargo test -p axon-server  # all prior test files must still pass
    cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings clean

acceptance: docs/helix/02-design/api-contracts.md and ADR-018 pin the browser/API policy before implementation: (1) GraphQL is the primary documented interface for end-user and developer workflows, including single-entity CRUD, schema/collection admin, transaction commits, rollback where appropriate, and tenant/user/credential/database control-plane routes; (2) REST exceptions are limited to health/metrics/static assets, stream/file-oriented transports, compatibility endpoints, and break-glass operations where GraphQL is demonstrably not right; (3) CORS allowed-origin model, credentials mode, preflight TTL, exposed headers, and Tailscale browser behavior are documented; (4) idempotency-key placement is delegated to axon-b8bdd7a3 but this policy references the final body/input decision; (5) Axon UI is declared the first/canary GraphQL consumer across control-plane and data-plane workflows; (6) any REST-only behavior has docs and tests proving GraphQL either avoids promising it or returns a stable unsupported error with extensions.code.
description:
<context>
Follow-up on axon-d42292e5 (HTTP interface gaps for nexiq FEAT-010), which closed with a good api-contracts.md shipping /auth/me, /schema handshake with x-axon-schema-hash, GraphQL filter/sort/pagination, POST /traverse, POST /transactions with idempotency-key header, and a TS SDK. The closing review itself flagged CORS as an explicit outstanding gap; two additional questions surfaced from downstream (nexiq) review.
</context>

<gap-1-cors>
api-contracts.md has zero mention of CORS or Origin policy. A static browser bundle making direct cross-origin calls to Axon needs:
- Allowed-origins model: global static allowlist, per-tenant configurable, or dev-only wildcard
- Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true/false (affects whether cookie/Authorization can be sent on cross-origin)
- Preflight cache TTL (Access-Control-Max-Age) — matters on chatty GraphQL surfaces
- Access-Control-Expose-Headers: at minimum x-idempotent-cache, x-axon-schema-hash so browser code can read them
- Interaction with Tailscale: Tailscale-resolved requests may bypass CORS concerns within the tailnet, but the browser itself still enforces Same-Origin — a Tailscale-hosted SPA talking to a differently-named Axon host still preflights

Document the policy; show the concrete response headers Axon sends on preflight and on actual requests.
</gap-1-cors>

<gap-2-graphql-scope>
Current contract splits: GraphQL for lists/queries/traversals, REST (POST /collections/{name}/query, /entities/{id}, /transactions) for single-entity CRUD. Downstream (nexiq FEAT-010) argues GraphQL can cover single-entity CRUD too (get/create/update/delete by ID), leaving REST only for potential local-first batch-edit streams where JSON-line stream semantics are a better fit than GraphQL.

Decide and pin:
  A) GraphQL is canonical for everything-except-streams. REST entity CRUD routes are marked Legacy with a removal timeline. /transactions stays only if GraphQL mutations cannot do atomic multi-entity writes (and if that is the case, document why).
  B) REST entity CRUD is canonical for simple get/update-one paths; GraphQL is for lists and complex reads. Document the decision criterion for 'when to use which' so SDK users are not guessing.
  C) Both supported indefinitely; pick one as canonical in documentation and examples so users have a paved path.

Downstream preference: A — simpler client, one set of error/OCC/auth conventions, fewer transport-layer bugs.
</gap-2-graphql-scope>

<gap-3-idempotency-canonical>
Current: POST /transactions accepts 'idempotency-key' as an HTTP header. Open questions:
  - Is the header canonical, or will a body field be accepted and preferred?
  - Does GraphQL mutation equivalent (if one exists) accept idempotency via an argument on the root Mutation, via a directive, or via the transport header?
  - Is case-sensitivity of the header name normalized (Idempotency-Key vs idempotency-key)?
  - What is the key's max length, character set, and collision semantics across databases with the same name on different tenants?

Downstream argument for a body field instead of/in addition to the header:
  - Idempotency applies only to /transactions, not to arbitrary endpoints — no cross-endpoint uniformity win from a header
  - Body fields show up in schema/protos and logs; header is a hidden contract
  - A header can be stripped by middleware; body survives
  - Matches how /transactions already carries structured operations; one more structured field is trivial

Downstream preference: idempotency_key as a body field on the transaction root, canonical; accept the header for Stripe-familiar callers but deprecate later. SDK uses body field.
</gap-3-idempotency-canonical>
notes: Implemented policy decision docs. Evidence: docs/helix/02-design/api-contracts.md now states GraphQL is primary for end-user/developer workflows including CRUD, schema/collection admin, control plane, transactions, and UI canary; REST exceptions limited to health/metrics/static assets, stream/file transports, compatibility endpoints, and break-glass recovery. ADR-018 section 5 updated from REST/GraphQL division to GraphQL-first interface policy. CORS policy documented with origin model, credentials mode, preflight headers/TTL, exposed headers, and Tailscale browser behavior. rg check confirms stale REST-only language is gone except health/metrics.

description:
REVIEW-DRIVEN ITERATION 3 (reviewer: opus; iter 1/2 discovered blocking issues)

The bead's original types are IN PLACE at crates/axon-core/src/auth.rs from commit 91d4bdb. This iteration is a targeted fix-up. DO NOT rewrite from scratch; edit the existing file in place.

## BLOCKING gaps to resolve

1. CRITICAL NAMING: UserIdentity currently has field 'subject' (auth.rs:794) but ADR-018 §3 normatively requires 'external_id'. A1 SQL schema (crates/axon-storage/src/auth_schema.rs) already uses 'external_id' — these MUST match. Rename the field everywhere:
   - crates/axon-core/src/auth.rs:794 — the UserIdentity struct field
   - crates/axon-core/src/auth.rs:1065 — the serde round-trip test fixture
   - any other references (grep -n 'subject' crates/axon-core/src/auth.rs)

2. TEST CORRECTNESS: crates/axon-core/src/auth.rs:1213 (jwt_claims_aud_array_returns_credential_malformed) asserts the deserialization returns any serde_json::Error. AC requires it to produce AuthError::CredentialMalformed specifically. Wire this one of two ways:
   (a) Add 'impl From<serde_json::Error> for AuthError' that maps to CredentialMalformed, then change the test to assert the AuthError variant
   (b) Add a fn parse_claims(&str) -> Result<JwtClaims, AuthError> that wraps serde_json::from_str and maps errors, then test that entry point

3. VARIANT COUNT HARDENING: AUTH_ERROR_VARIANT_COUNT is currently a const usize = 14 at auth.rs:995, verified at runtime (auth.rs:1264). Replace with an exhaustive match that forces a compile error if variants are added/removed:

   const _: () = {
       const fn _exhaustive_check(e: &AuthError) -> usize {
           match e {
               AuthError::Unauthenticated => 0,
               AuthError::CredentialMalformed => 1,
               // ... all 14 variants named explicitly ...
           }
       }
   };

   The compiler will fail if a new variant is added without updating this match.

## Do NOT touch

- The type shapes (Tenant, User, TenantRole, Op, etc.) — they are correct per ADR-018
- The serde round-trip tests for types other than UserIdentity
- Any other file outside crates/axon-core/src/auth.rs (except cargo test + clippy run)

## Verification before commit

- cargo test -p axon-core  ← must be green
- cargo clippy -p axon-core -- -D warnings  ← must be clean
- grep -n 'subject' crates/axon-core/src/auth.rs  ← must return zero hits

## Base context

- Governing doc: docs/helix/02-design/adr/ADR-018-tenant-user-credential-model.md §3 (Users) and §4 (Credentials)
- A1 schema uses external_id in the 'user_identities' table
- Original bead was 'auth-core-types: Tenant/User/Credential/Grants/JwtClaims/AuthError' with spec laid out in ADR-018 §§2-4

notes:
<review iteration=1 reviewer=claude-opus-4-6>
VERDICT: REQUEST_CHANGES

Current commit: 91d4bdb (already merged to master)

GAPS:
1. BLOCKER: UserIdentity field is named 'subject' (auth.rs:794, :1065) but ADR-018 §3 normatively requires 'external_id'. A1 schema already uses 'external_id' — they must match or nothing will interoperate. Rename the field and update the serde test fixture on line 1065.

2. PARTIAL: aud-array rejection test (auth.rs:1213-1231) asserts serde_json::Error but AC requires AuthError::CredentialMalformed. Add a From<serde_json::Error> for AuthError impl (or a wrapper deserializer) that maps this case specifically.

3. MINOR: AUTH_ERROR_VARIANT_COUNT == 14 is runtime-only at auth.rs:1265. Prefer either:
   (a) an exhaustive match-all arm that forces a compile error on missing variants, OR
   (b) std::mem::variant_count if nightly is acceptable, OR
   (c) a proc-macro or build.rs check.
   (a) is the simplest — write a match { AuthError::Unauthenticated => 0, ..., AuthError::NotATenantMember => 13 } and let the compiler force all variants to be named.

4. NIT: TenantRole delegation proptests use fixed strategies (\"A\", \"W\", \"R\") which makes them behaviorally identical to unit tests. Use `prop_oneof![Just(TenantRole::Admin), Just(TenantRole::Write), Just(TenantRole::Read)]` so the proptest framework actually exercises random combinations.

INSTRUCTIONS FOR ITERATION 2:
- Keep commit 91d4bdb as base; ADD a fix-up commit
- Apply fixes 1, 2, 3 — fix 4 (proptest strategies) is optional but encouraged
- Run cargo test -p axon-core + cargo clippy -p axon-core -- -D warnings and ensure green
- The subject→external_id rename is the blocking issue; the other three are quality concerns

ACCEPTANCE remains as stated in the bead.
</review>

acceptance:
AC1. ui/src/lib/policy-impact-delta.ts exports computeCellDelta and CellDelta type with the shape documented above.

AC2. ui/src/lib/policy-impact-delta.test.ts has at least 14 tests covering the cases enumerated in the description; bun test src/lib/policy-impact-delta.test.ts is green.

AC3. computeCellDelta does not mutate its inputs (verify with frozen-input test).

AC4. Set-equality for redactedFields/deniedFields treats element-order as insignificant (test 7 above proves this).

AC5. bun run typecheck and bun run lint pass.
description:
Subdivision 3 of 4 of axon-ff92fed7. Depends on axon-80979cb8 (A1) only — independent of A2 because this is a pure module + tests, no UI changes.

NEW FILE: ui/src/lib/policy-impact-delta.ts
NEW TEST: ui/src/lib/policy-impact-delta.test.ts (bun test)

Implement and export:

  import type { ImpactCell } from './policy-evaluator';

  export type CellDelta = {
    decisionChanged: boolean;       // active.decision !== proposed.decision
    redactedFieldsChanged: boolean; // sets of strings differ
    deniedFieldsChanged: boolean;   // sets of strings differ
    approvalRoleChanged: boolean;   // active.approvalRole !== proposed.approvalRole
    diagnosticCodeChanged: boolean; // active.diagnostic?.code vs proposed.diagnostic?.code (specifically tracks policy_filter_unindexed remediation)
    isUnchanged: boolean;           // true iff none of the above changed AND both cells exist
    onlyActive: boolean;            // proposed cell missing → schema has no draft, or proposed fetch errored
    onlyProposed: boolean;          // active cell missing (rare, but possible if active fetch errored)
  };

  export function computeCellDelta(
    active: ImpactCell | null,
    proposed: ImpactCell | null,
  ): CellDelta;

Helper: a stable set-equality predicate for redactedFields/deniedFields (treat order as insignificant, deduplicate, case-sensitive). Do NOT mutate inputs.

Unit tests in policy-impact-delta.test.ts must cover:

  1. unchanged: identical cells → isUnchanged=true, all *Changed=false.
  2. decision allow→deny: decisionChanged=true.
  3. decision deny→allow: decisionChanged=true.
  4. decision deny→needs_approval: decisionChanged=true.
  5. redactedFields added: ['amount_cents'] → ['amount_cents', 'commercial_terms'] → redactedFieldsChanged=true.
  6. redactedFields removed: ['amount_cents'] → [] → redactedFieldsChanged=true.
  7. redactedFields reordered (no semantic change): ['a','b'] → ['b','a'] → redactedFieldsChanged=false.
  8. deniedFields added/removed: same pattern as redactedFields.
  9. approvalRole null→'reviewer': approvalRoleChanged=true.
  10. diagnostic code policy_filter_unindexed→null: diagnosticCodeChanged=true (remediation accepted).
  11. diagnostic code null→policy_filter_unindexed: diagnosticCodeChanged=true (remediation regression).
  12. proposed=null, active=non-null: onlyActive=true, isUnchanged=false.
  13. active=null, proposed=non-null: onlyProposed=true.
  14. both null: isUnchanged=false, onlyActive=false, onlyProposed=false (degenerate state).

Run: bun test src/lib/policy-impact-delta.test.ts must be green. The function will be wired into the render block in A4.

description:
Wave 2 bead. Final recovery-tool bead for wave 2. Independent of the other wave-2 beads; touches ui/src/routes/tenants/[tenant]/databases/[database]/audit/+page.svelte.

## Problem

The audit log shows entries that have a \`transaction_id\` when they were part of a multi-entity commit. The backend exposes \`POST /transactions/{transaction_id}/rollback\` that undoes every mutation in that transaction atomically. The UI has no way to invoke it, and no way to see which audit entries belong together under a single transaction.

## Backend (already in place — no changes)

- \`POST /tenants/{t}/databases/{d}/transactions/{transaction_id}/rollback\`
- Handler: crates/axon-server/src/gateway.rs (search for \`transactions\` routes)
- Request body shape: **read the handler to confirm** — probably \`{ \"actor\": \"ui\", \"dry_run\": bool }\` or similar
- Response: **read the handler** — probably lists affected entities and whether it was applied or dry_run

Also check: is there an endpoint that lists all audit entries for a transaction id, or do we filter client-side? The audit page already supports filtering; check if it accepts a \`transaction_id\` filter in \`GET /audit/query\`. If not, add one (small backend change) OR filter client-side.

## UI changes

### 1. api.ts

Add:
\`\`\`ts
export async function previewTransactionRollback(
  transactionId: number,
  scope: Scope,
): Promise<{ affected_entities: EntityRecord[]; audit_entries: AuditEntry[] }> { ... }

export async function applyTransactionRollback(
  transactionId: number,
  scope: Scope,
): Promise<{ reverted_count: number; audit_entries: AuditEntry[] }> { ... }
\`\`\`

Shapes depend on the actual handler — verify first, adjust accordingly.

### 2. Audit page — Transaction grouping + rollback

File: ui/src/routes/tenants/[tenant]/databases/[database]/audit/+page.svelte

Changes:
1. **Group indicator**: in the audit table, visually mark entries that share a transaction_id (e.g., a pill showing \`tx: #42\` next to the mutation type, clickable to filter to that transaction).
2. **Transaction panel**: when the selected entry has a transaction_id, show a section in the detail panel titled \"Transaction #42\" that lists all entries sharing that transaction_id (load them via filtered audit query or client-side filter of the already-loaded entries).
3. **\"Rollback transaction\" button** in that panel. Clicking opens a confirmation modal:
   - List of affected entity IDs and the operations that will be undone
   - \"Cancel\" + \"Confirm rollback\" buttons
4. **Apply**: call \`applyTransactionRollback\`. On success, show a toast \"Reverted N entities\", reload the audit list, clear the transaction selection. On error, show the server error.

### 3. Add \"Transaction ID\" filter

Add an input next to the existing collection/actor/date filters: \"Transaction ID\" (number). When set, the \`fetchAudit\` call adds \`transaction_id=N\` to the query string. If the backend doesn't support that filter, filter client-side.

## Tests

New specs in ui/tests/e2e/wave2-rollback.spec.ts:

- **Transaction rollback preview** — use the transactions endpoint (POST /transactions) to commit a multi-entity transaction in test setup (helper needed). Navigate to audit page, find an entry with transaction_id, click it, assert the Transaction section lists all entries in that transaction.
- **Transaction rollback apply** — continue from above. Click \"Rollback transaction\", confirm. Assert every affected entity is reverted (fetch each via API and compare). Assert new audit entries appear for the reverts.
- **Filter by transaction_id** — set the filter input, assert only matching entries render.

Helper in tests/e2e/helpers.ts: \`commitTestTransaction(request, db, operations)\` that calls POST /transactions.

## Files to touch

- ui/src/lib/api.ts — previewTransactionRollback, applyTransactionRollback, fetchAudit with transaction_id filter
- ui/src/routes/tenants/[tenant]/databases/[database]/audit/+page.svelte — grouping, transaction panel, rollback flow, filter
- ui/tests/e2e/helpers.ts — commitTestTransaction
- ui/tests/e2e/wave2-rollback.spec.ts — new describe OR new file

## Verification

- bun run typecheck / build / cargo rebuild / restart / playwright green

description:
Migrate SqliteStorageAdapter (rusqlite, sync) and PostgresStorageAdapter (tokio-postgres + block_in_place bridge) to sqlx with async Pool<Postgres> / Pool<Sqlite>.

## Motivation
- Eliminate the block_in_place hack in PostgresStorageAdapter::block()
- Eliminate the dedicated per-adapter Tokio runtime (_rt: Runtime)
- Unified async pooling via sqlx::Pool<DB>
- Optionally: compile-time verified SQL via sqlx::query! macros

## Migration Plan

Step 1 — Make StorageAdapter async
  Use async fn in trait (stable in Rust 1.75, workspace minimum).
  Async trait methods propagate through AxonHandler.

Step 2 — Postgres adapter
  Replace { client, handle, _rt } with { pool: Pool<Postgres> }.
  All ~40 SQL methods become async; remove block_in_place entirely.
  Keep provision/deprovision helpers with short-lived pools.

Step 3 — SQLite adapter
  Replace { conn: Connection } with { pool: Pool<Sqlite> }.

Step 4 — Gateway integration
  TenantHandler pattern can simplify once blocking is no longer needed.
  handler.lock().await.method().await replaces the current sync calls.

Step 5 — Test migration
  Update all tests that call SqliteStorageAdapter::open_in_memory() to async.

## Key Files
- crates/axon-storage/src/adapter.rs (trait)
- crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs (rewrite)
- crates/axon-storage/src/sqlite.rs (rewrite)
- crates/axon-storage/Cargo.toml (add sqlx, remove rusqlite + tokio-postgres)
- crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs (async handler methods)
- Cargo.toml (workspace dep: sqlx 0.8 with runtime-tokio-rustls, postgres, sqlite, json)

## Acceptance Criteria
- All axon-storage and axon-server tests pass
- No block_in_place or dedicated runtime in any adapter
- Clippy clean

acceptance: (a) axon-c5cc071a has been reopened with a note explaining the false closure (verified already); (b) a 48-hour audit of axon bead closures has been run against the pattern (status=closed AND updated_at - claimed_at < 10 minutes AND zero events AND no execution bundle on disk AND no commit referencing the bead id) — any matches have been reopened with notes; (c) ddx review-malfunction issue is externally tracked or a workaround is in place so it does not recur on the axon execute-loop; (d) closure rule in the worker requires both a valid primary result and a valid review result before setting status=closed, OR the project has explicitly opted into no-review closures for specific bead kinds.
description:
<context>
Filed as a tracking follow-up on the reopening of axon-c5cc071a (field/row/entity RBAC — the single biggest nexiq consumer need). That bead was claimed by the axon execute-loop at 2026-04-19T23:20:45Z and marked "closed" 4 minutes 33 seconds later at 23:25:18Z, with no execution bundle, no commit referencing the bead id, no branch implementing any RBAC work, and no notes on the bead. The bead's scope (three-level policy enforcement, schema-annotated declarative expression language, full downstream role matrix, denial contract, performance/introspection affordances) cannot be completed in 4.5 minutes.

Pattern recognition: this is the same class of failure nexiq observed extensively yesterday with the ddx execute-loop's post-merge reviewer. Known symptoms on nexiq:
- Reviewer returns malformed `{"verdict":"BLOCK"}` with no rationale even when the actual model output was APPROVE with detailed AC grades.
- Oversized reviewer stream (1.27MB in one case) captured as a bead event body, blowing bufio.Scanner 64KB default buffer on subsequent bead reads and crashing the loop outright.
- Reviewer call overflowing the model's context window (observed: 1.87M-token prompt against a 196K-token-limit backend) and the failure-fallback marking the bead closed.

On the axon side, axon-c5cc071a's 4-minute closure without evidence is consistent with the reviewer force-closing the bead on a malformed or erroring review step, regardless of primary-task outcome.

Nexiq has drafted a comprehensive bug report of the review-malfunction pattern and plans to forward it to the ddx maintainers for resolution.
</context>

<this-bead-scope>
This bead is a meta-issue tracking the axon-side exposure to the review-malfunction pattern. It does NOT re-implement RBAC (that's axon-c5cc071a). It asks the axon owner to:

1. Treat axon-c5cc071a as the canonical owner of the RBAC implementation. It is reopened with a note explaining the false closure. Next claim should verify evidence (execution bundle, commits, or explicit deferred decision) before closing.

2. Audit recent axon bead closures (last ~48 hours, especially those claimed and closed within a few minutes of each other) for the false-closure pattern. Same SQL-shaped query: beads where status=closed, updated_at - claimed_at < 10 minutes, zero events, no execution bundle on disk, no commit references. Reopen any that turn up.

3. Track the ddx review-malfunction issue externally. Nexiq's forwarded report will name the specific failure modes; axon should apply any ddx fixes or workarounds on its own worker infrastructure.

4. Consider a defensive closure rule: an execute-loop worker should NEVER close a bead with status=closed unless BOTH the primary task status and the review status are valid-and-non-malformed. A malformed review should mark the bead as in_progress or retry_after, not closed.
</this-bead-scope>

<why-p1>
High impact because a silently false-closed bead disappears from the ready queue and disappears from any reporting that filters by status=open. Nexiq's plan references axon-c5cc071a as a Phase 2 prerequisite; had this false closure not been caught by a downstream audit, the plan would have proceeded on the assumption RBAC was shipped. The blast radius is "every bead that flows through the reviewer," not just this one.
</why-p1>
notes:
<measure-results>
  <timestamp>2026-04-20T14:22:00Z</timestamp>
  <status>PASS</status>
  <acceptance>
    <criterion name='axon-c5cc071a false closure note verified' status='pass' evidence='ddx bead show axon-c5cc071a shows the prior false closure note; current closure has commit 1fa04de5bb1a29fa0935c2dffaf5f3437fe66e65 documenting FEAT-029 spec/defer scope.'/>
    <criterion name='48-hour audit run' status='pass' evidence='Audit window 2026-04-18T14:18:20Z to 2026-04-20T14:18:20Z found eight fast closures; only axon-6c33fab3 matched zero events, no bundle, and no commit reference.'/>
    <criterion name='matches reopened' status='pass' evidence='Reopened axon-6c33fab3 with audit note and reopen event.'/>
    <criterion name='workaround in place' status='pass' evidence='AGENTS.md now requires durable closure evidence and forbids closing on malformed/empty/errored review results; audit report recorded at docs/helix/06-iterate/review-malfunction-audit-2026-04-20.md.'/>
  </acceptance>
  <gates>

  </gates>
</measure-results>

description:
Three files in axon-server use rusqlite::Connection directly (NOT through StorageAdapter). Rewrite all 3 to use sqlx::SqlitePool.

## Files to rewrite
- crates/axon-server/src/control_plane.rs (324 lines) — control plane SQLite DB for tenant/node management
- crates/axon-server/src/cors_config.rs (233 lines) — CORS configuration SQLite DB
- crates/axon-server/src/user_roles.rs (326 lines) — user role assignment SQLite DB

## Pattern
Each file currently does:
- `rusqlite::Connection::open(path)` or `::open_in_memory()`
- `conn.execute(sql, params![...])`
- `conn.prepare(sql).query_map(params, |row| ...)`

Replace with:
- `sqlx::SqlitePool::connect(url)` (via a small Runtime block_on wrapper or using an existing Runtime handle)
- `sqlx::query(sql).bind(...).execute(&pool).await` (wrapped in block_on)
- `sqlx::query(sql).bind(...).fetch_all(&pool).await` (wrapped in block_on)

## Cargo.toml changes
- crates/axon-server/Cargo.toml: remove `rusqlite.workspace = true`, add `sqlx.workspace = true`

## Do NOT change
- Any files in axon-storage
- The StorageAdapter trait
- Any other axon-server files

## Acceptance Criteria
- cargo check --workspace passes
- cargo test -p axon-server passes
- `use rusqlite` no longer appears in any axon-server .rs file
- rusqlite is not in crates/axon-server/Cargo.toml
title: sqlx-step-4: migrate axon-server direct rusqlite usage to sqlx

REST methods are marked compatibility/lower-level where retained, with explicit exceptions only for health/metrics/static assets, stream/file transports, and break-glass recovery operations. acceptance: OPTIONS requests bypass auth and return correct CORS headers; actual requests from allowed origins include Access-Control-Allow-Origin; --cors-allow-origins and --cors-allow-all flags work; no CORS headers when flag absent; browser integration test passes with real fetch from allowed origin
description:
Nexiq is migrating to a static browser bundle that talks directly to Axon over the Tailscale network, bypassing the Next.js server layer entirely. Browsers enforce the Same-Origin Policy, so all cross-origin requests from the nexiq bundle to Axon will be blocked unless Axon returns the correct CORS response headers.

## Context

The nexiq bundle will be served from a static origin (e.g. https://sindri:5173 or a dedicated static-file port). Axon runs on the same tailnet node or a nearby one (e.g. http://100.x.x.x:4170 or https://axon.ts.net:4170). Because these origins differ, every browser request — including reads — triggers CORS. Tailscale is the security boundary; Axon does not need to restrict which tailnet origins may call it, but it must emit the headers that tell browsers the request is permitted.

## Required CORS behaviour

### Preflight (OPTIONS)

For any cross-origin request with a non-simple method (POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE) or a non-simple header (Content-Type: application/json), the browser sends an OPTIONS preflight before the real request. Axon must respond with HTTP 200 (or 204) and the following headers:

  Access-Control-Allow-Origin: <request Origin header value>  -- or * if no-credentials mode
  Access-Control-Allow-Methods: GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE, OPTIONS
  Access-Control-Allow-Headers: Content-Type, Authorization
  Access-Control-Max-Age: 86400   -- cache preflight for 24h to reduce OPTIONS chatter

Preflight responses must NOT require ts-net auth (OPTIONS requests carry no body and are not mutating). The CORS middleware should short-circuit before auth for OPTIONS.

### Actual requests

All non-OPTIONS responses from Axon must include:

  Access-Control-Allow-Origin: <request Origin header value>  -- or *

Because Axon's ts-net auth is peer-IP-based (not cookie/credential-based), credentials mode is not required. Access-Control-Allow-Credentials is not needed.

### Configuration

Add a CLI flag --cors-allow-origins <origins> accepting a comma-separated list of allowed origins, e.g.:

  axon-server --cors-allow-origins 'https://sindri:5173,https://nexiq.ts.net'

When the incoming Origin header matches an entry in the list, echo that origin back in Access-Control-Allow-Origin. When the flag is set to '*', use the wildcard (appropriate for tailnet-only deployments where network ACLs are the security boundary). When the flag is absent, CORS headers are not emitted (current behaviour, no regression).

A separate --cors-allow-all flag (equivalent to --cors-allow-origins '*') may be added for ergonomics.

## What nexiq sends

The nexiq browser client will make requests of the form:

  GET  /entities/{collection}           -- list all
  GET  /entities/{collection}/{id}      -- get one
  POST /entities/{collection}           -- create (body: {data: {...}})
  PUT  /entities/{collection}/{id}      -- full replace (body: {data: {...}})
  POST /entities/{collection}/{id}      -- partial update / upsert
  DELETE /entities/{collection}/{id}    -- delete
  GET  /links/traverse?...              -- graph traversal

All request bodies are JSON. The Content-Type header is always application/json. No cookies are sent (credentials: 'omit').

## Testing

### Unit tests (Rust, no network)

1. OPTIONS preflight for POST /entities/tasks → 200, correct CORS headers present, no auth check triggered
2. OPTIONS preflight with disallowed origin → 200 (OPTIONS always permitted), but no ACAO header (or ACAO: null)
3. GET /entities/accounts with allowed origin → response includes Access-Control-Allow-Origin
4. GET /entities/accounts with no Origin header → no CORS headers added (non-browser client, no regression)
5. --cors-allow-origins flag not set → no CORS headers on any response
6. --cors-allow-all flag → ACAO: * on all responses

### Integration / browser test

Start axon-server with --no-auth --cors-allow-origins 'http://localhost:8080'. Serve a minimal HTML page from localhost:8080 containing:

  fetch('http://localhost:4170/entities/accounts')
    .then(r => r.json())
    .then(data => document.body.innerText = JSON.stringify(data))
    .catch(e => document.body.innerText = 'CORS ERROR: ' + e)

Open in a real browser (or Playwright). Verify the fetch resolves (not blocked). Verify the response body is JSON, not a CORS error. Then restart axon-server without --cors-allow-origins and verify the fetch is blocked.

### Preflight caching test

With Access-Control-Max-Age: 86400, a browser should not send a second OPTIONS for the same endpoint within 24h. Verify via browser devtools network tab that repeated POSTs to the same endpoint do not generate repeated OPTIONS requests after the first.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] OPTIONS requests return 200/204 with correct CORS headers and bypass ts-net auth
- [ ] Actual requests from an allowed origin include Access-Control-Allow-Origin
- [ ] --cors-allow-origins CLI flag controls which origins are echoed
- [ ] --cors-allow-all flag sets wildcard ACAO
- [ ] No CORS headers emitted when flag is absent (no regression for non-browser clients)
- [ ] All unit tests pass (bun test / cargo test)
- [ ] Browser integration test passes (fetch from allowed origin succeeds; fetch from disallowed origin blocked)
title: feat: CORS support for browser-based clients
