description:
Umbrella bead consolidating all eitri-apr13 work that was dropped during the 2026-04-14 integration session.

The execute-loop on eitri ran from a stale base for a week (April 6 → April 13) producing 134 commits that never reached origin. On 2026-04-14 a manual integration session salvaged what could be cleanly cherry-picked and dropped the rest. This bead lists the dropped work so it can be re-implemented against current origin/master.

Each dropped item has its eitri-apr13-snapshot commit SHA referenced for context. The eitri-apr13-snapshot branch (on the eitri local clone, tip 088d09b) preserves the original commits indefinitely. Use those as design reference, NOT as cherry-pick sources — re-implement against current origin.

## Successfully integrated (for context)
1. ADR-016 agent guardrails docs (9300a16) — bead axon-6f69ef62
2. PRD isolation: snapshot vs serializable (04d9f53) — bead axon-f63ac16f
3. LifecycleDef in CollectionSchema (e3dfd83) — bead axon-eec93920
4. transition_lifecycle method on AxonHandler (ea0c43e) — bead axon-0f9c54cf
5. ADR-015 rollback/recovery docs (4507ca0 + 0e7fd31) — beads axon-1c343de8 + axon-f1c3183c
6. PRD isolation complete fix (f334ff1) — bead axon-4c08a548
7. Clock trait + SystemClock + FakeClock (e0a7e7b) — bead axon-a0081a82
8. ChangeEvent.audit_id field (1a2cd12 + 8d5ca1c) — beads axon-7adc58e9 + axon-7836eb3e
9. AuditLog audit_id ordering invariant + known_collections impl (f6f597b) — bead axon-77b53b36
10. Agent guardrails layer (5f12e8c) — original orphan 035e5f6e, FEAT-022
11. axon-control-plane crate (5460720 + cf1e4f2) — original orphans a99978ae + 8c050811, FEAT-025
12. Kafka CDC sink (b38732c + bceb06c + 16f1347) — original orphans, FEAT-021

## DROPPED — needs reimplementation

### FEAT-019: Gate results refactor
Eitri commit f0baab59 + 2ff352d2 + 2d4fa346 + da7bc346 + 74c287ae. Original beads: axon-88b4b5f1, axon-ba577e48.
- Move RuleViolation + GateResult from axon-schema to axon-core (with PartialEq)
- Add gate_results: HashMap<String, GateResult> field to Entity (with serde default)
- Remove gate side-table from StorageAdapter trait + all impls
- Add gate-based query filter using persisted gate_results
- Lifecycle 'save' gate enforcement
- Reason dropped: 10-file conflict in storage adapters; origin still uses gate side-table approach

### FEAT-026 sync protocol — wire audit_id from write path
Eitri commit d8c65959. Original bead: axon-4fd3ba6e.
- Populate ChangeEvent.audit_id from the actual audit append result in gateway broadcasts
- Currently using String::new() placeholder
- Reason dropped: cherry-pick brought in references to markdown templates and snapshot APIs from unmerged commits

### FEAT-013: Background index build completion channel
Eitri commit 5eb67fd0. Original bead: axon-5973f060.
- Add tokio mpsc channel to IndexBuilder so background index population emits 'completed'
- Reason dropped: eitri version targeted newer testcontainers API ('SyncRunner', '.start()'); origin's testcontainers-modules is 0.15.0

### FEAT-012: Thread CallerIdentity through request pipeline
Eitri commits e228d77c, 3a6d390a, 8e4727ef, 268f6328. Original bead: axon-e0817efb.
- x-axon-actor HTTP header → CallerIdentity → audit entry actor
- *_with_caller wrapper methods on AxonHandler
- Body 'actor' field is ignored; header is authority
- Empty/missing header → 'anonymous'
- /no-auth mode equivalent semantics
- Reason dropped: cherry-pick added call sites for self.ensure_collection_exists() but no method definition; origin has no such method

### FEAT-023: Rollback handlers (entity, transaction, point-in-time collection)
Eitri commits 717f33c2, e22ebdb5, 5b1eb02c, 91a6e6b1, bbc88b13. Original bead: axon-310ce252 + axon-f1c3183c (docs portion landed).
- Entity rollback (rejects when origin op was create/delete)
- Transaction rollback (compensating tx, rejects if any entity has been further modified)
- PIT collection rollback (best-effort, partial_failures field)
- ADR-015 already landed describes the semantics
- Reason dropped: 120 errors after cherry-pick; references many unmerged supporting types

### Lifecycle: enforce initial state + invalid-state errors
Eitri commits 6ef08463 + 586b4277. Original bead: axon-d6acb336.
- Auto-populate lifecycle field with initial state on create
- Return LifecycleFieldMissing if entity has no value at lifecycle.field
- Return LifecycleStateInvalid if value is not a known state
- New AxonError variants are already in axon-core/src/error.rs
- Reason dropped: cherry-pick brought in tests referencing SnapshotRequest and snapshot_entities (unmerged)

### FEAT-008 US-081: Wire IdempotencyStore into POST /transactions
Eitri commits 72a9b251 + 7403246. Original bead context.
- Idempotency-Key header dedups POST /transactions for 5min TTL
- In-flight dedup returns 409 retryable
- Schema/conflict errors not cached
- Per-database scope
- Reason dropped: cherry-pick gateway.rs references CallerIdentity and other auth machinery that's unmerged

### FEAT-003 US-079: Multi-collection audit tail with unified cursor
Eitri commit 02481341. Original bead context.
- AuditQuery.collection_ids: Vec<CollectionId>
- Unified cursor across collections in audit_id order
- Reason dropped: also adds AuditQuery.database field that origin doesn't have; cascaded into QueryAuditRequest.database

### FEAT-004 US-080: Atomic snapshot-with-cursor endpoint
Eitri commit b9ecd3e3. Original bead context.
- POST /db/{db}/snapshot returns { entities, audit_cursor } atomically
- Optional collections filter
- Pagination

description:
Follow-up from the tenant-selector reactivity fix (commit 2c2e49f).

ui/src/routes/schemas/+page.svelte and ui/src/routes/audit/+page.svelte still use Svelte 4 legacy module-level 'let' for their page-local state instead of Svelte 5 $state. Because of this, adding a $effect that watches getSelectedTenant() would flip each file into runes mode and break reactive invalidation for all the other non-runes 'let' vars (verified: svelte-check produced 30 non_reactive_update warnings on experimental conversion).

The reactivity fix commit 2c2e49f limited itself to making the loaders accept dbName as an explicit parameter so call sites can pass the current selection. It did NOT add $effect-based re-fetch on these two pages. Consequence: switching tenants in the dropdown does not re-fetch schemas or audit entries on those two pages until a manual refresh.

Migrate both files to runes mode:
1. Convert every module-level 'let' to '$state'
2. Convert every '$:' reactive block to '$derived' or '$effect'
3. Add a '$effect(() => { const t = getSelectedTenant(); void loadX(t?.db_name); })' pattern consistent with the fix on collections pages
4. Verify 'bun run build', 'bun run typecheck', 'bun test src/' all pass
5. Verify e2e smoke (schemas list, audit filter) still works against a live server

- [ ] Self-issue works when grants ⊆ caller's own ceiling

- [ ] revoke stores jti in credential_revocations
- [ ] Next request with revoked jti → credential_revoked via B1 pipeline
- [ ] credential_issuances table migration

description:
Credential (JWT) issuance, listing, and revocation endpoints on the control plane. Integrates B1's JwtIssuer, B4's role-ceiling enforcement, and C1's authz pattern.

## Scope

### 1. Routes (extend control_plane_routes.rs)

    .route("/tenants/:id/credentials", post(issue_credential))
    .route("/tenants/:id/credentials", get(list_credentials))
    .route("/tenants/:id/credentials/:jti", delete(revoke_credential))

### 2. Handlers

#### issue_credential

Body: { target_user: "<uuid>", grants: { databases: [...] }, ttl_seconds: u64 }.

Authorization logic:
- Require the caller is either a deployment admin OR the target_user is the caller themselves (self-issue).
- If caller is a deployment admin, they can issue to any user in the tenant (subject to target's role ceiling).
- If self-issue, the grants must be within the caller's own TenantRole::delegable_ops ceiling (via TenantRole::enforce_ceiling from B4).
- Call the same enforce_ceiling for the TARGET user's role even when the caller is a deployment admin — you cannot issue a credential exceeding the target's own rights.

Look up target_user's TenantMember.role via storage.get_tenant_member. If not a member, return 404/403.

Build JwtClaims:
- iss = the deployment's issuer string (from ControlPlaneState config or a const like "axon-local")
- sub = target_user UUID
- aud = tenant_id string
- jti = fresh UUID
- iat = now seconds
- nbf = now seconds (no future window for basic issuance)
- exp = now + ttl_seconds
- grants = request body grants

Sign with B1's JwtIssuer using the deployment signing key (pull from ControlPlaneState or a new configurable HMAC key). For this bead, accept a hardcoded dev key via env var AXON_JWT_KEY (fallback to a fixed default for tests). Production key management is a later bead.

Persist issuance metadata (jti, user_id, tenant_id, issued_at, expires_at, revoked=false, grants JSON) via a new storage method track_credential_issuance. The signed JWT is returned ONCE in the response body; never persist the signed string.

Response: { jwt: "eyJ...", jti: "<uuid>", expires_at: <seconds> }

#### list_credentials

Require tenant_admin (sees all) OR the authenticated user themselves (sees own only). Return metadata rows — never the signed JWT (not persisted anyway). Schema: [{ jti, user_id, issued_at, expires_at, revoked, grants }].

#### revoke_credential

Require tenant_admin OR the jti's owner. Insert row into credential_revocations (table from A1). Invalidate the in-memory LRU cache if B1 exposed one; if not, the next request's SQL check will catch it. Return 204.

### 3. Storage adapter trait methods

    fn track_credential_issuance(
        &self,
        jti: Uuid,
        user_id: UserId,
        tenant_id: TenantId,
        issued_at_ms: i64,
        expires_at_ms: i64,
        grants_json: &str,
    ) -> Result<(), AxonError>;

    fn list_credentials(
        &self,
        tenant_id: TenantId,
        user_filter: Option<UserId>,
    ) -> Result<Vec<CredentialMetadata>, AxonError>;

    fn revoke_credential(
        &self,
        jti: Uuid,
        revoked_by: UserId,
    ) -> Result<(), AxonError>;

CredentialMetadata shape: { jti, user_id, tenant_id, issued_at_ms, expires_at_ms, revoked, grants_json }.

New table (add to auth_schema.rs):

    CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS credential_issuances (
        jti            TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
        user_id        TEXT NOT NULL,
        tenant_id      TEXT NOT NULL,
        issued_at_ms   INTEGER NOT NULL,
        expires_at_ms  INTEGER NOT NULL,
        grants_json    TEXT NOT NULL
    )

credential_revocations table exists already from A1.

Implement in all 3 backends.

### 4. Integration tests: crates/axon-server/tests/control_credentials_test.rs (NEW)

- issue_with_deployment_admin — POST with admin JWT, verify response has signed JWT + jti; verify credential_issuances has a row.
- issue_over_scoped_returns_403 — caller's role is write; tries to issue admin grants → 403 grants_exceed_role.
- self_issue_within_ceiling — caller issues to themselves with grants ⊆ their own role; succeeds.
- list_credentials_admin — admin sees all credentials in tenant.
- list_credentials_self — non-admin sees only their own.
- list_never_returns_signed_jwt — assert response does not contain a "jwt" field anywhere.
- revoke_by_admin — admin revokes a jti; credential_revocations has the row.
- revoke_by_owner — credential owner revokes their own; succeeds.
- revoke_by_unrelated_user_returns_403 — different user tries to revoke someone else's credential; 403.
- revoked_credential_rejected_on_next_request — issue a credential, revoke it, use it via B1's verify pipeline, assert the rejection with error_code credential_revoked.

## Context

- B1 landed JwtIssuer and the 8-step verify pipeline — reuse JwtIssuer::issue and the existing signing key plumbing.
- B4 landed TenantRole::enforce_ceiling and AuthError::GrantsExceedRole — use them verbatim.
- C1 landed control_plane_authz::{require_deployment_admin, require_tenant_admin} — extend with self-issue rules.
- A1 landed credential_revocations table.
- D3/E-phase will later wire the middleware into every data-plane route; for this bead, the revoke flow is validated via a direct call to the B1 pipeline, not a real HTTP request.

## Files to touch

- crates/axon-core/src/auth.rs — add CredentialMetadata struct
- crates/axon-storage/src/auth_schema.rs — add credential_issuances table
- crates/axon-storage/src/adapter.rs — 3 new trait methods
- crates/axon-storage/src/memory.rs, sqlite.rs, postgres.rs — impl
- crates/axon-server/src/control_plane_routes.rs — add routes + handlers
- crates/axon-server/src/control_plane_authz.rs — add self-issue helper or extend require_tenant_admin to allow owner
- crates/axon-server/tests/control_credentials_test.rs — NEW

## Files NOT to touch

- crates/axon-server/src/auth_pipeline.rs — B1 is canonical; only read from it
- crates/axon-server/src/control_plane.rs — legacy CRUD
- crates/axon-core/src/auth.rs variants — do NOT add new AuthError variants (grants_exceed_role already exists from B4)

## Verification

- cargo test -p axon-server --test control_credentials_test (must pass all tests)
- cargo test --workspace green
- cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings clean

acceptance:
- [ ] gateway.rs: /graphql and /graphql/ws moved inside the /tenants/:t/databases/:d/ nested router
- [ ] Playground endpoint URL updated to point at the nested path
- [ ] graphql_mutations.rs hardcoded paths rewritten to /tenants/default/databases/default/graphql
- [ ] graphql_contract.rs hardcoded paths rewritten similarly

description:
Move the GraphQL endpoints under /tenants/:t/databases/:d/graphql so every data-plane surface — REST and GraphQL — is consistently path-prefixed. D3 already migrated the REST routes. This bead is the analogous migration for GraphQL, plus rewriting the 2 graphql test files.

## Scope

### 1. gateway.rs — move GraphQL routes into the nested data-plane router

Currently in gateway.rs around line 2884:

    .route("/graphql", get(graphql_handler).post(graphql_handler))
    .route("/graphql/ws", get(graphql_ws_handler))

And line 2937:

    .route("/graphql/playground", get(graphql_playground_handler))

D3 already nested the REST routes under /tenants/:tenant/databases/:database/. Put the graphql_handler and graphql_ws_handler INSIDE that same nested router so they become /tenants/:tenant/databases/:database/graphql and /tenants/:tenant/databases/:database/graphql/ws.

The playground (/graphql/playground) is a developer UI — it can either:
(a) stay at the top level /graphql/playground, and the playground HTML is updated to point at /tenants/default/databases/default/graphql as the endpoint, OR
(b) move with the rest, under /tenants/:tenant/databases/:database/graphql/playground.

Go with (a) — keep /graphql/playground as a top-level convenience route, but update the playground_source's endpoint URL to the nested path. The playground is a dev tool, not a production API surface.

Update line 2628 region:

    GraphQLPlaygroundConfig::new("/tenants/default/databases/default/graphql").subscription_endpoint("/tenants/default/databases/default/graphql/ws"),

Hard-code `default/default` in the playground's endpoint — the playground is meant for local dev against the default tenant.

### 2. GraphQL handler signature

The handler currently reads the database via X-Axon-Database (maybe indirectly through a middleware). After D3, the REST path uses axum Path<(String, String)> extractor. Do the same for graphql_handler:

    async fn graphql_handler(
        Path((tenant, database)): Path<(String, String)>,
        State(state): State<AppState>,
        req: async_graphql_axum::GraphQLRequest,
    ) -> async_graphql_axum::GraphQLResponse {
        // Resolve the handler for (tenant, database) — reuse the same
        // pattern D3 uses for the REST handlers.
        ...
    }

Same for graphql_ws_handler.

If D3 installed a shared middleware that resolves the handler from path params and puts it into request extensions, just read from extensions — don't re-resolve.

### 3. Rewrite the 2 graphql test files

crates/axon-server/tests/graphql_mutations.rs — lines with `.post("/graphql")`:

- Line 118: .post("/graphql") → .post("/tenants/default/databases/default/graphql")
- Line 128: .post("/graphql") → .post("/tenants/default/databases/default/graphql")
- Similar for any other hardcoded paths

crates/axon-server/tests/graphql_contract.rs — same pattern.

### 4. Add a ws_subscriptions test file (DEFER if too large)

The bead originally asked for a new ws_subscriptions.rs test exercising path-nested subscriptions. If GraphQL subscriptions are already covered in the existing test files, skip this — it's a nice-to-have, not a must-have. If NOT covered, add a minimal test that opens a WebSocket at /tenants/default/databases/default/graphql/ws, sends a subscribe message for a simple entity change, and asserts one event is received. If this test is non-trivial to set up (the WS infra is complex), write it as a compile-only test (#[test] that takes a TypedHandler reference but does not actually connect) or defer to a follow-up bead.

Prefer shipping the core migration + existing test rewrites in this bead over fighting with a new WS test.

### 5. L6 grep assertion for graphql paths

Extend the existing L6 test or add a new one:

    #[test]
    fn no_unprefixed_graphql_references() {
        // Scan source files for "\"/graphql\"" that is NOT under /tenants/
        // Accept /graphql/playground as an exception.
        ...
    }

Alternatively, just assert `git grep '"/graphql"' crates/axon-server/src/ -- --not-playground` finds only the expected nested strings.

## Out of scope

- SDK rewriting (G1)
- UI rewriting (H1)
- Full subscription roundtrip test (defer if non-trivial)

## Context

- D3 landed the REST path migration; reuse its pattern for GraphQL
- The playground is a dev-only HTML page; can stay top-level
- graphql_handler and graphql_ws_handler are in gateway.rs around lines 2524-2576
- The existing graphql_mutations.rs and graphql_contract.rs tests are D3's explicit "don't touch" scope — D4 is when they get migrated

## Files to touch

- crates/axon-server/src/gateway.rs — move the 2 graphql routes into the nested router, update the playground endpoint URL
- crates/axon-server/tests/graphql_mutations.rs — rewrite the hardcoded paths
- crates/axon-server/tests/graphql_contract.rs — rewrite hardcoded paths

## Files NOT to touch

- crates/axon-graphql/* — the internal resolver machinery doesn't care about HTTP paths
- crates/axon-server/src/auth_pipeline.rs — B1
- The REST routes — D3 already migrated

## Verification

- cargo test -p axon-server --test graphql_mutations --test graphql_contract (must both pass)
- cargo test --workspace (all prior tests green)
- cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings clean

AC1. docs/helix/02-design/decisions/create-semantics.md exists and chooses pattern A or B with rationale, including a survey table of current create behavior across (typed GraphQL, untyped commitTransaction, HTTP, gRPC, storage adapter).

AC2. The document explicitly addresses nexiq's downstream contract (which already routes around the bug via commitTransaction) and whether their migration cost is zero (just unskip tests) or non-zero.

AC3. A follow-up implementation bead is filed targeting the chosen pattern. axon-27ee5f04 itself can then be closed as 'superseded by <implementation bead>' or kept open as the implementation tracker.

description:
Codex review of axon-27ee5f04 disagreed with my 'narrow GraphQL bug' framing. Storage put is overwrite by contract (matches NoSQL conventions); the rejection logic for create-with-existing-id lives in commit_transaction's op:create handler, NOT in the storage adapter itself. Fixing only the typed GraphQL path leaves HTTP and gRPC create endpoints with the same upsert behavior — inconsistent semantics across surfaces.

Investigate and decide:

Pattern A: make ALL create paths (typed GraphQL, HTTP /entities POST, gRPC CreateEntity, commitTransaction op:create) reject duplicates uniformly. Move the duplicate-check into the storage adapter or a shared helper, OR enforce at every callsite. Cost: more surface to test; may break existing callers that rely on upsert.

Pattern B: keep storage put as overwrite (existing contract); make the typed GraphQL createXxx + commitTransaction op:create the strict surfaces, leave HTTP/gRPC as documented upsert with createOrFail variants if needed. Cost: API surface inconsistency; need clear docs.

Locations to inspect (codex-supplied):
- crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:3031-3285 (HTTP entity create handler)
- crates/axon-graphql/src/dynamic.rs:7310-7355 (typed createXxx generation)
- crates/axon-server/tests/graphql_mutations.rs:391-430 (existing test scaffolding)
- Storage layer: search for 'put' and 'AlreadyExists' to map current rejection vs upsert behavior

Document the decision in docs/helix/02-design/decisions/create-semantics.md (new file). Then file an implementation bead pointing at the chosen path.

description:
SqliteStorageAdapter owns a current_thread tokio Runtime. When the adapter is constructed and used inside a multi_thread tokio context (gateway tests, production server), block_in_place delegates to the caller's runtime — but the owned current_thread runtime panics on drop because its blocking pool has leftover threads from block_in_place.

## Root Cause
tokio::task::block_in_place registers blocking tasks with the CURRENT runtime's blocking pool. If the current runtime is multi_thread (gateway), tasks get registered there. But when the owned current_thread Runtime drops, it tries to shut down its own blocking pool, which may have stale references.

## Fix
Same pattern as ControlPlaneDb: make the Runtime field Optional. When constructed inside an existing tokio context, don't create a runtime at all — just create the pool using the outer runtime. When constructed outside a tokio context (CLI, embedded), create and own a runtime.

## Files
- crates/axon-storage/src/sqlite.rs: change `rt: tokio::runtime::Runtime` to `_rt: Option<tokio::runtime::Runtime>`
- Update constructors to detect existing runtime via Handle::try_current()

## Acceptance Criteria
- All 258 axon-server gateway lib tests pass
- All axon-storage conformance tests pass
- cargo clippy clean
title: fix(storage): make SqliteStorageAdapter runtime optional to prevent shutdown panics in async test contexts

acceptance: Either --tls-self-signed accepts a SAN list (or auto-includes non-loopback hostnames/IPs) or --tls-cert/--tls-key path flags exist and are documented; downstream consumers can drop NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0 and secure:false.
description:
**Reporter**: nexiq (downstream consumer). Observed 2026-04-22 → 2026-04-26.

## Problem

`axon serve --tls-self-signed` generates a cert with `CN=localhost` and a SAN list limited to `localhost,127.0.0.1,::1,0.0.0.0`. Any consumer that reaches axon by a different name — machine hostname (`sindri`), LAN IP, tailnet hostname (`*.ts.net`), Docker bridge IP — fails the TLS name-check. Browsers refuse to load; Node clients fail with `DEPTH_ZERO_SELF_SIGNED_CERT` / `ERR_TLS_CERT_ALTNAME_INVALID`.

The downstream workarounds nexiq landed (committed `dd5e47a` and similar):
- `vite.config.ts` proxies `/__axon` with `secure: false`, disabling cert verification for the dev proxy.
- `NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0` on every script that talks to axon (`scripts/seed-axon-dev.ts`, `bun run axon:lock`, etc.).

Both are TLS-verification bypasses — they paper over the SAN gap rather than fix it. The bypass leaks beyond dev: every script that imports `@nexiq/axon-client` and runs in Node now has to know whether to trust an unverified cert, and the answer differs per environment.

## Why this matters now

Nexiq is in the middle of FEAT-010 (static Vite SPA + direct-to-Axon). The browser bundle talks straight to axon (with a same-origin proxy in dev). Without a trusted cert path, the dev story is permanently \"click through the browser warning\" + \"export NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0 in your shell rc\". That's bad ergonomics and worse hygiene — teammates leave the env var set across projects.

The downstream bead (`nexiq-036aae6a`) attempts to solve this with a `scripts/dev-cert.sh` that wraps `mkcert` and feeds the leaf cert to `axon serve`. That works, but it's pushing the SAN problem into every consumer's repo. It belongs upstream.

## Suggested fixes (pick one, or do both)

**Option A — accept a SAN list on the self-signed flag**

```
axon serve --tls-self-signed \\
  --tls-self-signed-san localhost,127.0.0.1,sindri,sindri.local,100.x.x.x
```

Generate the cert with the supplied SANs at startup. Default SAN list is the current one (back-compat). Optional: auto-detect machine hostname + non-loopback interface IPs and include them by default if no `--tls-self-signed-san` is provided.

**Option B — accept a custom cert/key**

```
axon serve --tls-cert /path/leaf.pem --tls-key /path/key.pem
```

Skip self-signed generation entirely; trust whatever cert the operator provides. This is the mkcert workflow: `mkcert -install` then `mkcert sindri localhost 127.0.0.1 ...` produces a SAN-rich leaf cert signed by a CA browsers and Node already trust.

If the flags exist already and we missed them, document them prominently in `axon serve --help` and the README's dev-quickstart section. The discovery cost was high enough that the downstream maintainer didn't find them.

## Acceptance

- A fresh checkout + a documented one-liner produces an `axon serve` instance whose cert is trusted by browsers and Node clients reaching axon over a non-loopback hostname (machine hostname, tailnet, LAN IP).
- Downstream consumers can drop `NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0` and `secure: false` from their dev configs.
- `axon serve --help` documents the workflow (whichever option is chosen).

## Related

- nexiq downstream bead `nexiq-036aae6a` will be amended to point at this bead as a blocker once it has an ID.
title: axon serve: --tls-self-signed cert needs SAN list (or document the mkcert leaf-cert workflow)

acceptance: docs/helix/02-design/api-contracts.md adds an Audit Writes section alongside the existing Audit Reads coverage pinning: shape of a standalone audit entry a consumer can emit outside of a mutation; whether audit entries are a separate endpoint/GraphQL mutation OR always piggyback on a transaction; event schema (event_kind, actor, subject_ref, payload_json, origin) and whether consumers can define new event_kind values; idempotency/replay semantics for duplicate audit writes; retention/queryability guarantees relative to entity audit rows; rejection shape (rate limit, unknown event_kind, missing actor); reference invocation from a browser client and from an integration worker. Downstream nexiq can then write access_denied events, login-attempt events, and business-level markers not coupled to a single entity mutation.
description:
<context>
Filed on behalf of nexiq. nexiq's FEAT-010 plan references writing audit events in multiple places:
- access_denied events when a user hits a forbidden route
- invoice approved / engagement status transition audit entries
- first-run user bootstrap events
- dashboard views / PII exposures that policy wants recorded

Axon's current api-contracts.md (2026-04-19 state) covers audit READS at L269-L302 but does not document a consumer-facing audit WRITE path. Entity mutations already leave audit footprints in Axon's internal audit log, but application-level events NOT tied to a specific entity mutation (e.g. access_denied where no entity was touched) need a first-class write contract.

Codex fresh-eyes review of nexiq's plan 2026-04-19 flagged this as a handwaved assumption that blocks Phase 2 implementation.
</context>

<what-nexiq-needs>

Event kinds used by nexiq, priority order:
- access_denied (route, attempted_action, caller_identity, reason)
- engagement.status_changed (piggyback candidate)
- invoice.approved / invoice.voided (piggyback candidate)
- user.first_run_bootstrapped (no entity mutation; separate write)
- login_attempted (no mutation; write before resolving identity)
- sensitive_view (user viewed rate_card, contractor rates — compliance event)
- bulk_export (user exported a report — compliance event)

Shape options:
A) Extend POST /transactions to accept optional audit_events[] alongside operations[], so an app-level event can travel with a mutation OR alone (empty ops + one event).
B) Separate endpoint POST /audit/events with idempotency semantics.
C) GraphQL mutation emitAuditEvent(input) once GraphQL mutations ship.

Downstream preference: (A). Matches transaction-is-the-unit-of-change model, keeps idempotency uniform, lets a route-denied event write atomically with a downgrade operation if wanted.

Schema: same as entity audit rows (actor from auth context, at from server time, subject_ref polymorphic or null, metadata JSON). Free-form event_kind string is fine; Axon records what the consumer asserts.

Query parity: these events must be readable via the same audit-read API so nexiq can show 'all access_denied events on engagement X this week' regardless of source.
</what-nexiq-needs>

<interaction-with-axon-c5cc071a>
The RBAC feature ask includes an access_denied denial shape. If Axon's RBAC layer emits audit entries automatically on denial, this bead narrows to the other event kinds. Confirm.
</interaction-with-axon-c5cc071a>

<priority-rationale>
P1, not P0. Nexiq can ship Phase 2 by piggybacking on entity mutations and suppressing access_denied audit interim. Workaround is cumbersome and creates a compliance gap; filing now so Axon scopes it alongside RBAC.
</priority-rationale>
title: feature: application audit-write API — first-class emit-audit-event contract for downstream consumers

acceptance: docs/helix/02-design/api-contracts.md and ADR-012 pin: transaction idempotency keys are canonical body/input fields, not HTTP headers; GraphQL uses idempotencyKey on CommitTransactionInput and REST compatibility uses idempotency_key in the JSON body. The docs specify field name, character set, max length, case-sensitivity, tenant/database collision scope, TTL, whether failures are cached, exact replay indicator behavior, exact same-key/different-payload behavior, and matching SDK expectations. Empty operations follow FEAT-008: empty transactions commit as no-ops with no audit entry. A downstream client can retry the same transaction after a dropped response without reading source code.
description:
<context>
Filed as a follow-up to axon-d42292e5, which closed shipping api-contracts.md with the idempotency mechanism: POST /transactions accepts an 'idempotency-key' HTTP header, successful responses are cached for five minutes per database, and replays return x-idempotent-cache: hit. Conflicts and validation failures are not cached.

That implementation works, but the placement choice (header) deserves a focused review because it does not fit Axon's API shape as well as a body field would. This bead exists to make the design call explicit, pin the canonical form, and keep back-compat if both are supported. axon-3e37240f (the combined CORS/scope/idempotency follow-up) also mentions this question; this bead supersedes that gap and should be linked there.
</context>

<the-issue>
A static browser bundle making writes over an unreliable connection needs a way to retry after a dropped response without creating duplicates. Axon today exposes that capability via an HTTP header:

    POST /tenants/{t}/databases/{d}/transactions
    idempotency-key: browser-retry-2026-04-19T15:00Z

Successful transaction responses are cached for five minutes per database; a replay returns x-idempotent-cache: hit. Conflicts and validations are intentionally not cached.

The shape (header + success-only cache + scoped per database) is correct in spirit and matches the Stripe/IETF-draft idiom. The question is whether the HTTP header is the right CARRIER given Axon's specific design.
</the-issue>

<arguments-for-header-as-chosen>
1. Industry standard. Stripe, Square, and the IETF draft 'The Idempotency-Key HTTP Header Field' all use this exact pattern. Developers who have seen one of those APIs will not be surprised.
2. Transport-layer separation. The key is part of delivery semantics, not the transaction content. Keeping them separate is logically clean.
3. Allows middleware to short-circuit replays without parsing the body.
4. Works for any endpoint uniformly if the mechanism is generalized later.
</arguments-for-header-as-chosen>

<arguments-for-body-field-instead>
1. Scope is narrow. Idempotency applies only to POST /transactions. There is no cross-endpoint uniformity win to harvest from a header convention.

2. Hidden contract vs visible contract. A header does not appear in the transaction's schema, protobuf, GraphQL mutation type, or SDK method signature. Developers must know it exists from docs alone. A body field (e.g. idempotency_key under the transaction root) appears in every contract surface, shows up in request logs, is self-documenting, and is harder to forget.

3. Middleware fragility. HTTP headers can be stripped, normalized, or case-mangled by reverse proxies, service meshes, or browser security policies. Body fields are inert bytes through any pipeline. Axon on Tailscale largely avoids proxies, but 'avoids' is not 'cannot.' The CORS preflight plus Access-Control-Allow-Headers requirement already proves headers are path-dependent; a body field has no equivalent gotcha.

4. Transport agnosticism. If a GraphQL mutation equivalent is ever added (see axon-3e37240f gap 2), the header convention does not translate: GraphQL has no native HTTP-header-per-mutation pattern. A body field becomes a normal field on the mutation input — one definition, two transports. If REST is demoted to legacy or retired and only GraphQL remains, the header convention strands.

5. Debuggability. '/var/log shows idempotency-key=abc' is discoverable in a curl -v or HAR dump; body fields are reproducibly present in structured logs at the same level as the rest of the operation. A header can be missed by a log formatter that only dumps the body.

6. Structure already exists. The transaction payload is already a JSON object with operations, expected_version, etc. Adding one more field at that layer is natural, matches the existing shape, and costs nothing.

7. Key collision safety. A body field can sit inside the existing tenant/database namespace naturally because the whole request is already scoped by path; there is no temptation to treat the key as a global token the way header-based APIs sometimes do.
</arguments-for-body-field-instead>

<proposed-resolution>
Canonical form: idempotency_key as a body field on the transaction root.

    POST /tenants/default/databases/default/transactions
    Content-Type: application/json

    {
      idempotency_key: browser-retry-2026-04-19T15:00Z,
      operations: [ /* ... */ ]
    }

Supporting details:
  - Field name: idempotency_key (snake_case matches existing request fields like expected_version).
  - Type: string.
  - Length: 1..128 chars (reject shorter/longer with validation_error).
  - Character set: [A-Za-z0-9_.:-]. Explicitly reject whitespace, control characters, and non-ASCII to avoid normalization surprises.
  - Optional: yes. If absent, request is treated as non-idempotent (no cache write, no replay check).
  - Scope: (tenant, database) tuple, as today. Same key under different databases is a different key.
  - Cache TTL: five minutes after successful commit, as today.
  - Failure caching: unchanged — conflicts and validation errors are not cached; retries can resubmit and re-evaluate.
  - Response shape on replay: same body as the original success + x-idempotent-cache: hit header. (Alternatively, echo idempotency_replayed: true in the body for consistency; decide.)
  - GraphQL equivalent: when a GraphQL mutation for transactions exists, idempotency_key is simply an input field on the mutation — no separate convention.

Back-compat: the HTTP header 'idempotency-key' continues to be accepted during a one-release deprecation window. When both are present and differ, reject with a 400 and error code 'idempotency_key_mismatch'. When both are present and match, allow. Document that the body field is canonical and SDKs must emit it; the header is legacy.

SDK change: TS client's commitTransaction(idempotencyKey) sets the body field. Any code today that sets the header should be updated in the same release; emit a deprecation warning if only the header form is used by a direct HTTP caller.

New ADR optional: if this decision is non-trivial for the team, a short ADR (2-3 paragraphs) capturing the rationale is cheap insurance against the question reopening. Otherwise a section in api-contracts.md is sufficient.
</proposed-resolution>

<edge-cases-to-confirm>
- Body size impact: negligible (a 128-char field added to a transaction that likely already carries hundreds of bytes of operations).
- What if a transaction has zero operations and only an idempotency_key? Reject as empty_transaction.
- What if the same key is used on two different operation sets? Same as today with the header — if the first succeeded, second replay returns the cached success regardless of what the second submitted. Document this clearly so callers do not rely on key uniqueness per payload.
- Scope of clock skew for the TTL: cache is server-side, so no client clock involvement. Document anyway.
- Rotation story: if the cache is flushed (restart, database failover) inside the TTL window, replays fall through to normal execution. The OCC expected_version pattern still protects against duplicate writes in that case for most operations. Make sure this is spelled out.
</edge-cases-to-confirm>

<cross-references>
- axon-3e37240f gap 3 — this bead should close it. Leave a note there.
- axon-d42292e5 (closed) — original bead that shipped the header-based mechanism.
- docs/helix/02-design/api-contracts.md — Mutations/OCC/Retries section, line ~193.
- Downstream nexiq FEAT-010 — the Axon client SDK usage pattern depends on this decision.
</cross-references>
notes:
Implemented idempotency decision docs. Evidence: docs/helix/02-design/api-contracts.md and ADR-012 now define idempotency as body/input only: GraphQL idempotencyKey on CommitTransactionInput and REST compatibility idempotency_key in JSON body. No canonical idempotency HTTP header. Docs specify length, ASCII character set, case sensitivity, tenant/database scope, TTL, success replay header, failure caching, same-key/different-payload replay, and FEAT-008 empty transaction no-op behavior.

Nexiq consumer stance, posted 2026-04-19: Nexiq prefers a schema-visible idempotency field on the transaction root, with the old idempotency-key HTTP header accepted only during a deprecation window. Rationale: the retry key is visible in the GraphQL/REST transaction contract, easier to capture in structured logs, and transport-agnostic for GraphQL commitTransaction mutations. Concrete Nexiq SDK usage after the Axon decision: GraphQL calls will use commitTransaction({ idempotencyKey: "invoice-gen-{engagement_id}-{period_start}-{period_end}", operations: [...] }) against CommitTransactionInput.idempotencyKey; REST fallback calls will send JSON { "idempotency_key": "invoice-gen-{engagement_id}-{period_start}-{period_end}", "operations": [...] }. Nexiq accepts Axon’s closed decision in this bead: body/input field canonical, header not canonical.
title: design: idempotency-key canonical placement — body field on /transactions, not HTTP header

description:
Rewrite crates/axon-storage/src/sqlite.rs to use sqlx::SqlitePool instead of rusqlite::Connection.

## Architecture
- Replace struct fields: `conn: Mutex<Connection>` becomes `pool: sqlx::SqlitePool` + `rt: tokio::runtime::Runtime`
- Add block_on helper that handles both async-context and non-async-context callers:
  ```rust
  fn block_on<T>(&self, fut: impl Future<Output = Result<T, sqlx::Error>>) -> Result<T, AxonError> {
      match tokio::runtime::Handle::try_current() {
          Ok(_) => tokio::task::block_in_place(|| self.rt.handle().block_on(fut)),
          Err(_) => self.rt.handle().block_on(fut),
      }.map_err(|e| AxonError::Storage(e.to_string()))
  }
  ```
- Rewrite all ~70 StorageAdapter trait method bodies:
  - rusqlite `params![a, b]` → sqlx `.bind(a).bind(b)`
  - `conn.execute(sql, params)` → `sqlx::query(sql).bind(...).execute(&self.pool).await`
  - `conn.prepare_cached(sql).query(params)` → `sqlx::query(sql).bind(...).fetch_all(&self.pool).await`
  - `conn.query_row` → `sqlx::query(sql).bind(...).fetch_optional(&self.pool).await`
  - Row access: `row.get::<_, String>(0)` → `row.get::<String, _>("column_name")` or use sqlx::FromRow
- Constructors: `open(path)` creates Runtime + pool via `block_on(SqlitePool::connect(url))`. `open_in_memory()` uses `sqlite::memory:` URL.
- Transaction control: `BEGIN IMMEDIATE` / `COMMIT` / `ROLLBACK` via raw SQL through pool

## Also update
- crates/axon-storage/src/auth_schema.rs: rewrite `apply_auth_migrations_sqlite` to accept a `&sqlx::SqlitePool` instead of `&rusqlite::Connection`

## Do NOT change
- The StorageAdapter trait in adapter.rs (stays sync)
- The MemoryStorageAdapter
- The conformance macro or its invocation
- Any downstream crates (axon-api, axon-server, etc.)

## Acceptance Criteria
- cargo check --workspace passes
- cargo test -p axon-storage passes (all SQLite conformance tests)
- `use rusqlite` no longer appears in sqlite.rs
- StorageAdapter trait in adapter.rs is NOT modified
title: sqlx-step-2: rewrite SQLite adapter from rusqlite to sqlx::SqlitePool

description:
Wave 2 bead. Narrow single-mutation undo complementing the wave2-entity-rollback bead. Independent of that bead but touches adjacent code — merge whichever lands first, then rebase the other.

## Problem

The audit log page shows a table of past mutations. Each entry has \`data_before\` and \`data_after\`. The backend already exposes \`POST /audit/revert\` that undoes a single audit entry by rewriting the entity to \`data_before\`. The UI has no action that invokes it.

This is a narrower, safer operation than full entity rollback — \"undo this one specific change\" rather than \"restore to version N\".

## Backend (already in place — no changes)

- \`POST /tenants/{t}/databases/{d}/audit/revert\`
- Handler: crates/axon-server/src/gateway.rs (search for \`audit/revert\`)
- Request body: probably \`{ \"audit_id\": 123, \"actor\": \"ui\" }\` — **verify shape by reading the handler before writing api.ts**
- Response: probably the new entity + new audit entry — verify

## UI changes

### 1. api.ts

Add:
\`\`\`ts
export async function revertAuditEntry(
  auditId: number,
  scope: Scope,
): Promise<{ entity: EntityRecord; audit_entry: AuditEntry }> { ... }
\`\`\`

(Exact shape depends on what the handler returns.)

### 2. Database-wide audit page

File: ui/src/routes/tenants/[tenant]/databases/[database]/audit/+page.svelte

In the right-side \"Entry Detail\" panel (which already shows data_before/data_after), add a \"Revert this change\" button next to the entry metadata, shown only when \`data_before\` is non-null (nothing to revert for a pure create). Clicking:

1. Opens a confirmation modal showing: \"Revert entry #123 (collection/entity_id)?\" + the diff. 
2. On confirm, calls \`revertAuditEntry\`.
3. On success, shows a success toast, reloads the audit list, keeps the panel focused on the entry.
4. On error (especially if the entity was mutated after this audit entry), surfaces the server error clearly.

### 3. Per-entity History tab

File: ui/src/routes/tenants/[tenant]/databases/[database]/collections/[name]/+page.svelte

In the History tab (inside the entity detail panel), add the same \"Revert this change\" button per audit entry row, gated on data_before being non-null.

## Tests

Add to ui/tests/e2e/wave2-rollback.spec.ts (or new file):

- **Revert via per-entity history** — create entity v1, update to v2, navigate to entity, click History tab, click \"Revert this change\" on the v2 update entry, confirm. Assert entity data matches v1.
- **Revert via database audit page** — same data setup, navigate to audit page, select the update entry, click Revert, confirm. Assert a new audit entry appears and entity data reverts.
- **Revert of a create fails or is hidden** — entity has only v1 (create). Revert button should not be shown (data_before is null).

## Files to touch

- ui/src/lib/api.ts — revertAuditEntry
- ui/src/routes/tenants/[tenant]/databases/[database]/audit/+page.svelte — button + confirm modal
- ui/src/routes/tenants/[tenant]/databases/[database]/collections/[name]/+page.svelte — button in History tab
- ui/tests/e2e/wave2-rollback.spec.ts — new describe OR extend existing

## Verification

- bun run typecheck / build / cargo rebuild / restart / playwright green

acceptance: docs/helix/01-frame/features/FEAT-*-access-control.md (new spec) + ADR(s) as needed pin the three levels of access control Axon enforces at the data layer: (a) entity-level (caller cannot see an entity at all if no policy grants visibility); (b) row-level (caller sees only rows matching a policy predicate, evaluated against their resolved identity + role); (c) field-level (on otherwise-visible rows, sensitive fields are redacted or rejected). Policies are expressed in/alongside the schema (not ad-hoc code), applied uniformly to HTTP REST and GraphQL reads and writes, and include a documented denial shape the caller can distinguish from 'row does not exist'. A reference 'nexiq' policy set demonstrates: consultants see only engagements where engagement.members[].user_id == current_user_id; contractors additionally lose visibility on rate_card and budget_cents fields on engagements they can otherwise see; ops_managers read all billing/invoice entities firm-wide but cannot see contract rate cards. Mutations honor the same rules — a consultant cannot write to engagements.status on an engagement they cannot read. The denial contract is explicit (e.g. error code 'forbidden' with optional field path).
description:
<context>
Filed on behalf of nexiq (/home/erik/Projects/nexiq). nexiq's architecture is ADR-009 static browser bundle talking directly to Axon over Tailscale. That means every tailnet user can reach Axon endpoints directly with whatever query/mutation they want — there is no intermediate nexiq server that can filter responses. The prior nexiq assumption was that RBAC would be a browser-side data-access-layer concern (auth-design.md:96-118). A codex fresh-eyes review of nexiq's FEAT-010 execution plan correctly flagged that this is not a security boundary: a malicious or curious tailnet user with a dev tools console can bypass any browser-side filtering. Nexiq's owner has decided: Axon needs first-class field/row/entity filtering, enforced at the data layer.

This is the single biggest axon consumer need surfaced by nexiq to date. It is not satisfied by any existing axon bead (the create-time role guard in axon-0eeb9fa5 is scoped to self-promotion, not general read/write RBAC).
</context>

<three-levels>

# 1. Entity-level — whole entity hidden

Some entities are invisible to some roles. A consultant cannot enumerate or read engagements they are not a member of; Axon should behave as if those engagements do not exist (404 / empty list), not leak existence via an authorization error.

Specific nexiq policies:
- engagements: consultants/contractors only see rows where engagement.members[].user_id == current_user_id (link-based); partners/admins see all
- contracts: same scope as engagements (since contract ∈ engagement)
- rate_card entries: only partners/admins/ops_managers can see any rate_card entity
- time_entries (own): consultants/contractors only see their own; partners see for engagements they lead; admins see all; ops_managers see submitted+ across firm

# 2. Row-level — filtered list results

list/query operations must apply the row filter server-side before pagination, so a consultant's 'SELECT * FROM engagements LIMIT 100' returns only their assigned engagements, not the first 100 engagements of the firm filtered to 3 after the fact.

Specific nexiq policies (continue from above):
- invoices: ops_managers see all; partners see for engagements they lead; admins see all; consultants/contractors see none (redacts would leak pricing structure)
- tasks: same scope as engagements for consultants/contractors
- users: admins/partners see all; consultants/contractors see users who share at least one of their engagements; ops_managers see all

# 3. Field-level — row visible, specific fields hidden

On rows the caller CAN see, some fields remain hidden.

Specific nexiq policies:
- engagements.budget_cents, engagements.rate_card_id: hidden from contractor user_role on any row
- time_entries.rate_cents, time_entries.cost_cents: hidden from consultants/contractors on their own rows (they see hours, not dollar amounts)
- users.email, users.tailscale_login: visible to admins/partners/ops_managers; hidden from consultants/contractors when they can see the user row

Field-level redaction should return null or omit the field, not reject the whole query. This lets one GraphQL query work for every role with consistent shape (nulls where denied), avoiding per-role query permutations.
</three-levels>

<policy-expression-options>

A) Policy-as-code in a new Axon extension language / Rust closures — maximum flexibility, coupled to backend.
B) Schema-annotated declarative (e.g. @visibleTo(roles: [admin, partner]) directives on fields and collections, plus @rowFilter(expr: "engagement.members[].user_id == .id") on collections) — expressive enough for nexiq's matrix, schema-first, introspectable.
C) Capability-based (named capabilities per role; resolvers check) — simple but doesn't naturally express row filters.

Downstream (nexiq) preference: (B). Matches Axon's schema-first posture, works for both HTTP and GraphQL, policies can be unit-tested against the schema independently of any client.
</policy-expression-options>

<denial-contract>

Must distinguish (from the caller's perspective):
- entity/row does not exist (404 on GET, empty list on list) — caller cannot infer whether the entity is there but denied, or genuinely absent
- write to a denied row fails with 'forbidden' with a stable error code the SDK can classify — not 'validation error' (would be misleading) and not 'not_found' (distinct case where the caller is trying to create at a new path)
- field-level denial: field comes back null on read OR is missing from the payload; writes that include a denied field return 'forbidden' with the field path

Idempotency interaction: idempotent replays of a write that was forbidden should return the same forbidden response, not silently succeed.
</denial-contract>

<nexiq-role-matrix>

Full permission matrix per docs/helix/01-frame/prd.md:177-195. Five user-level roles × nine collections × read/write operations. Axon's policy engine must be rich enough to encode all cells. If a cell cannot be expressed in the chosen policy language, nexiq cannot safely migrate that feature to direct browser-to-Axon.

Key example rows:
- Consultant × invoices: no read (not even list in own engagement context), no write
- Contractor × engagements: read own assignments only, budget/rate fields redacted, no write (status or otherwise)
- Ops Manager × contracts: read full, but rate_card entries within the contract are field-redacted
- Ops Manager × any: cannot be assigned as engagement member, cannot approve time, cannot transition engagement status
- Partner × any they don't lead: read-only access (cannot approve time, cannot transition status)
- Admin × any: full

Nexiq will provide a machine-readable version of this matrix once Axon decides on the expression language.
</nexiq-role-matrix>

<performance-and-introspection>

- Row filters must be index-assisted when the underlying scope link (e.g. engagement_members) is indexable; otherwise a consultant's list query becomes a full-scan filter in Axon.
- Introspection: an authenticated caller should be able to discover (via a metadata endpoint or field) what they would be allowed to do on a collection — required for nexiq to hide affordances in the UI without separately checking client-side.
- Dry-run / check: a '/policy/explain' or similar endpoint that returns 'would this operation be allowed' without executing, for UI pre-validation of destructive actions.
</performance-and-introspection>

<what-this-unblocks>

- Nexiq FEAT-010 Phase 2 (data layer migration per domain) can proceed safely; per-role queries become natural instead of 'every consumer filters'.
- The 'consultants never receive rate_card fields' language in nexiq's plan becomes a real guarantee, not a client-filtering fiction.
- Future Axon consumers (agentic, other frontends, integration workers) benefit from the same data-layer enforcement and don't re-implement filtering.
- Removes the risk a leaking browser bundle ever exposes data a user should not see.
</what-this-unblocks>

<what-nexiq-does-while-waiting>

Until this ships, nexiq's Phase 2 migration will land with browser-side filtering as a 'best effort' layer, explicitly documented as not a security boundary. Sensitive dashboards (firm-wide billing) remain behind a defensive posture: nexiq's code requires partner/admin role before issuing the query; any consultant who bypasses the UI and hits Axon directly will succeed at this intermediate stage. This is acceptable in a tailnet-only dogfood posture but must be closed before any multi-firm or adversarial deployment.

Nexiq will consume this feature incrementally: entity-level first (biggest wins), row-level second (most of the matrix), field-level last (fewest surfaces, and nulls are acceptable).
</what-nexiq-does-while-waiting>

Most likely cause: execute-loop worker malfunction during the close step. Do not re-close without an actual implementation or explicit deferred-v2 decision.

description:
Add the ADR-018-compliant tenant-membership control plane on top of the existing /control/tenants routes. The existing create/list/delete in crates/axon-server/src/control_plane.rs and control_plane_routes.rs STAY AS-IS — they use a legacy tenants table (id, name, db_name, created_at) that pre-dates ADR-018. Full schema migration is a later cleanup pass post-D3.

This bead adds:

1. RBAC gating on the existing create/list/delete endpoints via a new deployment-admin check
2. NEW membership endpoints (POST/DELETE /control/tenants/:id/members/:user_id) that write to the auth_schema tenant_users table from A1 (not the legacy tenants table)
3. NEW retention policy endpoints (GET/PUT /control/tenants/:id/retention) that read/write a per-tenant retention policy from a new storage adapter method
4. Integration tests proving the whole thing works end-to-end with JWT credentials

## Scope — exactly what to add

### 1. Deployment-admin check

Add crates/axon-server/src/control_plane_authz.rs:

    //! Authorization helpers for the control plane.

    use axon_core::auth::{ResolvedIdentity, TenantId, TenantRole, UserId};
    use axon_core::error::AxonError;

    /// Check that the caller is a deployment-level administrator.
    pub fn require_deployment_admin(
        identity: &ResolvedIdentity,
        user_roles: &crate::user_roles::UserRoleStore,
    ) -> Result<(), AxonError> {
        // Look up the caller's login/email in user_roles.
        // If role is Admin, return Ok.
        // Otherwise return AxonError::Forbidden("deployment admin required").
    }

    /// Check that the caller is an administrator of the named tenant.
    pub fn require_tenant_admin(
        identity: &ResolvedIdentity,
        tenant_id: TenantId,
    ) -> Result<(), AxonError> {
        // Check identity.grants — caller must have admin op, or be deployment admin.
    }

Wire both into the route handlers below.

### 2. New membership endpoints in control_plane_routes.rs

Add to the existing routes:

    .route("/tenants/:id/members", get(list_tenant_members))
    .route("/tenants/:id/members/:user_id", put(upsert_tenant_member))
    .route("/tenants/:id/members/:user_id", delete(remove_tenant_member))

Handlers:

- list_tenant_members — require tenant-admin or deployment-admin, call state.storage.list_tenant_members(tenant_id) (new method), return JSON array.

- upsert_tenant_member — body is { role: "admin"|"write"|"read" }. Require deployment-admin. Call state.storage.upsert_tenant_member(tenant_id, user_id, role). Return 200/201.

- remove_tenant_member — require deployment-admin. Call state.storage.remove_tenant_member. Return 204 on success, 404 if not found.

### 3. New list_tenant_members storage method

Extend StorageAdapter trait in crates/axon-storage/src/adapter.rs:

    fn list_tenant_members(
        &self,
        tenant_id: TenantId,
    ) -> Result<Vec<TenantMember>, AxonError> {
        let _ = tenant_id;
        Ok(vec![])
    }

Implement in memory.rs, sqlite.rs, postgres.rs. SQL:
    SELECT tenant_id, user_id, role, added_at_ms
    FROM tenant_users
    WHERE tenant_id = ?
    ORDER BY added_at_ms ASC

### 4. Retention policy endpoints

    .route("/tenants/:id/retention", get(get_retention_policy))
    .route("/tenants/:id/retention", put(set_retention_policy))

Default retention: 7 years (220_752_000 seconds) with archive action. Stored as a per-tenant row in a new tenant_retention_policies table. Add the migration here:

    CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS tenant_retention_policies (
        tenant_id TEXT NOT NULL,
        archive_after_seconds INTEGER NOT NULL,
        purge_after_seconds INTEGER,
        updated_at_ms INTEGER NOT NULL,
        PRIMARY KEY (tenant_id)
    )

Wire into apply_auth_migrations_sqlite and apply_auth_migrations_postgres.

Storage adapter methods:

    fn get_retention_policy(&self, tenant_id: TenantId) -> Result<Option<RetentionPolicy>, AxonError>;
    fn set_retention_policy(&self, tenant_id: TenantId, policy: &RetentionPolicy) -> Result<(), AxonError>;

RetentionPolicy lives in axon-core/src/auth.rs (additive):

    #[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, Serialize, Deserialize)]
    pub struct RetentionPolicy {
        pub archive_after_seconds: u64,
        pub purge_after_seconds: Option<u64>,
    }

    impl Default for RetentionPolicy {
        fn default() -> Self {
            Self {
                archive_after_seconds: 7 * 365 * 24 * 60 * 60,
                purge_after_seconds: None,
            }
        }
    }

### 5. Wire the JWT middleware into /control routes

The existing /control routes are currently protected by legacy auth. This bead does NOT re-wire the entire router — that's D3's job. For this bead, add the JWT middleware as an OPTIONAL layer: if the request has Authorization header, run the JWT pipeline and install ResolvedIdentity. If not, fall back to legacy auth. The handlers check for ResolvedIdentity first and fall through to legacy if not present.

Concretely: add a new middleware application on the NEW membership + retention routes only — do NOT touch the existing tenant CRUD routes. Blast radius stays small.

### 6. Integration test: crates/axon-server/tests/control_tenants_test.rs (NEW)

- admin_can_list_members — set up tenant with 2 members, issue JWT for deployment admin, GET /control/tenants/:id/members, assert 2 entries.
- non_admin_cannot_upsert_member — non-admin JWT, PUT .../members/:uid, assert 403.
- admin_upsert_creates_row — PUT with admin JWT, assert 200/201 and the row appears in GET.
- admin_remove_deletes_row — DELETE with admin JWT, assert 204 and row is gone.
- retention_default_is_7y — on fresh tenant, GET /retention returns 220752000.
- retention_can_be_overridden — PUT custom policy, GET returns it.
- SCN-012 user_in_two_tenants — tenant A with user as admin + tenant B with same user as read; GET /members on both; verify independence.

SCN-016 (BYOC deployment boundary) deferred to a follow-up bead — requires multi-process harness.

## Context you must know

### Already landed
- A1: users, user_identities, tenant_users, credential_revocations, tenant_databases tables
- A2: TenantId, UserId, User, TenantMember, TenantRole, ResolvedIdentity
- B1: JWT pipeline, middleware that installs ResolvedIdentity, StorageAdapter::get_tenant_member + get_user + is_jti_revoked
- B2: record_auth_rejection observability envelope
- B3: upsert_user_identity for federation
- B4: upsert_tenant_member + remove_tenant_member + TenantRole::delegable_ops + TenantRole::enforce_ceiling + AuthError::GrantsExceedRole

### What exists already in control plane
- crates/axon-server/src/control_plane.rs — ControlPlaneDb struct with create_tenant/list_tenants/delete_tenant (LEGACY schema with db_name, DO NOT TOUCH)
- crates/axon-server/src/control_plane_routes.rs — routes nested at /control
- crates/axon-server/src/user_roles.rs — existing deployment-admin user_roles table

### Why a new retention table
A1 intentionally left retention out. Adding it here keeps the migration to this one bead. Name it tenant_retention_policies.

## Files to touch

- crates/axon-core/src/auth.rs — add RetentionPolicy struct
- crates/axon-storage/src/adapter.rs — add 3 trait methods: list_tenant_members, get_retention_policy, set_retention_policy
- crates/axon-storage/src/auth_schema.rs — add tenant_retention_policies table migration
- crates/axon-storage/src/memory.rs, sqlite.rs, postgres.rs — implement the 3 new methods
- crates/axon-server/src/control_plane_authz.rs — NEW
- crates/axon-server/src/control_plane_routes.rs — add new routes + handlers
- crates/axon-server/src/lib.rs — register the new authz module
- crates/axon-server/tests/control_tenants_test.rs — NEW

## Files NOT to touch

- crates/axon-server/src/control_plane.rs — leave the legacy tenant CRUD alone
- crates/axon-server/src/auth.rs — legacy Tailscale path, out of scope
- The existing /control/tenants, /control/users, /control/cors routes — out of scope

## Verification

- cargo test -p axon-server --test control_tenants_test (new file, must pass)
- cargo test -p axon-server (all prior tests still pass)
- cargo test -p axon-storage (new trait methods)
- cargo test -p axon-core (RetentionPolicy serde)
- cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings clean

acceptance: Tenant, user, tenant member, credential, and database control-plane views use GraphQL queries/mutations instead of REST once axon-de1f0536 lands. Collection list and collection metadata views use collections/collection GraphQL queries. Entity list views use typed or generic GraphQL connection queries with filters, sorting, pagination, and total counts. Entity detail views fetch entity data, relationship edges/neighbors, lifecycle state, and recent audit entries through a single GraphQL query where practical. Entity create/read/update/patch/delete, link create/delete, lifecycle transition, schema/collection admin, entity rollback, and credential issue/list/revoke workflows use GraphQL. REST calls in the UI are limited to health/metrics/static assets, stream/file transports, and documented break-glass operations such as transaction/point-in-time rollback. Playwright E2E runs without human intervention against the real in-memory server and proves workflows by asserting persisted state, version increments, audit rows, relationship rendering, credential secrecy, and error messages. E2E includes network assertions that migrated workflows call /graphql and do not call REST except documented exceptions. The GraphQL console remains available and has meaningful query/error tests beyond page-load checks. UI docs identify Axon UI as the canonical GraphQL consumer example.

acceptance:
AC1. effectivePolicy and explainPolicy GraphQL fields accept optional policyOverride: JSON argument.

AC2. When policyOverride is non-null, the evaluation uses the override's access_control; when null/omitted, behavior is unchanged.

AC3. Malformed policyOverride returns a typed error (invalid_policy_override) with a field-level diagnostic, not a 500.

AC4. Rust unit tests cover the four scenarios above and pass: cargo test -p axon-graphql.

AC5. cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings clean.

AC6. tests/e2e/policy-authoring.spec.ts passes (no regression in active-policy path).
description:
Codex review of axon-80979cb8 flagged this hidden gap: the UI side now sends `policyOverride` arguments in AxonUiEffectivePolicy and explain queries (api.ts:1502-1565), but the GraphQL resolvers in crates/axon-graphql/src/dynamic.rs do NOT accept or apply policyOverride. Verified: `grep policyOverride crates/axon-graphql/src/` returns nothing. The UI work landed with a silent no-op — proposed-matrix state fetched against the active access_control regardless of caller intent.

Scope:

A. Add an optional `policyOverride: JSON` argument to two field resolvers in crates/axon-graphql/src/dynamic.rs:
   - effectivePolicy(collection, entityId, policyOverride)
   - explainPolicy(input, policyOverride) — and the dry-run variant explainPolicyDryRun if it exists

B. Plumb the override through the policy-evaluator path so that when policyOverride is non-null, the evaluation runs against the supplied access_control instead of the schema's persisted active access_control. The schema's draft.access_control is the canonical shape.

C. Validate the override at parse time; reject malformed policy with a typed error code (e.g. `invalid_policy_override` with field-level diagnostics matching the policy-validation error shape used elsewhere).

D. Ensure existing callers that pass null/omitted policyOverride get exactly the previous behavior (active policy).

E. Rust unit tests in crates/axon-graphql/tests/policy_dryrun_test.rs (or extending an existing test file) covering: null override → active policy result; valid override → override applied; malformed override → typed error; override that flips a decision allow→deny → reflected in result.

F. UI integration sanity check: run the existing tests/e2e/policy-authoring.spec.ts — should pass without changes (no UI changes needed; UI already sends the field). If a fixture needs updating to exercise the new path, do that minimally.

Out of scope: UI render of proposed column (axon-93fcc7f7), delta module (axon-4b264f4e), Playwright delta assertions (axon-1fb17586). This bead is server-only.
title: build(feat-031): GraphQL policyOverride support for explainPolicy and effectivePolicy (backend half of axon-80979cb8)

acceptance:
- [ ] api.ts has previewEntityRollback and applyEntityRollback with correct request/response shapes
- [ ] Collection detail page has a Rollback tab alongside Data/History/Links/Lifecycle/Markdown
- [ ] Rollback tab lists prior versions from entity audit
- [ ] Clicking a version shows the dry_run diff (per-field path/kind/description) and target entity JsonTree

description:
Wave 2 bead. Independent of the user-provisioning beads — can be worked in parallel but touches the same file as the lifecycle tab (ui/src/routes/tenants/[tenant]/databases/[database]/collections/[name]/+page.svelte), so serialize with other wave-2 UI beads that touch that file.

## Problem

FEAT-023 says operators should be able to preview and apply entity-level rollbacks. The backend endpoint exists and is well-shaped, but nothing in the UI surfaces it. The audit History tab on the entity detail (shipped in wave 1) shows the timeline but has no \"go back to this version\" action.

## Backend (already in place — no changes needed)

- \`POST /tenants/{t}/databases/{d}/collections/{c}/entities/{id}/rollback\`
- Handler: crates/axon-server/src/gateway.rs:1662
- Request body:
  \`\`\`json
  {
    \"to_version\": 2,              // optional — use one of to_version or to_audit_id
    \"to_audit_id\": \"12345\",       // optional
    \"expected_version\": 3,         // optional OCC guard
    \"actor\": \"ui\",
    \"dry_run\": false               // set true to get preview
  }
  \`\`\`
- Response on dry_run: \`{ \"current\": Entity | null, \"target\": Entity, \"diff\": { field: FieldDiff, ... } }\`
- Response on apply: \`{ \"entity\": Entity, \"audit_entry\": AuditEntry }\`

## UI changes

### 1. api.ts

Add:
\`\`\`ts
export type FieldDiff = { path: string; kind: string; description: string };

export type RollbackPreview = {
  current: EntityRecord | null;
  target: EntityRecord;
  diff: Record<string, FieldDiff>;
};

export type RollbackApplied = {
  entity: EntityRecord;
  audit_entry: AuditEntry;
};

export async function previewEntityRollback(
  collection: string,
  id: string,
  toVersion: number,
  scope: Scope,
): Promise<RollbackPreview> { /* POST ?dry_run=true */ }

export async function applyEntityRollback(
  collection: string,
  id: string,
  toVersion: number,
  expectedVersion: number,
  scope: Scope,
): Promise<RollbackApplied> { /* POST dry_run=false */ }
\`\`\`

### 2. Collection detail page — new \"Rollback\" tab

File: ui/src/routes/tenants/[tenant]/databases/[database]/collections/[name]/+page.svelte

Add a new tab button alongside Data / History / Links / Lifecycle / Markdown: \"Rollback\". Inside the tab:

- Load the entity audit via the already-fetched \`auditEntries\` state (from the History tab) — if empty, call fetchEntityAudit.
- Render a table of prior versions (v1, v2, ... up to current — 1) with columns: version, timestamp, actor, mutation, button \"Preview rollback\".
- Clicking \"Preview rollback\" calls \`previewEntityRollback(...)\`. Show the returned diff below the table: for each field in \`preview.diff\`, list path + kind + description. Also show the target entity data as a JsonTree below.
- Below the diff: \"Apply rollback\" button (primary) + \"Cancel\" button. Clicking Apply calls \`applyEntityRollback\` with \`expected_version = selectedEntity.version\`. On success, update selectedEntity + entities list (same pattern as saveEntity), show a success message, switch back to the Data tab, clear the preview state.
- On version conflict (409 / stale version), show an error and prompt to reload.

### 3. Reuse auditEntries cache

The History tab already loads \`auditEntries\`. The Rollback tab should reuse the same state; refactor \`loadAuditTab\` to be callable from either tab without redundant fetches. Add a helper \`async function ensureAuditLoaded()\` that loads if empty.

### 4. Test data setup — need entities with multiple versions

Tests must create an entity, update it a few times to generate history, then roll back.

## Tests

New describe in ui/tests/e2e/wave1-capabilities.spec.ts OR a new wave2-rollback.spec.ts:

- **Entity rollback preview** — create entity v1, update to v2, update to v3. Navigate to entity. Click Rollback tab. Click \"Preview rollback\" for v1. Assert diff is visible and contains the changed fields. Assert target data matches v1's data.
- **Entity rollback apply** — continue from above. Click \"Apply rollback\". Assert a success message. Assert selectedEntity.version is 4 (a new version). Assert the data matches v1's data. Assert a new audit entry appears in the History tab.
- **Version conflict** — (optional, harder to trigger reliably) concurrently update the entity out-of-band and assert the apply surfaces a meaningful error.

Test helper in tests/e2e/helpers.ts: \`updateTestEntity(request, db, collection, id, data, expectedVersion)\`.

## Files to touch

- ui/src/lib/api.ts — types + preview/apply functions
- ui/src/routes/tenants/[tenant]/databases/[database]/collections/[name]/+page.svelte — new tab + state + handlers
- ui/tests/e2e/helpers.ts — updateTestEntity
- ui/tests/e2e/wave2-rollback.spec.ts OR wave1-capabilities.spec.ts — NEW describe block

## Verification

- bun run typecheck in ui/ (0 errors)
- bun run build in ui/ green
- cargo build --release --bin axon -p axon-cli (UI embedded)
- install + systemctl restart
- bunx playwright test --config playwright.smoke.config.ts — all green

acceptance: docs/helix/02-design/api-contracts.md (or equivalent interface spec) pins each of the five gaps below with request/response examples so a static-bundle browser client can call Axon without guessing: (1) current-user resolution; (2) filter syntax; (3) link traversal; (4) schema-evolution/version handshake; (5) mutation failure/idempotency/OCC semantics. A reference browser client could be implemented against the doc alone.
description:
<context>
Filed on behalf of the downstream nexiq project (erik.labianca, ~/Projects/nexiq). nexiq has ADR-009 (static browser bundle + direct browser→Axon over Tailscale) in Accepted status. Decomposition of FEAT-010 (Vite SPA migration) is blocked because the Axon HTTP interface is only partially specified. This bead collects the specific unknowns an engineer would hit immediately so Axon can close them in one pass.

Confirmed already pinned (do not revisit): HTTP transport for browser, Tailscale whois auth, entity schemas for all 9 collections, 13 typed link declarations, cursor pagination convention.
</context>

<gaps>

# 1. Current-user resolution on app load

A static browser bundle has no other identity source besides what Axon can tell it. nexiq FEAT-010 R3 currently marks this TBD.

Need documented:
- Canonical endpoint shape (e.g. GET /whoami returning { user_id, tailscale_login, email, roles[] }) OR explicit confirmation that a user-select flow is permanent.
- Behavior when the Tailscale peer has no matching users-collection record (first-run bootstrap vs 401 vs auto-create).
- Whether the response carries a user entity reference (user_id) that can be used in subsequent writes as the 'actor' for audit_log.

# 2. List filter syntax

Browser needs to issue queries like 'approved, uninvoiced time_entries for engagement X, week of 2026-W16'. Currently no doc specifies how filters are expressed in the HTTP API.

Need documented:
- Filter language: URL query params? JSON body on POST /query? GraphQL-over-HTTP? Choose and pin.
- Operator set: equality, IN, range (gte/lte), null-check, boolean AND/OR. Show each with a concrete example.
- Sort: field + direction, multi-field precedence.
- Interaction with pagination (cursor stable across filter changes? documented?).
- Index requirements — which fields are queryable on each collection without a schema change.

# 3. Link traversal HTTP shape

data-design.md lists 13 link types. Architecture sequence diagrams assume the browser can do one-hop and multi-hop traversals (e.g. 'engagement → phases → time_entries where status=approved'). HTTP shape unspecified.

Need documented:
- Single-hop: GET /entities/engagements/{id}/links/phases? Or /entities/engagements/{id}?include=phases?
- Traversal with filter: how to combine a link traversal with a filter on the far side (see gap #2).
- Multi-hop: is there a depth parameter or does the client stitch sequential calls?
- Inverse traversal: links are directional — document both directions when relevant.

# 4. Schema evolution / version handshake

Static bundles cannot be hot-patched. Once shipped, a browser build is pinned against an Axon schema version. Axon's schema may evolve; FEAT-010 must know how to detect incompatibility at load time and handle it (reload? feature-flag off? fail closed with a user-visible message?).

Need documented:
- Version handshake endpoint on app load (GET /schema or /version returning current schema hash/version and a compatibility window).
- How the browser expresses its expected schema version on requests (header? query param?).
- Axon behavior on mismatch: 409? reject specific fields? accept and coerce? silent.
- Policy for additive vs breaking changes (new optional field = no browser change; renamed field = how is this signalled?).
- Deprecation window (nexiq bundles may be cached in browsers for days).

# 5. Mutation failure, idempotency, OCC semantics

Browser will do create/update/delete over unreliable networks. The contract must be specific enough that a static bundle can implement safe retries.

Need documented:
- OCC: which entities carry version; what status code + payload is returned on conflict; whether the response includes the current server version for optimistic refresh.
- Idempotency keys: is there a header like Idempotency-Key the browser can send so a dropped response doesn't duplicate a write? What's the retention window?
- Partial-failure: if a write touches multiple entities or a link + entity atomically, is that a single transaction? How is partial failure surfaced?
- Error shape: concrete JSON body for each failure class (validation, conflict, unauthorized, not-found, server error). Field names an SDK can switch on.
- Retry guidance: which status codes are safe to retry, which are not, and whether retry requires the idempotency key.

</gaps>

<priority-rationale>
Gap #1 (/whoami) and gap #2 (filter syntax) block the very first endpoint the browser would call. Gaps #3, #4, #5 block invoice generation, long-term deployment, and network-layer correctness respectively. All five are needed before FEAT-010 beads can be written with concrete ACs.
</priority-rationale>

<how-to-verify>
The output of this bead should be a doc (ideally under docs/helix/02-design/ or docs/helix/03-interface/) a nexiq engineer can open and write a working browser client from. Sanity test: a reviewer unfamiliar with Axon internals should be able to answer each of the five questions above with a specific endpoint shape + example in <1 minute of reading.
</how-to-verify>

events.summary: Reviewed against current gateway routes, API request types, TS client, and tests. Recommended changes: add CORS/static-SPA origin policy as an explicit gap; frame identity around existing GET /auth/me rather than inventing /whoami; distinguish current schema endpoints from the not-yet-implemented app-load schema/version handshake; document /traverse limitations because HTTP traversal currently has no far-side filters/hop_filter or link metadata response; pin idempotency as transactions-only with 5-minute success cache scoped by database; either add SDK examples/follow-up work for /auth/me, traversal, transactions, schema handshake, or state those are raw HTTP only.

Implemented the static-SPA contract behind the doc: GraphQL list fields now accept filter/sort/limit/afterId and map to FilterNode/SortField; HTTP POST /traverse/{collection}/{id} accepts hop_filter and returns link metadata; GET /schema returns collection schemas plus stable schema_hash and 409 schema_mismatch on x-axon-schema-hash mismatch; /auth/me includes nullable user_id/tenant_id for JWT context; TS HTTP client exposes me(), schemaManifest(), graphql(), traverse(), and commitTransaction(idempotencyKey). Added docs/helix/02-design/api-contracts.md with working examples. Verified cargo fmt --check, cargo check --workspace, clippy -D warnings, axon-server graphql_contract/api_contract tests, auth_me tests, and SDK lint/test.
