acceptance: GET /traverse/{collection}/{id}?direction=reverse&link_type=assigned-to returns entities whose forward 'assigned-to' links point TO the given entity; existing forward traverse behavior is unchanged (no direction param or direction=forward); at least one API contract test covers reverse traversal; bun test passes
description:
The HTTP traverse endpoint GET /traverse/{collection}/{id} currently ignores traversal direction (hardcodes Forward in gateway.rs). The TraverseRequest struct and AxonHandler::traverse() already support TraverseDirection::Reverse, but the HTTP handler passes `direction: Default::default()` unconditionally.

Add a `direction` query parameter to the traverse endpoint. When `direction=reverse` is supplied, follow inbound links (entities that link TO the starting entity). When absent or `direction=forward`, keep the existing forward behavior.

Change required: In crates/axon-server/src/gateway.rs, read `params.get("direction")` and deserialize to TraverseDirection using serde_json or manual match. Pass the parsed direction to TraverseRequest.

This unblocks the nexiq integration which uses findLinkedReverse("users", userId, "assigned-to") for role-scoped access control and dashboard filtering.
title: feat(gateway): expose direction query param in HTTP traverse endpoint

acceptance: docs/helix/02-design/api-contracts.md adds an 'Audit Writes' section (alongside the existing 'Audit Reads' coverage at L269-L302) pinning: (a) the shape of a standalone audit entry a consumer can emit outside of a mutation (e.g. 'user viewed X', 'access_denied at route Y', 'login attempted from origin Z'); (b) whether audit entries are a separate endpoint/GraphQL mutation OR always piggyback on a transaction; (c) the event schema (event_kind, actor, subject_ref, payload_json, origin) and whether consumers can define new event_kind values or must use a curated list; (d) idempotency/replay semantics (do duplicate audit writes collapse?); (e) retention / queryability guarantees relative to entity audit rows; (f) the response shape when Axon rejects an audit write (rate limit, unknown event_kind, missing actor). A reference invocation example from a browser client and an integration worker is included. Downstream (nexiq) can then write access_denied events, user-facing login-attempt events, and business-level 'invoice approved' markers that are not coupled to a single entity mutation.
description:
<context>
Filed on behalf of nexiq (/home/erik/Projects/nexiq). nexiq's FEAT-010 plan references writing audit events in multiple places:
  - access_denied events when a user hits a forbidden route (plan line 554, auth-design.md)
  - invoice approved / engagement status transition audit entries (plan line 104, line 486, line 497)
  - first-run user bootstrap events
  - dashboard views / PII exposures that policy wants recorded

Axon's current api-contracts.md (2026-04-19 state) covers audit READS at L269-L302 but does not document a consumer-facing audit WRITE path. Entity mutations already leave audit footprints in Axon's internal audit log (per ADR), but application-level events that are NOT tied to a specific entity mutation (e.g. 'user attempted to access /invoices but was denied by RBAC — no entity touched') need a first-class write contract.

Codex fresh-eyes review of nexiq's plan 2026-04-19 flagged this as a handwaved assumption that blocks Phase 2 implementation.
</context>

<what-nexiq-needs>

1. **Event kinds used by nexiq** — in priority order:
   -  (route, attempted_action, caller_identity, reason)
   -  (already event-tied; could piggyback on the mutation)
   -  /  (piggyback on mutation)
   -  (no entity mutation; separate write)
   -  (no mutation; write before resolving identity)
   -  (user viewed rate_card, contractor rates, etc.)
   -  (user exported a report — compliance event)

   Most are piggyback-candidates; a few (access_denied, login_attempted, first_run) have no natural entity mutation to ride on.

2. **Shape options**:
   - A) Extend  to accept an optional  field alongside , so an app-level event can travel with an entity mutation OR alone (empty ops array + one event).
   - B) Separate endpoint  with idempotency semantics.
   - C) GraphQL mutation  once the GraphQL mutation layer ships.

   Downstream preference: (A) — matches the transaction-is-the-unit-of-change mental model, keeps idempotency uniform, and lets a route-denied event be written atomically with a downgrade operation if both are wanted.

3. **Schema**: same rationale as entity audit rows (actor from auth context, at from server time, ref is polymorphic entity reference or null, metadata is JSON). Nexiq doesn't need Axon to define application event_kinds ahead of time — a curated list is brittle; a free-form string is fine if Axon records what the consumer asserts.

4. **Query parity**: these events need to be readable via the same audit-read API so a nexiq dashboard can show 'all access_denied events on engagement X this week' regardless of whether the event came from an entity mutation or a standalone emit.
</what-nexiq-needs>

<interaction-with-axon-c5cc071a>

The RBAC feature ask (axon-c5cc071a) includes an access_denied denial shape. If Axon's RBAC layer itself emits audit entries on denial, that might cover the 'access_denied' event kind automatically — in which case this bead narrows to the other event kinds above. Confirm whether RBAC denials go to audit automatically.
</interaction-with-axon-c5cc071a>

<priority-rationale>

P1, not P0. Nexiq can ship Phase 2 without this by piggybacking on entity mutations for most cases and suppressing access_denied audit in the interim. But the workaround is cumbersome (every route component carries its own audit-write path) and creates a gap in compliance stories; filing now so Axon can scope it into the work-breakdown alongside RBAC enforcement.
</priority-rationale>

description:
Implement the full JWT verification middleware in axon-server:
1. Parse Authorization: Bearer header
2. Verify signature (HS256 dev / RS256 prod via jsonwebtoken crate)
3. Check exp / nbf with 30s clock skew
4. Check jti against in-memory LRU cache → SQL fallback on miss
5. Compare aud against URL {tenant} segment
6. Resolve sub → User row; reject if suspended
7. Walk grants.databases[] for URL {database}; intersect ops with method-op
8. Install (user_id, tenant_id, grants) into axum request extension

Depends on: A2, A1. Blocks B2, C-phase, D-phase.

Test as you implement:
- Unit test per verification step (happy path + failure)
- Integration test hitting a dummy protected route with valid JWT → 200
- Revocation propagation: DELETE jti, wait 1s, request → 401 credential_revoked
- Clock skew: exp +5s accepted, exp -5s rejected
- INV-018 grant enforcement workload in axon-sim: random ops × random grants, assert intersection correctness under 1k seeds
title: auth-jwt-pipeline: sign + verify with revocation cache + request extension

description:
Tenant-scoped database CRUD on the control plane. Extends C1's authz pattern and wires to the tenant_databases table from A1. Legacy /control/tenants CRUD stays untouched.

## Scope

### 1. Routes (extend control_plane_routes.rs)

    .route("/tenants/:id/databases", get(list_tenant_databases))
    .route("/tenants/:id/databases", post(create_tenant_database))
    .route("/tenants/:id/databases/:name", delete(delete_tenant_database))

Handlers:

- list_tenant_databases — require_tenant_admin (from C1 control_plane_authz) OR require_deployment_admin; return JSON array from storage.list_tenant_databases(tenant_id).

- create_tenant_database — require_tenant_admin or deployment_admin. Body: { name: "orders" }. Validate name per the D1 identifier rule (1-63 ASCII, no leading digit, [a-zA-Z0-9_-]). Reject duplicates within tenant with 409 Conflict. Return 201 + the new row on success.

- delete_tenant_database — require_tenant_admin or deployment_admin. Delete the tenant_databases row for (tenant_id, name). Return 204 on success, 404 if not found. Does NOT cascade-delete actual stored data (that's a later bead); just removes the registration.

### 2. Storage adapter trait methods (adapter.rs)

    fn list_tenant_databases(
        &self,
        tenant_id: TenantId,
    ) -> Result<Vec<TenantDatabase>, AxonError> {
        Ok(vec![])
    }

    fn create_tenant_database(
        &self,
        tenant_id: TenantId,
        name: &str,
    ) -> Result<TenantDatabase, AxonError> {
        Err(AxonError::Internal("create_tenant_database not supported by this adapter".into()))
    }

    fn delete_tenant_database(
        &self,
        tenant_id: TenantId,
        name: &str,
    ) -> Result<bool, AxonError> {
        Ok(false)
    }

Add a small `TenantDatabase` struct to axon-core (or axon-storage) with `{ tenant_id, name, created_at_ms }`. Check axon-core/src/auth.rs first — if there is a near-equivalent already, use it. Otherwise add to axon-core/src/auth.rs alongside TenantMember.

Implement on memory/sqlite/postgres. SQL uses the existing tenant_databases table (PK tenant_id, name). INSERT with ON CONFLICT (tenant_id, name) DO NOTHING and return-the-result pattern; DELETE returns affected row count; SELECT for list.

### 3. Integration tests: crates/axon-server/tests/control_databases_test.rs (NEW)

- admin_can_create_database — deployment admin creates a database in a tenant; 201 + row present in GET.
- non_admin_cannot_create_database — non-admin JWT, 403.
- create_duplicate_returns_409 — two create calls for same (tenant, name), second returns 409.
- delete_removes_row — create then delete; GET shows empty.
- delete_missing_returns_404 — delete a never-created database; 404.
- two_tenants_same_database_name — verify (tenant_a, "orders") and (tenant_b, "orders") coexist (different rows, different tenants).
- invalid_database_name_rejected — names violating the D1 identifier rule return 400.

### Context

- A1 schema already has tenant_databases (tenant_id, name, created_at_ms) with PK (tenant_id, name)
- C1 landed control_plane_authz require_tenant_admin/deployment_admin — reuse
- D1 landed the identifier validation rule — reuse/inline its regex or small helper
- B1's JWT middleware already runs on /control if the caller sends a token
- Legacy /control/tenants CRUD stays untouched

## Files to touch

- crates/axon-core/src/auth.rs — add TenantDatabase if not already present
- crates/axon-storage/src/adapter.rs — 3 new trait methods
- crates/axon-storage/src/memory.rs — impl
- crates/axon-storage/src/sqlite.rs — impl
- crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs — impl
- crates/axon-server/src/control_plane_routes.rs — add routes + handlers

## Files NOT to touch

- crates/axon-server/src/auth.rs — legacy Tailscale

- cargo test -p axon-server --test control_databases_test (must pass 7 tests)
- cargo test --workspace (all prior tests green — additive)
- cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings clean

AC1. docs/helix/02-design/decisions/preview-audit-threading.md exists and explicitly chooses pattern A or B with rationale.

description:
Codex review of axon-ab2e52e0 (4-time intractable) identified the structural blocker: preview records are created in lifecycle storage WITHOUT audit-log access in the function signature, while audit-append helpers exist for later lifecycle events. The agent has nowhere natural to thread the audit-write call. Without resolving this design question first, every implementation attempt stalls.

Investigation/design only — produce a decision document (no code commits required, but a small interface change is fine if obvious).

Locations to inspect (codex-supplied):
- crates/axon-api/src/intent.rs:870-895 (create_preview_record signature)
- crates/axon-api/src/intent.rs:1670-1740 (callers and surrounding context)
- crates/axon-graphql/src/dynamic.rs:4817-4910 (GraphQL preview endpoint)
- crates/axon-mcp/src/handlers.rs:674-724 (MCP preview endpoint)

Decide between two patterns and document in docs/helix/02-design/decisions/preview-audit-threading.md (new file):

Pattern A: create_preview_record takes &dyn AuditLog (or equivalent) and emits the operational event itself before returning. Pros: single point of truth, callers can't forget. Cons: storage layer takes an audit dependency.

Pattern B: callers (GraphQL, MCP) emit the operational event after calling create_preview_record, mirroring how later lifecycle events are handled. Pros: storage stays pure. Cons: callers must remember; risk of drift between surfaces.

Also resolve the queryability ambiguity in the parent bead's AC: 'queryable by intent ID' — does it mean auditLog(collection: '__mutation_intents', entityId: intentId) using existing filters, or a new dedicated filter? Codex flagged this ambiguity as a likely cause of prior-attempt stalls.

Output: docs/helix/02-design/decisions/preview-audit-threading.md with the chosen pattern, rationale, signature changes (if any), and the queryability mechanism.
title: build(feat-030): preview audit threading design (decomp of axon-ab2e52e0)

The key is validated for length, ASCII character set, tenant/database scope, TTL, replay hit indicator, and same-key/different-payload replay behavior. Transaction limits follow FEAT-008/PRD: >100 operations are rejected before execution; timeout defaults to 30s and aborts the transaction; empty operations commit as a no-op with no audit entry. Tests cover success, empty no-op, 50-operation bulk transaction across at least 3 collections, 101-operation rejection, version conflict rollback, schema validation rollback, link op rollback, per-operation error identification, idempotent replay, same key in different tenant/database, audit entries grouped by transaction id, and GraphQL error extensions. TS SDK uses the canonical body/input idempotency field. api-contracts.md documents GraphQL and REST compatibility examples.

Precondition for axon-8a15f833. Wiring a 'policy-filtered subscription handling' layer on top of nothing is impossible: the backend already exposes a GraphQL Subscription type via the change-feed broker (crates/axon-graphql/src/subscriptions.rs and dynamic.rs:7805+), but the UI client (ui/src/lib/api.ts) only ships a request/response graphqlRequest + controlGraphqlRequest pair. There is no graphql-ws / SSE / WebSocket transport, no reactive event-source, no per-collection reactive store.

This bead delivers the transport-only scaffolding so a follow-up bead can apply the policy-filtered behavior:

4. Add a unit test for the subscribe entry point using the fetch-mocking pattern already in api.test.ts.

This bead does NOT change any UI route — that is reserved for axon-8a15f833 (and any sibling beads that need live updates).

description:
Concurrency-safe auto-bootstrap for zero-tenant deployments. On first authenticated request, create a default tenant + default database + add the caller as admin. All under ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING so concurrent first-requests converge on a single row.

## Scope

### 1. New module: crates/axon-server/src/bootstrap.rs

    //! Default-tenant auto-bootstrap per ADR-018 §6.
    //!
    //! On a fresh deployment with zero tenants, the first authenticated
    //! request creates:
    //!   1. A "default" tenant row in the auth_schema tenants table
    //!   2. A "default" database in tenant_databases
    //!   3. A tenant_users row adding the caller as admin
    //!
    //! Idempotent — runs only when storage.count_tenants() returns 0, and
    //! concurrency-safe via ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING.

    pub async fn ensure_default_tenant(
        storage: &dyn StorageAdapter,
        caller_user_id: UserId,
    ) -> Result<DefaultTenantHandle, AxonError>;

    pub struct DefaultTenantHandle {
        pub tenant_id: TenantId,
        pub database_name: String,
    }

The caller identity comes from B1 (ResolvedIdentity) for JWT flow or B3 (resolve_tailscale_identity) for Tailscale. In either case, by the time we reach bootstrap, the user row exists.

### 2. Storage adapter: add a fast count + upsert path

    fn count_tenants(&self) -> Result<usize, AxonError> {
        Ok(0)
    }

    fn upsert_default_tenant(
        &self,
        name: &str,  // always "default"
    ) -> Result<TenantId, AxonError>;

upsert_default_tenant SQL:

    INSERT INTO tenants (id, name, display_name, created_at_ms, updated_at_ms)
    VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?, ?)
    ON CONFLICT (name) DO NOTHING;

    SELECT id FROM tenants WHERE name = ?;

The first call wins with its generated UUID; subsequent callers get the winning UUID back. Wrap in a transaction.

### 3. ensure_default_tenant logic

    1. count_tenants() — if > 0, we're NOT in bootstrap; return Err(AxonError::NotFound) so caller uses the explicit tenant URL instead.
    2. upsert_default_tenant("default") — get the tenant_id.
    3. create_tenant_database(tenant_id, "default") — ignore ON CONFLICT (idempotent).
    4. upsert_tenant_member(tenant_id, caller_user_id, TenantRole::Admin) — idempotent.
    5. Return DefaultTenantHandle { tenant_id, database_name: "default" }.

Each step uses an idempotent write, and all callers converge on the same (tenant_id, database_name) pair.

### 4. Tests: crates/axon-server/tests/bootstrap_test.rs (NEW)

#### bootstrap_first_call_creates_everything
Empty DB → ensure_default_tenant → verify tenants has 1 row, tenant_databases has 1 row, tenant_users has 1 row.

#### bootstrap_second_call_returns_same_handle
Call twice with different user IDs → first call creates; second call adds the second user as admin without creating a new tenant. Verify tenants still has 1 row, tenant_users now has 2 rows.

#### bootstrap_skips_when_tenants_present
Seed with an explicit tenant → ensure_default_tenant returns NotFound error (not the default tenant). The middleware caller will then use the explicit URL path.

#### concurrent_bootstrap_converges (INV-020 primitive)
Spawn N=64 threads calling ensure_default_tenant with 64 distinct user UUIDs on a fresh DB. After join:
- Exactly 1 tenant row with name="default"
- Exactly 1 tenant_databases row
- 64 tenant_users rows (one per caller), all pointing at the same tenant_id
- Every caller returned the same tenant_id in their DefaultTenantHandle

Run on memory AND sqlite backends. This is INV-020 at the primitive level; the full axon-sim workload is a separate bead.

#### bootstrap_uses_default_database_name
Assert DefaultTenantHandle::database_name == "default".

## Out of scope

- Router wiring: this bead is the primitive + tests. The HTTP middleware/router that DECIDES when to call ensure_default_tenant (based on path prefix or config) is D3's job after the path-router is finalized.
- --no-auth integration: E2 handles the no-auth path-based namespace. E1 is the auth-mode bootstrap.
- Deleting the "default" tenant: out of scope. The default tenant behaves like any other tenant for deletion purposes (uses the legacy path).

## Context

- A1 schema: tenants table has UNIQUE(name) — that's the conflict target.
- A2 types: TenantId, TenantRole, User, etc.
- B1: ResolvedIdentity has user_id
- B3: upsert_user_identity creates the user row before bootstrap runs
- B4: upsert_tenant_member is what we call for the admin grant
- C1: list_tenant_members exists (for tests)
- C2: create_tenant_database exists (call it here)

## Files to touch

- crates/axon-server/src/bootstrap.rs — NEW
- crates/axon-server/src/lib.rs — pub mod bootstrap; re-export
- crates/axon-storage/src/adapter.rs — count_tenants + upsert_default_tenant trait methods
- crates/axon-storage/src/memory.rs — impl
- crates/axon-storage/src/sqlite.rs — impl
- crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs — impl
- crates/axon-server/tests/bootstrap_test.rs — NEW

## Files NOT to touch

- crates/axon-server/src/serve.rs — router wiring is D3
- crates/axon-server/src/auth_pipeline.rs — B1 is canonical
- crates/axon-server/src/control_plane.rs — legacy
- crates/axon-server/src/control_plane_routes.rs — tenant CRUD handlers are C1/C2 territory

## Verification

- cargo test -p axon-server --test bootstrap_test  (5 tests must pass)
- cargo test -p axon-storage  (2 new trait methods)
- cargo test --workspace  (all prior tests green — additive)
- cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings clean
title: default-tenant-bootstrap: concurrency-safe zero-tenant auto-bootstrap

acceptance: GraphQL schema auto-generates <Collection>Aggregate(filter, groupBy, aggregations) or the policy-approved equivalent for every collection. Aggregations support COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX and single- and multi-field GROUP BY. Aggregation filters reuse the same FilterNode/typed filter semantics as list queries. Empty collections return totalCount 0 with empty groups. Null/missing values follow FEAT-018 semantics and are covered by tests. Invalid aggregations such as SUM on a non-numeric field return structured GraphQL errors with stable extensions. Aggregation can run in the same GraphQL request as regular entity queries. Server tests cover indexed and full-scan paths, filter plus groupBy, multiple aggregate functions, type mismatch errors, empty collections, and concurrent writes observing a consistent snapshot. api-contracts.md documents examples.

acceptance: All child beads are closed. docs/helix/02-design/api-contracts.md, ADR-012, ADR-018, and relevant FEAT docs agree that GraphQL is the primary documented interface for end-user and developer workflows, including single-entity CRUD and control-plane UI workflows, with REST exceptions only where GraphQL is demonstrably the wrong shape. A browser or SDK consumer can implement app load, tenant/user/member/credential/database administration, metadata discovery, CRUD, schema/collection admin, filtered/sorted/paginated lists, aggregations, audit reads, relationship traversal, link discovery/autocomplete, neighbor graph exploration, lifecycle operations, entity rollback, multi-entity bulk transactions with body/input idempotency, and subscriptions using GraphQL plus documented exceptions only. The native Axon UI is the first/canary GraphQL consumer across control-plane and data-plane workflows. The parity suite asserts persisted state, versions, audit rows, relationships, subscription payloads, tenant isolation, bulk operation semantics, credential secrecy, and GraphQL error extensions rather than HTTP 200 alone. cargo fmt/check/clippy/test and relevant SDK/UI tests pass.

description:
## Problem

When `--storage postgres` is passed to `axon serve`, startup correctly provisions the
`axon_master` PostgreSQL database via `spawn_blocking` (crates/axon-server/src/serve.rs:342,363).
However, the HTTP gateway middleware in `crates/axon-server/src/gateway.rs:347-357` explicitly
checks `router.is_postgres()` and, when true, **injects a fresh ephemeral in-memory SQLite
handler** as a placeholder for every request.  All data written via HTTP immediately goes into
an in-memory store and is lost on the next request.

```rust
// gateway.rs ~L347 — the placeholder path
if router.is_postgres() {
    let placeholder: TenantHandler = Arc::new(Mutex::new(AxonHandler::new(
        SqliteStorageAdapter::open_in_memory().expect("..."),
    )));
    request.extensions_mut().insert(placeholder);
    return next.run(request).await;   // ← data goes nowhere permanent
}
```

The `TenantRouter::get_or_create_pg` method (tenant_router.rs:228) and the `SharedPgHandler`
type already exist and correctly wrap a `PostgresStorageAdapter`, but they are never called
from the HTTP middleware.

## Root Cause

The gateway handler trait and extension system are typed around
`Arc<Mutex<AxonHandler<SqliteStorageAdapter>>>` (alias `TenantHandler`).  Wiring in Postgres
requires either:
1. Making `TenantHandler` an enum/trait-object that dispatches to either backend, or
2. Adding a parallel Postgres-aware handler path through axum extensions.

## Discovery Context

Discovered while attempting to run nexiq against a real PostgreSQL cluster
(postgresql://erik@localhost/nexiq).  The runtime symptom is a `tokio-rt-worker` panic
because an earlier code path (old binary 0.1.0) called `postgres::Client::connect` directly
from an async context without `spawn_blocking`.  After rebuilding to 0.1.2 the panic
moved to a `spawn_blocking` thread during startup, but the per-request placeholder injection
means all HTTP writes still go to a throwaway in-memory store.

## What Needs to Happen

1. Replace the placeholder injection in `gateway.rs` with a call to `router.get_or_create_pg`.
2. The `route_request` / handler dispatch path needs to accept `SharedPgHandler` alongside
   `SharedSqliteHandler` (enum dispatch or dynamic dispatch via a storage-agnostic trait).
3. **Full E2E coverage**: the nexiq playwright suite (packages/web e2e/) must pass with
   `AXON_STORAGE=postgres` before this bead can be closed.  SQLite-only CI is not sufficient
   to declare postgres production-ready.

## Acceptance Criteria

- `axon serve --storage postgres` stores and retrieves entities correctly via the HTTP API.
- All existing HTTP integration tests pass with a real Postgres backend.
- The nexiq playwright e2e suite passes end-to-end with Axon backed by Postgres.
- No in-memory SQLite placeholder remains in the postgres code path.
title: postgres: HTTP gateway injects placeholder SQLite handler — data routing unimplemented

Plan-agent review of axon-3fdfeb33 surfaced three list surfaces still rendering raw, unfiltered storage entity_count (not the policy-filtered GraphQL totalCount):

1. ui/src/routes/tenants/[tenant]/databases/[database]/collections/+page.svelte — collections.reduce((sum, c) => sum + c.entity_count, 0) aggregate and per-row {collection.entity_count} cell.
2. ui/src/routes/tenants/[tenant]/databases/[database]/schemas/+page.svelte — {collection.entity_count} entities in the collection picker.
3. ui/src/routes/tenants/[tenant]/+page.svelte — {db.entity_count ?? '—'} in the per-database row.

Each leaks the existence of hidden rows to a caller who cannot see them. The fix per surface: prefer a per-collection GraphQL totalCount that the backend filters by policy, OR drop the cell when the policy-filtered count isn't cheap to compute.

- [ ] metrics crate added to workspace and wired into crates/axon-server

description:
Wire the full observability envelope for auth rejections: every AuthError gets a structured log event with a canonical field set, plus a metrics counter increment. B1 already landed AuthError::status_code() and AuthError::error_code() in axon-core, and AuthErrorResponse + IntoResponse in crates/axon-server/src/auth_pipeline.rs. This bead extends that path — it does NOT duplicate it.

## Scope — exactly what to add

### 1. Add the metrics crate to the workspace

Add to root Cargo.toml under [workspace.dependencies]:
  metrics = "0.24"

Add to crates/axon-server/Cargo.toml under [dependencies]:
  metrics.workspace = true

The metrics crate provides a facade — the recording backend is configured at startup in main/serve. For this bead, just emit via metrics::counter!(); no global recorder wiring is required (the counter becomes a no-op if no recorder is installed, which is fine for tests).

### 2. Extend crates/axon-server/src/auth_pipeline.rs

Add a helper at the end of the module:

    /// Observability envelope emitted on every auth rejection.
    ///
    /// Fields:
    /// - error_code: stable SDK-facing code from AuthError::error_code()
    /// - tenant_path: the URL's {tenant} segment when known, else "(unknown)"
    /// - database_path: the URL's {database} segment when known, else "(unknown)"
    /// - op: required op for the HTTP method ("read"|"write"|"admin"), when known
    /// - user_id_if_known: user UUID if sub resolved, else unset
    /// - jti_if_known: credential jti if JWT parsed, else unset
    /// - remote_addr: client IP as a string, when available
    fn record_auth_rejection(
        err: &AuthError,
        ctx: &AuthRejectionContext,
    ) { ... }

    /// Context passed from the verification pipeline to the observability
    /// helper. All fields are optional because a rejection can happen before
    /// any of them are known.
    #[derive(Default)]
    struct AuthRejectionContext {
        tenant_path: Option<String>,
        database_path: Option<String>,
        op: Option<Op>,
        user_id_if_known: Option<UserId>,
        jti_if_known: Option<Uuid>,
        remote_addr: Option<String>,
    }

record_auth_rejection does three things:
1. Emit a tracing::warn!() event with target="axon.auth.reject" and all the fields as structured kv pairs. Use the error_code as the event name / message, e.g. tracing::warn!(target: "axon.auth.reject", error_code = %err.error_code(), ...).
2. Increment metrics::counter!("axon_auth_rejections_total", "error_code" => err.error_code()).
3. Return nothing; side-effect only.

Update verify_request to build an AuthRejectionContext incrementally (populate fields as they become available: tenant_path once the path is extracted, jti once the JWT is parsed, user_id once sub is resolved) and call record_auth_rejection on every early-return.

### 3. Add scenario test: crates/axon-server/tests/auth_rejection_matrix.rs

Table-driven scenario test that walks every row of ADR-018 §4 failure table (14 rows). For each row:
1. Set up the minimal server state (in-memory storage, a test JwtIssuer with a fixed key)
2. Build the minimal defective request per the row
3. Invoke the middleware via axum's testing infra (you can reuse the pattern from auth_pipeline_integration_test.rs)
4. Assert: response.status matches ADR-018 §4 table, response body parses to { code: <expected>, ... }

Unlike B1's unit tests which test the middleware in isolation, this scenario test hits the full middleware-plus-handler stack and exercises the observability envelope (the counter and log event must both fire). You do not need a metrics recorder installed — counter!() with no recorder is a no-op, but the call itself must happen (verify via a simple atomic counter wrapper around metrics::counter! if testing the fire-rate is required; otherwise rely on tracing subscriber capture).

For the tracing side, install a tracing_subscriber::registry() with a capture layer (the tracing-subscriber test helper crate or a simple Vec<Event> capture) and assert that every rejection produces exactly one axon.auth.reject event with the expected fields.

### ADR-018 §4 failure table (NORMATIVE — copy into test cases)

| Failure | Status | error_code |
|---|---|---|
| No Authorization header | 401 | unauthenticated |
| Header not Bearer | 401 | credential_malformed |
| JWT structurally invalid / missing claims | 401 | credential_malformed |
| aud as JSON array | 401 | credential_malformed |
| Signature invalid | 401 | credential_invalid |
| exp in past (beyond skew) | 401 | credential_expired |
| nbf in future (beyond skew) | 401 | credential_not_yet_valid |
| jti revoked | 401 | credential_revoked |
| iss unknown | 401 | credential_foreign_issuer |
| aud != URL tenant | 403 | credential_wrong_tenant |
| sub suspended/deleted | 401 | user_suspended |
| sub not a tenant member | 403 | not_a_tenant_member |
| URL database not in grants | 403 | database_not_granted |
| Op not in grant | 403 | op_not_granted |

## Out of scope (file follow-up beads, do NOT include here)

- INV-019 determinism workload in axon-sim. That's its own bead — axon-sim integration is bigger than the observability envelope wiring.
- Multi-backend (SQLite + Postgres + memory) determinism. For this bead, memory-backed tests are sufficient; backend conformance tests land in a separate pass.
- Metrics recorder wiring in serve.rs. That's a deployment-time concern and should be its own bead when the ops story needs it.

## Files to touch

- Cargo.toml (workspace root) — add metrics to [workspace.dependencies]
- crates/axon-server/Cargo.toml — add metrics.workspace = true
- crates/axon-server/src/auth_pipeline.rs — add record_auth_rejection + AuthRejectionContext; wire into verify_request
- crates/axon-server/tests/auth_rejection_matrix.rs — new scenario test file
- Possibly crates/axon-server/Cargo.toml [dev-dependencies] — tracing-subscriber with 'env-filter' or 'test-util' features if the project doesn't already have a subscriber for tests

## Files NOT to touch

- crates/axon-core/src/auth.rs — AuthError is done; status_code/error_code are done
- crates/axon-server/src/auth.rs — that's the Tailscale path, leave it
- crates/axon-storage/* — no storage changes for this bead

## Verification

Run before committing:
- cargo test -p axon-server --test auth_rejection_matrix (must pass for all 14 rows)
- cargo test -p axon-server --test auth_pipeline_test (must still pass — this bead is additive to B1)
- cargo test -p axon-server --test auth_pipeline_integration_test (must still pass)
- cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings clean

description:
--no-auth mode per-URL in-memory namespace. When the server runs with --no-auth, every request synthesizes an anonymous admin claim, and the storage materializes a per-URL (tenant, database) namespace on first touch. No persistent bootstrap, no user rows, no tenant rows written to disk.

## Scope

### 1. No-auth synthetic claim helper

Add to crates/axon-server/src/auth_pipeline.rs (or a new module crates/axon-server/src/no_auth.rs):

    /// Synthesize a ResolvedIdentity for --no-auth mode.
    ///
    /// The claim carries the anonymous user UUID (a deterministic zero UUID),
    /// the tenant and database names extracted from the URL path, and an
    /// admin grant on the database.
    pub fn synthesize_no_auth_identity(
        tenant: &str,
        database: &str,
    ) -> ResolvedIdentity {
        ResolvedIdentity {
            user_id: UserId::nil(),  // deterministic anonymous UUID
            tenant_id: TenantId::from_name(tenant),  // hashable mapping
            grants: Grants {
                databases: vec![GrantedDatabase {
                    name: database.to_string(),
                    ops: vec![Op::Read, Op::Write, Op::Admin],
                }],
            },
        }
    }

TenantId::from_name is a deterministic hash-to-UUID (use uuid::Uuid::new_v5 with a fixed namespace). This keeps the ID stable across calls for the same name without needing a DB lookup.

### 2. Middleware/layer glue

Add a new axum middleware in crates/axon-server/src/no_auth.rs (NEW):

    pub async fn no_auth_layer(
        resolved_path: Option<Extension<ResolvedPath>>,
        mut req: Request,
        next: Next,
    ) -> Response {
        if let Some(Extension(path)) = resolved_path {
            let identity = synthesize_no_auth_identity(&path.tenant, &path.database);
            req.extensions_mut().insert(identity);
        }
        next.run(req).await
    }

Uses D1's ResolvedPath extension as the upstream input. This means the path_router_layer from D1 must run BEFORE no_auth_layer — add a doc comment saying so.

### 3. In-memory namespace behavior

The storage adapter layer doesn't need to change — the existing memory adapter already materializes (tenant, database) key spaces on demand. What this bead adds is the PROOF that --no-auth reaches the right adapter + namespace.

Integration test proves:
- Writing to /tenants/a/databases/x/... and reading back succeeds (same namespace)
- Writing to /tenants/a/databases/x/... and reading /tenants/b/databases/y/... returns 404 (different namespace)
- After server restart (on an in-memory adapter), everything is gone

### 4. Tests: crates/axon-server/tests/no_auth_test.rs (NEW)

#### synthesize_returns_admin_grant
Unit: call synthesize_no_auth_identity("acme", "orders") and assert grants = [{ name: "orders", ops: [read, write, admin] }].

#### tenant_id_is_deterministic
Unit: two calls with the same (tenant, database) return the same tenant_id.

#### different_names_different_ids
Unit: ("a", "x") and ("b", "x") produce different tenant_ids.

#### middleware_installs_identity
Integration: axum router with path_router_layer + no_auth_layer. Hit /tenants/acme/databases/orders/ping and assert the handler sees ResolvedIdentity with admin grants on "orders".

#### middleware_skips_when_path_not_data_plane
Hit /health — no ResolvedPath extracted, no ResolvedIdentity installed, handler sees no identity.

## Out of scope

- Wiring no_auth_layer into the real router at serve.rs — that's D3's job.
- The storage adapter per-URL namespace materialization — already works for the memory adapter.
- --no-auth + Tailscale fallback — B3 federation handles the Tailscale path; --no-auth is the "no auth at all" path.

## Context

- D1 landed path_router_layer + ResolvedPath
- B1 landed ResolvedIdentity (in axon-core)
- A2: Grants, GrantedDatabase, Op, TenantId, UserId

## Files to touch

- crates/axon-server/src/no_auth.rs — NEW (both synthesize fn + layer)
- crates/axon-server/src/lib.rs — pub mod no_auth; re-export
- crates/axon-core/src/auth.rs — add TenantId::from_name helper (uses uuid::Uuid::new_v5 with a fixed namespace)
- crates/axon-server/tests/no_auth_test.rs — NEW

## Files NOT to touch

- crates/axon-server/src/serve.rs — router wiring is D3
- crates/axon-server/src/auth.rs — legacy Tailscale
- crates/axon-server/src/auth_pipeline.rs — B1 is canonical, no_auth is a peer module

## Verification

- cargo test -p axon-server --test no_auth_test (5 tests pass)
- cargo test -p axon-core (TenantId::from_name unit test)
- cargo test --workspace (all prior green)
- cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings clean

- [ ] Credentials issue modal uses UserPicker for target_user

description:
Wave 2 bead. Depends on wave2-users-provision-backend (axon-0a6eb28a) — do not start until that bead is merged.

## Problem

The existing /users page lists deployment-wide role assignments (login → role). It does not list or create underlying user rows from the ADR-018 \`users\` table. The Members and Credentials pages take a raw UUID text input, which fails with FOREIGN KEY / not_a_tenant_member errors when the UUID doesn't correspond to a real user.

After the backend bead lands, we have:
- POST /control/users → create user row
- GET /control/users/list → list user rows (separate from GET /control/users which returns role assignments)
- DELETE /control/users/{id} → suspend

Now wire the UI.

## UI changes

### 1. Rebuild the /users page

ui/src/routes/users/+page.svelte currently shows an ACL list (login + role). Replace with a two-section layout:

- **Section A: Users** (new) — table of real user rows from GET /control/users/list with columns: id (truncated), display_name, email, created_at, status (active/suspended), actions (Suspend). Add a "Create User" form above the table: display_name (required), email (optional), Submit → POST /control/users. On success, reload.

- **Section B: Role Assignments** (existing, retained) — keep the current login/role listing intact but move it below the Users section under its own heading (\"Deployment ACLs\"). Rename the page heading to \"Users & Access\".

### 2. User picker component

New component ui/src/lib/UserPicker.svelte:
- Props: \`value: string | null\` (user_id), \`onselect: (id: string) => void\`
- Fetches GET /control/users/list on mount, caches
- Renders as a searchable combo box (filter by display_name or email substring)
- Falls back to a plain text input (for UUIDs) when no users match — with a clearly-labeled \"paste UUID\" mode so it never becomes a dead-end
- Shows each option as \"display_name — id-prefix\" for disambiguation

### 3. Wire the picker into Members page

ui/src/routes/tenants/[tenant]/members/+page.svelte — replace the raw \"User ID (UUID)\" text input with \`<UserPicker bind:value={newUserId} />\`. Keep the role select unchanged.

### 4. Wire the picker into Credentials modal

ui/src/routes/tenants/[tenant]/credentials/+page.svelte — replace \"Target User ID\" input in the issue modal with \`<UserPicker />\`.

### 5. api.ts

Add:
- \`createUser(display_name: string, email: string | null): Promise<User>\`
- \`listUserRows(): Promise<User[]>\` (distinct from existing fetchUsers which returns ACL entries)
- \`suspendUser(id: string): Promise<void>\`
- \`User\` type: { id, display_name, email, created_at_ms, suspended_at_ms? }

## Tests

Un-skip the two wave-1 skipped tests in ui/tests/e2e/tenant-admin.spec.ts:
- \"Issue Credential modal mints a JWT and shows it once\" — create a user via API in beforeAll, then add them as tenant member, then run the credentials test against that user_id (use the new picker to select them in the modal)
- \"Tenant members — add, change role, remove\" — create a user via API in beforeAll, select them via the picker

Also add new specs to wave1-capabilities.spec.ts OR a new users.spec.ts:
- **Create user flow**: POST via UI form, verify row appears in table
- **Suspend user**: click Suspend, verify status pill changes
- **User picker search**: open modal, type substring, assert filtered options

The beforeAll in tenant-admin.spec.ts needs a helper like \`createTestUser(request, display_name)\` in tests/e2e/helpers.ts.

## Files to touch

- ui/src/lib/api.ts — new endpoints + User type
- ui/src/lib/UserPicker.svelte — NEW
- ui/src/routes/users/+page.svelte — rebuild
- ui/src/routes/tenants/[tenant]/members/+page.svelte — swap input for picker
- ui/src/routes/tenants/[tenant]/credentials/+page.svelte — swap modal input for picker
- ui/tests/e2e/helpers.ts — add createTestUser
- ui/tests/e2e/tenant-admin.spec.ts — unskip the 2 tests + wire to createTestUser
- ui/tests/e2e/users.spec.ts — NEW (or extend wave1-capabilities.spec.ts)

## Verification

- bun run typecheck in ui/ (0 errors; warnings allowed only in pre-existing files)
- bun run build in ui/
- cargo build --release --bin axon -p axon-cli (since the UI is embedded)
- install target/release/axon → ~/.local/bin/axon
- systemctl --user restart axon.service
- bunx playwright test --config playwright.smoke.config.ts — 0 skipped from tenant-admin, all new specs green, 0 failures

description:
Rewrite crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs to use sqlx::PgPool instead of tokio_postgres::Client + dedicated Runtime + block_in_place hack.

## Architecture
- Replace struct fields: `client: tokio_postgres::Client` + `handle` + `_rt` becomes `pool: sqlx::PgPool` + `rt: tokio::runtime::Runtime`
- Replace the `block()` helper with the same `block_on` pattern as the SQLite adapter (Bead 2)
- The dedicated multi-thread Runtime and block_in_place hack both disappear
- SQL stays identical — both tokio-postgres and sqlx use `$1, $2` positional params for PostgreSQL

## Key changes per method
- `self.block(self.client.query(sql, &[...]))` → `self.block_on(sqlx::query(sql).bind(...).fetch_all(&self.pool))`
- `self.block(self.client.execute(sql, &[...]))` → `self.block_on(sqlx::query(sql).bind(...).execute(&self.pool))`
- Row access: `row.get::<_, String>(0)` → `row.get::<String, _>("column_name")`
- Connection: `tokio_postgres::connect(params, NoTls)` → `PgPool::connect(url)`

## Also update
- crates/axon-storage/src/auth_schema.rs: rewrite `apply_auth_migrations_postgres` to use sqlx
- `provision_postgres_database` / `deprovision_postgres_database` standalone functions
- Test infra: testcontainers setup creates sqlx::PgPool instead of tokio_postgres::connect

## Do NOT change
- The StorageAdapter trait in adapter.rs
- The MemoryStorageAdapter or SqliteStorageAdapter
- Any downstream crates

## Acceptance Criteria
- cargo check --workspace passes
- `use tokio_postgres` no longer appears in postgres.rs
- block_in_place no longer appears in postgres.rs
- No dedicated tokio::runtime::Runtime for connection management
- StorageAdapter trait is NOT modified
title: sqlx-step-3: rewrite PostgreSQL adapter from tokio-postgres to sqlx::PgPool

description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-server/src/main.rs:54
Category: security
Severity: high
Description: The new auth implementation still configures  with , so a plain  boot enters the development bypass instead of enabling Tailscale identity resolution. Confirmed by running 2026-04-09T13:13:42.850330Z  INFO axon_server: running in --no-auth mode: all requests succeed as admin (actor=anonymous)
2026-04-09T13:13:42.851242Z  INFO axon_server: HTTP gateway listening on 0.0.0.0:0
2026-04-09T13:13:42.851278Z  INFO axon_server: gRPC service listening on 0.0.0.0:0, which logs  before serving both transports. ADR-005 and FEAT-012 define  as an explicit development escape hatch; leaving it on by default means the shipped server remains unauthenticated unless operators know to force auth on, which defeats the purpose of this implementation bead and leaves production deployments exposed to anonymous admin access.
Suggested fix: Make authenticated mode the default by removing the implicit no-auth default, so auth initialization runs unless  or  is explicitly provided. Add a startup regression test or CLI-level assertion that default startup requires auth initialization while the explicit bypass still works.

acceptance: An upgraded Postgres database containing a legacy schema row stored under the raw qualified collection string is fully cleaned up by unregister_collection; a regression test seeds that legacy row and verifies no schema metadata remains after qualified unregister.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs:215
File: crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs:1180
File: crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs:2166
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: ensure_namespace_catalog_tables backfills only database_name and schema_name for existing schema rows by joining on s.collection = c.name, but it never rewrites legacy schema rows whose collection column still contains the raw qualified identifier. The reviewed change makes unregister_collection delete schema rows only under the normalized bare key (key.collection). On an upgraded database that still has pre-fix schema metadata keyed as prod.billing.invoices, calling unregister_collection(prod.billing.invoices) deletes the normalized collection row but leaves those legacy schema-history rows orphaned. The new regression test covers only normalized rows, so this upgrade path remains unverified.
Suggested fix: Extend the upgrade or unregister cleanup path to remove legacy schema rows keyed by the raw qualified collection string in addition to the normalized bare key, and add a regression test that seeds a pre-fix schema row before running qualified unregister on an upgraded adapter.
title: correctness: upgraded Postgres schema metadata can survive qualified unregister

acceptance: A scoped validation fixture ignores <measure-results> snippets that appear outside actual top-level measurement blocks while still validating the real block; regression tests cover fake-before-real and fake-after-real notes; malformed or duplicate real measurement blocks still fail.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: scripts/check_tracker_measure_timestamps.py:15
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The new fix parses timestamps structurally only after MEASURE_RESULTS_RE has already scanned the entire notes string for <measure-results>...</measure-results> substrings. That means any quoted or narrative <measure-results> snippet that appears outside an actual measurement block is still treated as real evidence. Reproduction: run the validator against a one-record fixture whose notes are <report-summary>quoted prior <measure-results><timestamp>2026-04-09T18:53:00Z</timestamp></measure-results></report-summary><measure-results><timestamp>2026-04-09T18:59:11Z</timestamp></measure-results> with --id hx-fake-outside-real; the command exits 0 and iter_measure_timestamps() returns two valid timestamps even though only the second block is a real measurement record. This under-validates scoped review evidence because report-summary prose or other note text can synthesize fake measurement blocks.
Suggested fix: Replace regex block extraction with structural parsing that enumerates only actual top-level <measure-results> elements in notes, then keep the direct-child timestamp parsing within those blocks. Add regression tests for fake-before-real and fake-after-real summary snippets so only genuine measurement blocks count.
title: correctness: tracker validator counts quoted <measure-results> outside measurement blocks

acceptance: The commit that closes hx-7607eb91 changes .ddx/beads.jsonl, git rev-parse <closing-commit>^{tree} differs from its parent tree, git show <closing-commit>:.ddx/beads.jsonl | rg '"id":"hx-7607eb91"' succeeds, and ddx bead show hx-7607eb91 --json on the committed tracker data returns the latest closed record with machine-readable measure/report evidence.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: .ddx/beads.jsonl:687
Category: drift
Severity: high
Description: Commit ca3b3544f53ccff507211c271fdc1d8d24fd30c1 has the same tree as its parent bb729bfe06aeedad147eeff3f78b343ac591c5e0, so the supposed tracker-close step for hx-7607eb91 never became durable. Evidence: `git rev-parse HEAD^{tree}` and `git rev-parse HEAD~1^{tree}` both return 5ea47d2fb844deb436f995297568e4e53c1a08f0, `git diff HEAD~1 HEAD` is empty, and the committed `.ddx/beads.jsonl` blob still ends at line 687 with hx-02953298 and contains no hx-7607eb91 row. The commit message claims verification with `ddx bead show hx-7607eb91 --json`, but that command fails against the committed tracker data because the bead was never persisted. This breaks HELIX durability: the last commit reports a review-finding closure that later tracker queries cannot recover at all.
Suggested fix: Re-run the tracker close step with a non-empty commit that actually appends the hx-7607eb91 record to `.ddx/beads.jsonl` and records its measure/report closure evidence in committed state, so `ddx bead show hx-7607eb91 --json` succeeds on the committed tree.

acceptance: docs/helix/01-frame/features/FEAT-003-audit-log.md documents template.create, template.update, and template.delete as valid audit operation values, or explicitly marks the operation list as extensible and cross-references FEAT-026 for template lifecycle operations
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: docs/helix/01-frame/features/FEAT-003-audit-log.md:37
Category: drift
Severity: medium
Description: The implementation now emits template.create, template.update, and template.delete audit entries, and FEAT-026 depends on those public operation names for template lifecycle provenance. FEAT-003 still declares the audit operation field as one of only entity.create, entity.update, entity.delete, collection.create, collection.drop, and schema.update. That leaves the core audit specification out of sync with the shipped API surface and can mislead future API, CLI, or UI work that treats the enumerated list as exhaustive.
Suggested fix: Update FEAT-003 to include the template mutation operations in the operation taxonomy, or restate the field as an extensible operation namespace with FEAT-specific additions cross-referenced from FEAT-026.

acceptance: A regression test covers a shape-compatible helper contract that uses non-EntityRecord type names, and fetchEntity()/fetchEntities() accept the contract when the required fields and arguments are present; the targeted probe still avoids the full __schema.types scan.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: ui/src/lib/graphql.ts:90
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The narrowed helper-contract probe now assumes the backend exposes the helper object types under the exact names EntityRecord, EntityConnection, and EntityEdge. That assumption is not part of the helper query contract: GraphQL selection sets depend on field shape, and FEAT-015 / ADR-012 only standardize CollectionMeta, EntityConnection, EntityEdge, and PageInfo for the generic schema, not an EntityRecord type. The old implementation resolved the actual return type name from __schema.types, but the new switch in getNamedType() only returns metadata for the hard-coded aliases. Reproduced by stubbing /graphql so entity(collection, id) returns a shape-compatible TaskRecord and entities(...) returns TaskConnection; fetchEntity() now rejects with "Axon /graphql does not expose the entity helper contract yet" after a single probe call even though the queried fields are present.
Suggested fix: Keep the targeted probe, but resolve named types generically from the probed field metadata instead of keying on hard-coded aliases. For example, fetch the specific type names discovered on the root fields (or preserve a small name-to-metadata map) so helper support is validated by field shape rather than exact type names.
title: correctness: GraphQL helper probe hard-codes helper type names

description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: .ddx/beads.jsonl:182
Category: drift
Severity: medium
Description: The refreshed hx-1012e833 tracker record writes <measure-results><timestamp>2026-04-09T22:01:00Z</timestamp>, but the same bead record has updated_at=2026-04-09T17:31:57.867644231Z and the reviewing commit 9284f4f7c96697bbd0735de7b6c745feed6fee91 was committed at 2026-04-09T17:39:00Z. That means the measurement evidence is timestamped more than four hours after both the recorded bead update and the commit that introduced it. HELIX review notes are supposed to be durable, queryable audit evidence; a future-dated measurement block makes the record internally inconsistent and weakens traceability for when the review actually happened.
Suggested fix: Regenerate the hx-1012e833 measurement note with a timestamp derived from the actual write time in UTC and ensure tracker-written measure timestamps do not exceed the enclosing bead's updated_at/commit time for the same change. Add a tracker regression or write-path assertion if this timestamp skew can be produced automatically.

acceptance: With collections named tasks created in both default and prod, GET /db/prod/collections returns only the prod collection metadata, GET /collections still returns the unscoped result set, and gRPC ListCollections with x-axon-database=prod matches the prod-scoped HTTP behavior under automated regression tests.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-server/src/gateway.rs:880
File: crates/axon-server/src/gateway.rs:1331
File: crates/axon-server/src/service.rs:697
Category: integration
Severity: high
Description: The new current-database transport work nests data_routes() under /db/{database} and advertises gRPC metadata scoping, but the shared list_collections handlers still ignore the selected database entirely. GET /db/prod/collections calls the same global AxonHandler::list_collections(ListCollectionsRequest {}) path as GET /collections, and AxonServiceImpl::list_collections likewise ignores x-axon-database. I verified this live against the reviewed commit by creating tasks in both default and prod, then calling GET /db/prod/collections; the response returned both rows instead of only the prod collection. That violates FEAT-014's database-isolation acceptance (listing collections in database teamA returns zero results for teamB's collections) and makes the new scoped transport surface unusable for tenants.
Suggested fix: Thread the selected current database into collection-listing requests on both HTTP and gRPC, filter the results to that database before responding, and add regression coverage that seeds same-named collections in default and prod and asserts the scoped HTTP and gRPC listings only return the selected database's collections.
title: integration: current-database collection listings still return global results

acceptance: axon collection describe <name> shows entity count and schema. axon collection drop <name> drops with confirmation. axon schema show <collection> displays schema. All work with --output json.

description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: .ddx/beads.jsonl:411
Category: drift
Severity: medium
Description: The reviewed commit closes review-finding hx-f058cf32 by appending a closed tracker record, but it does not add any notes or close-summary evidence showing what actually satisfied the bead's acceptance criteria. ID:       hx-f058cf32
Title:    drift: rust-cargo dependency added outside workspace manifest
Type:     task
Status:   closed
Priority: 0
Labels:   helix, phase:build, review-finding, area:api
Owner:    erik
Desc:     Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-server/Cargo.toml:49
Category: drift (rust-cargo)
Severity: medium
Description: The reviewed commit adds reqwest directly under crates/axon-server dev-dependencies instead of threading the new third-party crate through [workspace.dependencies] and consuming it with reqwest.workspace = true. The active rust-cargo concern explicitly requires new dependencies to be added to the workspace manifest first so versioning, audits, and future upgrades stay centralized. This patch followed that rule for uuid but not for reqwest, so the dependency addition is inconsistent with the repository's declared Rust practices.
Suggested fix: Add reqwest to [workspace.dependencies] in the workspace Cargo.toml, switch crates/axon-server/Cargo.toml to reqwest.workspace = true while keeping the needed stream feature in the workspace declaration, and re-run the dependency hygiene gates.
Accept:   Workspace Cargo.toml declares reqwest under [workspace.dependencies], crates/axon-server/Cargo.toml references it via reqwest.workspace = true, and cargo deny check plus cargo machete crates both pass.
Created:  2026-04-09 17:17:20
Updated:  2026-04-09 17:20:18
Claimed:  2026-04-09T17:19:04Z
Machine:  sindri
spec-id: crates/axon-server/Cargo.toml now reports only the original description and ; it does not record that commit c655f51 centralized the reqwest dependency or that advisories ok, bans ok, licenses ok, sources ok and cargo-machete didn't find any unused dependencies in crates. Good job! passed. HELIX's measure/report workflow requires durable, queryable verification evidence on the governing bead. Without that note, later review or audit cannot tell why this finding was considered resolved, even though the underlying code fix is present.

For hx-f058cf32 specifically, record that c655f51 moved reqwest into [workspace.dependencies] and that advisories ok, bans ok, licenses ok, sources ok plus cargo-machete didn't find any unused dependencies in crates. Good job! passed.

acceptance: buildMcpStdioProvenance commandText is shell-safe: when scope.tenant or scope.database contains a space, single quote, or shell metacharacter, the produced commandText, when run through a shell parser, yields exactly two argument values matching the original tenant/database; new unit test asserts safe quoting for at least one adversarial input ('multi word tenant', 'db; rm -rf /', 'a\$b'); existing tests still pass
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review (parent: hx-0f99be1b).
File: ui/src/lib/mcp-provenance.ts:100-102 (buildLaunchCommand) and ui/src/routes/tenants/[tenant]/databases/[database]/intents/[intent]/+page.svelte:600 (commandText render)
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: buildLaunchCommand returns
  ['axon-server', '--mcp-stdio', '--tenant', scope.tenant, '--database', scope.database]
which is then joined with single spaces into commandText and rendered in a <pre> for operator copy-paste. The tenant/database values come from URL params and are not shell-quoted. If a tenant or database name contains a space, semicolon, ampersand, backtick, or other shell metacharacter, the displayed command is no longer safe or correct to copy into a terminal:
- Spaces split a single argument into two.
- Shell metacharacters (;, &, \$(...)) cause unintended command execution.
There is no XSS exposure (Svelte HTML-escapes), but this is an operational hazard: the panel exists to help operators reproduce an MCP stdio session, and the reproduction recipe is unsafe under non-trivial input.

Suggested fix: either (a) shell-quote scope.tenant and scope.database (single-quote escaping) when building commandText, or (b) validate at the boundary that tenant/database names match a safe identifier regex (e.g., /^[A-Za-z0-9_-]+\$/) and throw if not. (a) is preferred because it composes correctly even if validation rules later relax.

description:

Category: drift
Severity: medium
Description: This commit adds tokio-stream directly in crates/axon-server/Cargo.toml instead of declaring it under [workspace.dependencies] and consuming it with workspace = true. acceptance: Force-dropping a namespace or database removes entity rows only for collections inside the dropped scope, same-named collections in surviving namespaces/databases retain their entities, and regression tests cover Memory, SQLite, and Postgres shared-name scenarios.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-storage/src/memory.rs:201
File: crates/axon-storage/src/sqlite.rs:790
File: crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs:580
Category: integration
Severity: high
Description: The new purge paths delete by bare collection name, but all three storage adapters still key entity storage by bare collection name as well. FEAT-014 explicitly allows the same collection name to exist in multiple namespaces and databases. As a result, force-dropping prod.billing.invoices can also delete surviving data in prod.engineering.invoices or default.default.invoices. The existing same-name tests only check catalog membership, so this regression is currently untested.
Suggested fix: Scope entity storage and purge operations by fully qualified collection identity (database/schema + collection, or collection numeric ID), and add regression tests proving force-drop preserves same-named collections in surviving namespaces/databases across Memory, SQLite, and Postgres.
title: integration: force-drop purges same-named collections outside the target namespace/database

acceptance: grep -c 'same transaction' ADR-003 returns 0 for lines describing V1 audit write behavior (references to durable backend same-transaction goal are acceptable); diagram shows begin_tx() before version check; risk mitigation references post-commit strategy and recovery invariant
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: docs/helix/02-design/adr/ADR-003-backing-store-architecture.md:67,169,252,267
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: Commit f3262b1 updated the audit write path section of ADR-003 to describe the post-commit audit strategy, but four other sections in the same document still claim audit writes happen in the same storage transaction as entity writes:
  (1) Line 67: Atomicity bullet still says audit is in the same transaction
  (2) Line 169: StorageAdapter key design points says entity and audit writes share a transaction handle
  (3) Line 252: Consequences says audit overhead is from append in same transaction
  (4) Line 267: Risk mitigation says audit and entity writes share a transaction
Additionally, the audit write path diagram (lines 72-89) shows StorageAdapter.begin_tx() after version check and entity writes, but code calls begin_tx() first (transaction.rs:149).
Suggested fix: Update all four locations to reference the post-commit strategy. Fix the diagram to show begin_tx() before version check. Update the risk mitigation to reference the recovery invariant instead of shared-transaction atomicity.
title: correctness: ADR-003 has residual same-transaction audit claims after post-commit update

acceptance: Calling unregister_collection with a qualified collection ID removes the Postgres collections, schemas, and collection_views rows stored under the resolved bare collection key for that namespace; a regression test covers qualified unregister after schema and collection-view writes.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs:1168
File: crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs:1180
File: crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs:1192
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: This commit normalizes Postgres schema and collection-view rows to the resolved bare collection key (key.collection), but unregister_collection still deletes collection_views, schemas, and collections using the caller's raw collection string. A qualified unregister such as unregister_collection(prod.billing.invoices) resolves the namespace correctly, then issues WHERE collection = 'prod.billing.invoices' even though the persisted rows now use invoices. The delete statements become no-ops, leaving the registry row and metadata behind.
Suggested fix: Use the resolved catalog key consistently in unregister_collection (key.collection for collection/name) and add a regression test that unregistering a uniquely resolvable qualified collection removes the collection row plus associated schema/view metadata.
title: correctness: unregister_collection still targets raw qualified metadata keys

title: review: last-commit 00cda91 MCP SSE session reuse

acceptance: Dropping a collection removes any stored collection view in every storage adapter. Recreating the same collection starts with no collection view and versioning from 1. A regression test covers drop+recreate behavior.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:1160
Category: integration
Severity: high
Description: drop_collection deletes entities, schema, and the collection registry entry, but never calls delete_collection_view. The new collection_views stores introduced in this commit therefore retain templates after a collection is dropped. If the same collection name is recreated later, the stale template and incremented view version silently reappear, which violates collection lifecycle expectations and leaves orphaned presentation state behind.
Suggested fix: Delete the collection view as part of the drop path and add an integration test that creates a view, drops the collection, recreates it, and verifies no prior view remains.
title: integration: dropping a collection leaves its collection view behind

Category: correctness
Severity: high
Description: The new public template PUT/GET/DELETE surfaces persist and delete collection views directly, but neither put_collection_template nor delete_collection_template appends an audit entry, and the audit mutation enum has no template-specific operation at all. FEAT-026 explicitly requires template changes to be audited with actor, timestamp, and before/after state. As shipped, operators can change or remove a collection's presentation layer without any durable audit trail, violating both the feature contract and Axon's audit-first architecture.
Suggested fix: Introduce audit mutation types for template create/update/delete (or an equivalent collection-view mutation), capture before/after template state, and append audit entries from the handler on successful template writes and deletes. Add handler and gateway tests that query the audit log and verify actor plus before/after metadata for template changes.","id":"hx-9ede52ae","issue_type":"task","labels":["helix","phase:build","review-finding"],"owner":"erik","priority":0,"spec-id":"crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs","status":"closed","title":"correctness: template CRUD writes are not audited","updated_at":"2026-04-09T09:34:49.439736591Z"}

acceptance: MCP resource and prompt handlers map AxonError::NotFound to McpError::NotFound, validation-style failures to McpError::InvalidParams, and storage/internal failures to McpError::Internal; regression tests cover at least one non-not-found failure path.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-mcp/src/resources.rs:402
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The new read-side adapters convert multiple AxonHandler failures directly into McpError::NotFound (describe_collection, get_entity, and list_neighbors in resources; the same pattern also appears in crates/axon-mcp/src/prompts.rs:244). Because AxonError also carries storage, validation, and invalid-operation failures, backend faults or bad input can now be misreported to MCP clients as missing resources. That breaks structured agent handling and hides real incidents behind false not-found responses.
Suggested fix: Add a shared AxonError-to-McpError mapper that preserves not-found vs validation vs internal failures, and use it in resources and prompts instead of hard-coding McpError::NotFound.
notes: <measure-results><timestamp>2026-04-09T15:54:40Z</timestamp><status>PASS</status><acceptance><criterion name="mcp-error-kinds-preserved" status="pass" evidence="crates/axon-mcp/src/error_mapping.rs now maps AxonError::NotFound to McpError::NotFound, validation-style failures to McpError::InvalidParams, and storage/serialization failures to McpError::Internal; resources.rs and prompts.rs use the shared mapper for read-side handler calls."/><criterion name="non-not-found-regression-covered" status="pass" evidence="cargo test -p axon-mcp passes, including error_mapping::tests::entity_resource_preserves_internal_failures and error_mapping::tests::dependency_prompt_preserves_internal_failures."/></acceptance><gates><gate concern="rust-cargo" command="cargo fmt --check" status="pass"/><gate concern="rust-cargo" command="cargo test -p axon-mcp" status="pass"/><gate concern="rust-cargo" command="cargo clippy -p axon-mcp --all-targets --no-deps -- -D warnings -D clippy::todo -D clippy::unimplemented -D clippy::dbg_macro -D clippy::print_stdout -D clippy::unwrap_used" status="pass"/></gates><ratchets/></measure-results>
title: correctness: preserve AxonError kinds in MCP resource and prompt reads

description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: .ddx/beads.jsonl:476
Category: drift
Severity: medium
Description: Commit 8216d46a705b7fcfbf1c393d37dc8026e08ecf8a is the first commit that appends a closed hx-9b720e48 row to .ddx/beads.jsonl, but that new row records both closing_commit_sha and the top-level <report-summary><commit> as 26a9bd59688765acd9921c7ccef126c4499a2074, which is the earlier execution that repaired hx-faf753fb rather than the execution that actually measured and closed hx-9b720e48 itself. Evidence: git show HEAD~1:.ddx/beads.jsonl and git show 26a9bd59688765acd9921c7ccef126c4499a2074:.ddx/beads.jsonl contain no hx-9b720e48 row, while git show HEAD:.ddx/beads.jsonl shows the first closed hx-9b720e48 row. As a result, tracker queries against the latest closed hx-9b720e48 record misattribute which commit actually measured and closed the governing bead, breaking the durable traceability required by HELIX report semantics.
Suggested fix: Append a new closed hx-9b720e48 row that preserves the existing PARTIAL measure-results block but updates both closing_commit_sha and the top-level <report-summary><commit> to the commit that actually appends the corrective closure row, with summary text that names that same close step.

Category: drift
Severity: medium
Description: The last commit adds markdown regression tests that use `.unwrap()` on handler, storage, and gateway setup calls in the touched files. The active `rust-cargo` concern requires the strict clippy gate `cargo clippy --all-targets --no-deps -- -D warnings -D clippy::todo -D clippy::unimplemented -D clippy::dbg_macro -D clippy::print_stdout -D clippy::unwrap_used`, and the new lines are included in that failure set. That means this review-fix commit improves coverage but still lands new code that violates the declared no-`unwrap` quality bar for Rust targets.
Suggested fix: Replace the new `.unwrap()` calls in the added markdown tests with `expect()` or `expect_err()` carrying specific failure messages, or restructure the assertions so the tests remain explicit without using unwrap-family calls. Re-run the strict clippy command and confirm the touched markdown tests no longer contribute `unwrap_used` violations.
title: drift: markdown regression tests introduce unwrap_used violations

acceptance: When default and prod each contain audit history, GET /db/prod/audit/query and GET /audit/query with X-Axon-Database=prod return only prod audit entries, while an unscoped audit query still returns both databases' entries in regression tests.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-server/src/gateway.rs:748
File: crates/axon-server/src/gateway.rs:1331
File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:1177
Category: security
Severity: high
Description: The reviewed change qualifies the optional collection query parameter for /audit/query, but it never scopes the audit query itself to the selected current database. Because the router now exposes /db/{database}/audit/query and header-based current-database selection, a tenant-scoped request still returns every audit entry when collection is omitted. I verified this live against the reviewed commit by creating tasks in both default and prod and then calling both GET /db/prod/audit/query and GET /audit/query with X-Axon-Database: prod; both responses included the default-database collection.create entry. FEAT-014 requires database isolation and explicitly states audit log entries for teamA are not visible from teamB, so the new transport surface currently leaks cross-database provenance.
Suggested fix: Add database-level filtering to the audit query path when a current database is selected, preserving fully qualified explicit collection filters while excluding entries outside the selected database, and add regression tests covering both the /db/{database} path prefix and the X-Axon-Database header form.
title: security: current-database audit queries leak entries from other databases

description:

File: .ddx/beads.jsonl:401
Category: drift
Severity: medium
Description: Commit f6bdfd824c07ce0db2c1c77bd82d77a28c74867e closes review-finding hx-6064816c by changing only its status from in_progress to closed. The bead already contains a <measure-results> block, but unlike other closed review beads in this tracker it does not append any durable <report-summary> or equivalent close summary for the closure itself. As a result, `ddx bead show hx-6064816c` exposes only the original finding text and `Status: closed`, with no queryable summary of what was done, the reused verification basis mentioned in the commit message, or the final measure/report disposition. HELIX's report workflow requires the governing bead closure to summarize what was done, the measurement outcome, follow-on creation, and relevant references. Without that closure summary, later tracker queries cannot distinguish an intentional, measured close from a bare status flip.
Suggested fix: Update hx-6064816c with a durable close/report summary before or alongside closing it. The summary should name hx-6064816c as the governing bead, record commit f6bdfd824c07ce0db2c1c77bd82d77a28c74867e, state that the prior measure evidence on hx-f058cf32 was reused and rechecked via `ddx bead show hx-6064816c` and `scripts/check_tracker_measure_timestamps.py .ddx/beads.jsonl --id hx-6064816c`, and preserve the closed status.

acceptance: A gateway test injects a render-failing template and asserts GET /collections/{collection}/entities/{id}?format=markdown returns 500 with both error detail and the entity JSON payload; cargo test -p axon-server passes
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:278-302
File: crates/axon-server/src/gateway.rs:286-307
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The new markdown GET path renders through `AxonHandler::get_entity_markdown`, which returns only a `String`. When rendering fails, the handler maps the error into `AxonError::Storage`, and the gateway converts that into a generic 500 JSON error with no entity payload. FEAT-026 explicitly requires that if `format=markdown` rendering fails, the response still includes the entity JSON so callers retain access to the underlying data. As shipped, any invalid persisted template or other render-time failure drops the entity body entirely.
Suggested fix: Change the markdown GET flow to preserve the fetched entity on render failure and serialize it in the 500 response body alongside the error detail. Add an integration test that injects a bad stored template and asserts the 500 body includes both the error and the entity payload.
title: correctness: markdown GET 500 responses omit entity payload on render failure

acceptance: Dry-run rollback of a deleted entity returns a non-error preview without writing state, and handler plus HTTP regression tests cover the deleted-entity dry-run flow
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:1426
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The new deleted-entity rollback path restores the entity on an applied rollback, but it still hard-fails dry_run requests with InvalidOperation("dry-run rollback preview is unavailable for deleted entities"). FEAT-023 says all rollback operations support a dry_run flag, and the governing rollback bead records the HTTP contract as dry_run=true returns a non-writing preview. This commit codifies the deleted-entity case as an exception instead of extending the preview surface to cover the recovery flow it just added, so agents cannot safely inspect a restore-after-delete before applying it.
Suggested fix: Extend the rollback dry-run response to represent deleted current state and return a preview for restore-after-delete instead of an error, then add handler and HTTP regression tests for deleted-entity dry_run.
title: correctness: deleted-entity rollback still rejects dry-run preview
