acceptance: The markdown compiled-template cache has an explicit capacity bound; rendering more unique collections than that bound does not increase the stored entry count past the limit; evicted collections are recompiled on demand and still render correctly; regression tests cover the bounded-cache behavior.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:53
File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:115
Category: performance
Severity: medium
Description: FEAT-026's non-functional requirements explicitly say the template cache must be bounded, but the new MarkdownTemplateCache is a plain HashMap with no size limit or eviction policy. Every first render of a new collection inserts another compiled template that lives until process restart, so a long-lived server can grow memory monotonically with the number of collections it has ever rendered. Removed collections also leave stale entries behind unless a specific invalidation path happens to clear them.
Suggested fix: Replace the raw HashMap with a bounded cache (for example an LRU keyed by CollectionId with an explicit capacity), enforce the limit on insert, and add regression coverage that proves rendering more than the configured capacity does not let the cache grow without bound while still returning correct markdown.
title: performance: markdown template cache is unbounded

description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: .ddx/beads.jsonl:398
Category: drift
Severity: medium
Description: The new hx-1012e833 tracker record restores durable notes on hx-f058cf32, but its own acceptance field is populated with a pasted ddx bead show transcript instead of a deterministic acceptance criterion. That makes the review-finding bead harder to measure programmatically, bloats the tracker state with CLI formatting noise, and breaks the workflow rule that bead acceptance should state concise, verifiable completion criteria.
Suggested fix: Rewrite hx-1012e833 so its acceptance field contains a deterministic criterion about durable verification evidence being present on hx-f058cf32 while the bead remains closed, and keep the supporting evidence in notes rather than embedding CLI output in acceptance text.

acceptance: Attempting to drop the implicit default database either returns a deterministic error or leaves the instance with a recreated `default.default` namespace; unqualified collection creation still works after the operation; `/health` reports the real default-namespace status from storage; an HTTP integration test covers the chosen behavior
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:1157-1158
File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:1519-1550
File: crates/axon-server/src/gateway.rs:1042-1054
Category: integration
Severity: medium
Description: The new database drop path allows `default` to be removed, but the rest of the system still hardcodes unqualified collection work to `Namespace::default_ns()` and the HTTP health endpoint always reports `"default_namespace": "default.default"`. After `DELETE /databases/default?force=true`, zero-config collection creation and any other default-scoped paths fail because the backing catalog no longer contains `default.default`, while `/health` continues to claim that the default namespace exists and is healthy. That violates FEAT-014's zero-config invariant and makes the new health signal misleading for operators or future control-plane automation.
Suggested fix: Either forbid dropping the implicit `default` database/schema or recreate them immediately after a forced drop, and derive the health payload from actual storage state instead of hardcoding `default.default`. Add an integration test that exercises the default-database drop path and verifies both collection behavior and `/health` reporting.

acceptance: /mcp and /mcp/sse share durable MCP session state; a successful resources/subscribe survives subsequent HTTP requests; a subscribed client connected to /mcp/sse receives a notification after a matching resource mutation.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-server/src/mcp_http.rs:160
Category: integration
Severity: high
Description: The new HTTP transport constructs a fresh McpServer inside every POST /mcp request and drops it immediately afterward, while GET /mcp/sse emits a single placeholder ready event and closes. Any resources/subscribe call acknowledged over HTTP is therefore lost as soon as the request returns, unsubscribe cannot target durable state, and remote agents never receive notifications over the advertised SSE transport.
Suggested fix: Introduce durable MCP session state for the HTTP transport, keep the SSE stream open per client/session, route POST requests through that session state, and emit real MCP notifications over SSE when subscribed resources change.

acceptance: After hx-ac472b2d is implemented, mcp-provenance.test.ts contains no test that calls buildMcpStdioProvenance and then asserts redaction of values that buildMcpStdioProvenance itself synthesized; redaction-layer coverage (isSecretEnvKey/isSecretEnvValue/redactEnvEntry direct tests) is preserved or expanded with at least one new adversarial input; bun test src/lib/mcp-provenance.test.ts continues to pass
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review (parent: hx-0f99be1b).
File: ui/src/lib/mcp-provenance.test.ts:180-197 ('redacts credential and bearer entries from the env preview') and ui/src/lib/mcp-provenance.ts:149-166 (buildEnvPreview)
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: Test: 'redacts credential and bearer entries from the env preview' calls buildMcpStdioProvenance and asserts that AXON_API_TOKEN/AXON_BEARER/AXON_CREDENTIAL_ID arrive redacted. However, those keys/values are hardcoded inside buildEnvPreview (the function under test). The test cannot fail unless someone changes BOTH buildEnvPreview and the test in opposite directions. Specifically, it does not exercise:
- the redaction layer (isSecretEnvKey, isSecretEnvValue, redactEnvEntry) against synthesized real-world inputs,
- the contract that 'no env value from the live process environment ever reaches the UI without redaction' — because nothing in production ever passes a real env to redactEnvEntry.

This is the same root-cause as the 'fake hardcoded credentials' finding (hx-ac472b2d): SUT and fixture are coupled. Resolving hx-ac472b2d (removing the fake entries) requires fixing this test simultaneously to keep coverage of the redaction layer.

Suggested fix: keep direct unit tests on isSecretEnvKey, isSecretEnvValue, and redactEnvEntry (already present and decoupled — these are the real coverage). Replace the buildMcpStdioProvenance redaction test with a test that asserts the env list contains only the deterministic operator-facing config (tenant, database, agent_id, credential_id) and no values that match secret patterns. That assertion is independent of the implementation's chosen entries, so it cannot be tautological.
notes:
<measure-results>
  <timestamp>2026-04-27T08:13:00Z</timestamp>
  <status>PASS</status>
  <acceptance>
    <criterion name='no-tautological-redaction-test' status='pass' evidence='env-preview test asserts a structural contract over provenance.env (no displayed value matches isSecretEnvValue; any secret-keyed entry must be redacted). The contract holds independently of which keys buildEnvPreview synthesizes — adding/removing keys cannot make it tautological.'/>
    <criterion name='direct-redaction-tests-expanded' status='pass' evidence='Added 5 new test cases to isSecretEnvKey/isSecretEnvValue/redactEnvEntry: adversarial key shapes (OAUTH2_BEARER_TOKEN, x-api-key, myAPIKey, SERVICE_AUTHORIZATION_URL, ROTATING_CREDENTIAL_REF, SMTP_PASSWORD_FILE), false-positive guards (AXON_KEYBOARD, BEAVER_DAM, AXON_REGION), tab-separated bearer / mixed-case / dotenv-leak values, value-only redaction (PROXY_USER + Bearer token).'/>
    <criterion name='bun-test-passes' status='pass' evidence='bun test src/lib/mcp-provenance.test.ts: 17 pass / 0 fail / 80 expects'/>
  </acceptance>
  <gates>
    <gate concern='typescript-bun' command='bun test src/lib/mcp-provenance.test.ts' status='pass'/>
    <gate concern='typescript-bun' command='bun run lint' status='pass'/>
    <gate concern='typescript-bun' command='bun run typecheck' status='pass'/>
  </gates>
</measure-results>
title: test-quality: env-redaction tests are tautological — production code provides its own test fixtures

acceptance: Rolling back an entity after it has been deleted recreates the entity from the requested audit snapshot as an entity.revert mutation instead of returning 404, and regression tests cover the deleted-entity flow in both axon-api and axon-server.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:1316
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: rollback_entity immediately loads the current entity and returns NotFound when it has already been deleted. FEAT-023 defines entity-level rollback as restoring an entity to a prior version from the audit log, and recovery from accidental deletes is one of the core rollback use cases. With the current guard, the new endpoint can roll back updates on live entities but cannot recover a deleted entity even though the audit log still contains the target state.
Suggested fix: Resolve the rollback source entry first and support the deleted-entity case by recreating the entity from the target audit snapshot as a new entity.revert revision, preserving the latest known version semantics and adding handler + HTTP regression coverage for rollback-after-delete.
title: correctness: rollback endpoint cannot recover a deleted entity

acceptance: Design decision documented: either audit entries are only written after commit_tx succeeds, or the audit log documents that entries may correspond to rolled-back transactions.
description:
Learning from fresh-eyes review of commit 89314f0.

AuditLog::append is immediate and non-transactional. When Transaction::commit calls execute() and then either commit_tx() fails or execute() itself fails partway through, the storage-level transaction is rolled back via abort_tx(), but audit entries written during execute() persist.

This means the audit log can contain entries for mutations that were never committed. These entries have transaction_id set, but there is no status field to distinguish committed from rolled-back entries.

Evidence:
- AuditLog trait (crates/axon-audit/src/log.rs): append() is immediate, no rollback capability.
- Transaction::commit (crates/axon-api/src/transaction.rs:132-157): execute() writes audit entries before commit_tx is called.
- MemoryStorageAdapter::abort_tx restores snapshot, but audit log is separate.

Options to consider:
1. Buffer audit entries in Transaction and only append after commit_tx succeeds.
2. Add a status/outcome field to audit entries (committed vs rolled-back).
3. Accept as intentional and document the behavior.
title: design: audit log entries persist after transaction rollback

acceptance: ensureTargetPolicy retries on a subsequent toggle after a failed fetch (no permanent permissive cache entry). New unit/e2e coverage demonstrates: with the policy endpoint failing on first call and succeeding on second, the second expand of a row in the same target collection renders [redacted] markers.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review of c28a750.
File: ui/src/routes/tenants/[tenant]/databases/[database]/collections/[name]/+page.svelte:325-348 (ensureTargetPolicy)
Category: correctness (resilience)
Severity: medium
Description: ensureTargetPolicy swallows ALL fetchEffectivePolicy errors and writes a permissive sentinel into the cache (canRead: true, redactedFields: []). Subsequent toggles on rows in the same target collection short-circuit on `if (targetPolicyByScopedCollection[key] !== undefined) return;` and never retry. A transient network blip therefore disables [redacted] markers for the rest of the session for that scoped collection. The server still field-filters traverseLinks (the data values arrive as null at redacted paths), so this is NOT a value-leak — but it is a silent UX regression that masks an underlying bug: with empty redactedFields, redactValue (replace-nulls mode) leaves the server-supplied nulls in place and JsonTree renders them as literal "null" rather than [redacted].
Suggested fix: Distinguish "fetched: success" from "fetched: failed" in the cache (e.g., use `Record<string, EffectiveCollectionPolicy | { failed: true }>`, or store undefined and write only on success). On a failed fetch, allow the next expand of any row in that collection to retry. Optionally surface an inline non-blocking warning in the preview row when the policy is unavailable so reviewers can distinguish "server-filtered nulls" from "missing policy".
notes:
Closed with durable evidence on 2026-04-29.

Implementation: ensureTargetPolicy now stores { failed: true } sentinel on fetch error instead of a permissive policy with empty redactedFields. Subsequent toggles re-fetch on cache state matching '!canRead in cached'. targetRedactedFields returns [] for both the unfetched and failed states, so the server's already-applied redaction is respected without a permanent permissive cache poison.

Commits landed (cherry-picked onto master):
  17a4dfd  fix: ensureTargetPolicy retries on transient fetch failure
  9149964  test: fix link-policy-retry to properly mock GraphQL policy query
  0ed2c1f  test: fix mock targeting and skip broken duplicate

Files: ui/src/routes/tenants/[tenant]/databases/[database]/collections/[name]/+page.svelte, ui/tests/e2e/link-policy-retry.spec.ts (new), ui/tests/e2e/policy-enforcement.spec.ts (skipped duplicate variant + .skip rationale).

Test commands and results:
  cmd: bash scripts/test-ui-e2e-docker.sh -- tests/e2e/link-policy-retry.spec.ts
  result: 1 passed (2.6s) — chromium runner, on master at 0ed2c1f
  log: /tmp/hx-92456dd2-master-test.log

  cmd: bash scripts/test-ui-e2e-docker.sh -- tests/e2e/link-policy-retry.spec.ts tests/e2e/policy-enforcement.spec.ts
  result: 15 passed, 1 skipped (deliberate duplicate skip), 0 failed (21.8s)
  log: /tmp/hx-92456dd2-test6.log

Mock-targeting fix rationale: original test failed all AxonUiEffectivePolicy fetches up to count=1 without discriminating source vs target. When source==target collection, the page-load source fetch consumed the failure budget and the link-expand target fetch saw a successful response — the test never exercised the actual retry path. Replaced with a one-shot fail flag armed right before the toggle click, isolating the target-policy fetch as the failed call.
title: ui: ensureTargetPolicy permanently caches permissive fallback on fetch failure

description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:1535
File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:1554
File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:3385
File: crates/axon-server/src/gateway.rs:771
File: crates/axon-server/src/gateway.rs:794
File: crates/axon-cli/src/main.rs:446
Category: integration
Severity: medium
Description: The qualified-collection fix now lets PUT/GET template operations succeed for namespaced collections, but both handlers return the raw storage CollectionView without re-presenting it through the requested identifier. Storage normalizes view.collection back to the bare collection name, and the new test asserts that behavior. As a result, HTTP JSON and CLI output for prod.billing.tasks reports collection: tasks, which loses namespace identity and becomes ambiguous once the same collection name exists in multiple namespaces.
Suggested fix: Add a template-view presentation step analogous to present_entity/present_schema so PUT and GET return the caller-requested collection id, and cover qualified response payloads in handler plus one public surface test.

acceptance: Either: (1) a public API or CLI exists to create/read/delete collection views/templates and end-to-end tests cover creating a template then GET /collections/{collection}/entities/{id}?format=markdown; or (2) FEAT-026 explicitly states the markdown GET path is not yet a user-reachable shipped surface until template management APIs land
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: docs/helix/01-frame/features/FEAT-026-markdown-templates.md:132
File: docs/helix/01-frame/features/FEAT-026-markdown-templates.md:180
File: docs/helix/01-frame/features/FEAT-026-markdown-templates.md:303
File: crates/axon-server/src/gateway.rs:850
File: crates/axon-server/src/gateway.rs:970
File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:287
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The reviewed spec now says the "currently shipped V1 slice" exposes markdown entity rendering over HTTP, but the repository still exposes no public HTTP or gRPC route for creating, reading, or deleting the required collection template/view. The gateway only routes entity CRUD, schema, query, audit, and transaction endpoints, while the markdown tests seed the template by calling storage_mut().put_collection_view(...) directly. That means external clients cannot actually reach the documented markdown flow through the public API surface the spec describes.
Suggested fix: Either implement and test public template management endpoints before describing the markdown GET path as a shipped client-facing slice, or explicitly mark markdown rendering as internal/test-only until template management surfaces are added. If template management remains future work, keep those endpoints in the planned feature contract section rather than the shipped-slice description.
title: correctness: FEAT-026 shipped markdown slice lacks a public template API

acceptance: In .ddx/beads.jsonl, bead hx-be0bc797's durable closure record names commit 6e1bc7adbbbd6c6c8993bfe067d870471fd3a32a as the closing commit, preserves the historical c60135e13b9567b728cd4a9097a0daad4a46f5aa reference for hx-89740724, and ddx bead show hx-be0bc797 --json reflects the corrected closed record.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: .ddx/beads.jsonl:410
Category: drift
Severity: medium
Description: The last-commit patch closes review-finding hx-be0bc797 by appending a new tracker row with both <measure-results> and <report-summary>, but the new report summary does not record the actual closing commit 6e1bc7adbbbd6c6c8993bfe067d870471fd3a32a anywhere in the durable closure record. Instead, both the measure summary and report summary only cite the historical fix commit c60135e13b9567b728cd4a9097a0daad4a46f5aa for hx-89740724. HELIX report guidance expects the governing bead closure to preserve references to the commits produced by that execution step. Without the closing commit reference, later tracker queries can confirm that hx-be0bc797 is closed, but they cannot tell which commit actually performed and measured the closure, which weakens auditability of the governing bead itself.
Suggested fix: Update hx-be0bc797's durable closure record so its <report-summary> (or equivalent close-summary fields) includes commit 6e1bc7adbbbd6c6c8993bfe067d870471fd3a32a while preserving the existing reference to c60135e13b9567b728cd4a9097a0daad4a46f5aa as the historical fix for hx-89740724.

acceptance: Template PUT and DELETE append durable audit entries with actor, timestamp, and before/after template state; the audit log exposes distinct template mutation records; handler or gateway tests verify audited create/update/delete template flows.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:1505
File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:1564
File: crates/axon-audit/src/entry.rs:17
File: docs/helix/01-frame/features/FEAT-026-markdown-templates.md:322
Category: correctness
Severity: high
Description: The new public template PUT/GET/DELETE surfaces persist and delete collection views directly, but neither put_collection_template nor delete_collection_template appends an audit entry, and the audit mutation enum has no template-specific operation at all. FEAT-026 explicitly requires template changes to be audited with actor, timestamp, and before/after state. As shipped, operators can change or remove a collection's presentation layer without any durable audit trail, violating both the feature contract and Axon's audit-first architecture.
Suggested fix: Introduce audit mutation types for template create/update/delete (or an equivalent collection-view mutation), capture before/after template state, and append audit entries from the handler on successful template writes and deletes. Add handler and gateway tests that query the audit log and verify actor plus before/after metadata for template changes.
title: correctness: template CRUD writes are not audited

description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: .ddx/beads.jsonl:691
Category: drift
Severity: medium
Description: Commit 519a68a7cb1edfca33619e04c5acadd34d734e5e is the first commit that appends a closed hx-53b2c44f row to .ddx/beads.jsonl, but that new row has no "notes" field at all. As a result it carries neither the machine-readable <measure-results> block required by /home/erik/Projects/helix/workflows/references/measure.md nor the top-level <report-summary> required by /home/erik/Projects/helix/workflows/references/report.md, and it also omits closing_commit_sha. Evidence: `git diff --unified=0 HEAD~1..HEAD -- .ddx/beads.jsonl` shows commit 519a68a7cb1edfca33619e04c5acadd34d734e5e only appends the closed hx-53b2c44f row; `python3 scripts/check_tracker_measure_timestamps.py .ddx/beads.jsonl --id hx-53b2c44f --require-report-summary` fails with 'missing notes'; and parsing the committed row shows `closing_commit_sha=None` and `has_notes=False`. Later tracker queries can therefore see hx-53b2c44f as closed, but they cannot recover what measured it, which commit closed it, or what artifact that close step verified.
Suggested fix: Append a new closed hx-53b2c44f row that preserves the current acceptance and description fields while adding durable notes containing a machine-readable <measure-results> block and a top-level <report-summary> naming commit 519a68a7cb1edfca33619e04c5acadd34d734e5e and the verified artifact .ddx/beads.jsonl:hx-06abf84a for the execution that actually measured and closed hx-53b2c44f.

acceptance: SQLite stores and reads schema/version/view metadata under the same resolved qualified collection identity used for entities; qualified writes followed by bare reads on a uniquely resolvable namespaced collection succeed for schema and view APIs; regression tests cover the affected schema and collection-view methods.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-storage/src/sqlite.rs:1085
File: crates/axon-storage/src/sqlite.rs:1113
File: crates/axon-storage/src/sqlite.rs:1130
File: crates/axon-storage/src/sqlite.rs:1138
File: crates/axon-storage/src/sqlite.rs:1245
File: crates/axon-storage/src/sqlite.rs:1280
File: crates/axon-storage/src/sqlite.rs:1301
File: crates/axon-storage/src/sqlite.rs:1308
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The SQLite entity table fix resolves qualified collection identity to (database_name, schema_name, bare collection), but schema and collection-view persistence still use the caller's raw collection string in both inserts and reads. A qualified write such as put_schema(prod.billing.invoices) stores collection = prod.billing.invoices, while a later bare lookup that resolves uniquely to the same namespace queries for collection = invoices and misses. The same mismatch exists for collection views. That makes metadata access inconsistent with entity CRUD and breaks any flow that relies on bare-name resolution after registering a unique non-default namespace.
Suggested fix: Normalize schema-version and collection-view SQL to use the resolved bare collection key consistently, just like entity CRUD does. Add regression tests covering qualified writes followed by bare reads for get_schema, get_schema_version, list_schema_versions, and get_collection_view on a uniquely resolvable namespaced collection.
title: correctness: sqlite schema and view lookups key off the raw collection string

acceptance: buildEnvPreview no longer references the literals 'sk-redact-me-please' or 'Bearer fake-session-cookie'; gitleaks scan of ui/src/lib/mcp-provenance.ts produces no findings; the redaction tests in mcp-provenance.test.ts assert on inputs that are not generated by buildEnvPreview (no shared fixture between SUT and assertion); existing e2e mcp-envelope-preview.spec.ts MCP stdio provenance test still passes and continues to verify that any secret-keyed env entry is redacted before it reaches the DOM
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review (parent: hx-0f99be1b).
File: ui/src/lib/mcp-provenance.ts:155-165 (buildEnvPreview)
Category: security
Severity: high
Description: buildEnvPreview hardcodes literal credential-shaped strings into the env preview list:
  { key: 'AXON_API_TOKEN', value: 'sk-redact-me-please' },
  { key: 'AXON_BEARER', value: 'Bearer fake-session-cookie' },
These values are then passed through redactEnvEntry so the panel shows '[redacted]'. Three problems:
1. security-owasp concern declares 'no secrets in source control' and gitleaks/secret scanning in CI. Strings starting with 'Bearer ' and 'sk-' match common gitleaks rules even when the value is fake; this will produce false-positive scan failures and trains operators to ignore the scanner.
2. The env preview claims (in its docstring) to surface 'documented operator-facing configuration' but two of its six entries are fabricated solely to demonstrate redaction. Operators reading the panel cannot tell which entries are real config and which are demo. This contradicts US-056 ('Operator setup/status surface links stdio config to agent-originated audit') because the linkage is fake.
3. The test buildMcpStdioProvenance > 'redacts credential and bearer entries from the env preview' is tautological: the function under test hardcodes the data the test redacts. The test cannot fail unless someone changes both production code and test in opposite directions.

Suggested fix: remove the AXON_API_TOKEN and AXON_BEARER fake entries from buildEnvPreview entirely. Keep only entries derived from the routing scope (tenant/database/agent_id/credential_id). Move redaction unit tests off the env-preview path: feed redactEnvEntry/isSecretEnvKey/isSecretEnvValue arbitrary inputs that are not produced by buildEnvPreview itself, so the redaction layer is tested against synthesized inputs rather than its own production fixture.
notes:

    <criterion name='no-shared-fixture' status='pass' evidence='isSecretEnvKey/isSecretEnvValue/redactEnvEntry tests use synthesized inputs not produced by buildEnvPreview; the env-preview integration test now asserts on the real routing-scope keys (AXON_TENANT/DATABASE/AGENT_ID/CREDENTIAL_ID) rather than fake AXON_API_TOKEN/AXON_BEARER fixtures'/>
    <criterion name='e2e-redaction-preserved' status='pass' evidence='mcp-envelope-preview.spec.ts now asserts AXON_CREDENTIAL_ID env row is redacted in DOM and the literal cred-finance-bot does not appear inside mcp-stdio-env table'/>

acceptance: After force-dropping a namespace or database, no outbound or inbound link query returns links whose source or target belongs to a removed collection, unrelated links remain intact, and regression tests cover the namespace/database drop paths.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:1500
File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:1552
File: crates/axon-storage/src/adapter.rs:572
File: crates/axon-storage/src/memory.rs:857
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The new namespace/database force-drop flow purges entity rows and appends collection-drop audits, but it never removes links whose source or target belongs to the dropped collections. In the default storage path, links are stored as separate pseudo-collection entities (__axon_links__ / __axon_links_rev__), and the in-memory adapter also keeps them in a dedicated link map. None of those stores are touched by drop_namespace/drop_database, so link queries can still return edges to deleted entities after a forced drop, violating FEAT-014's 'all contents' requirement and the technical requirement that link integrity hold.
Suggested fix: Cascade force-drop through link storage for every removed collection (forward and reverse link entries, plus the in-memory dedicated link map), then add regression tests showing namespace/database drop removes links attached to deleted collections while preserving unrelated links.
title: correctness: force-drop leaves dangling link records for removed collections

description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: .ddx/beads.jsonl:338
Category: drift
Severity: medium
Description: Commit 4daa41ab90945ea8bb8ad38eacbf58ef7c339b07 refreshes review-finding hx-84991cc7 so hx-f15cc9f0 now records the missing closing commit, but the governing bead hx-84991cc7 is itself closed with a durable <report-summary> that records only <artifact>.ddx/beads.jsonl:hx-f15cc9f0</artifact> and never names the commit that actually measured and closed hx-84991cc7. HELIX report guidance requires closure summaries to preserve references to the commits produced by that execution step, and this repository's tracker-governance review chain has already ratcheted on storing the closing commit for repaired beads. As committed, ddx bead show hx-84991cc7 --json can confirm the bead is closed and which artifact was refreshed, but it cannot recover that commit 4daa41ab90945ea8bb8ad38eacbf58ef7c339b07 performed the hx-84991cc7 closure.
Suggested fix: Append a new closed hx-84991cc7 record that preserves the current PASS measure-results and artifact reference to .ddx/beads.jsonl:hx-f15cc9f0 while adding a machine-readable <report-summary><commit>4daa41ab90945ea8bb8ad38eacbf58ef7c339b07</commit> for the execution that closed hx-84991cc7.

description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: .ddx/beads.jsonl:553
Category: drift
Severity: medium
Description: Commit 0097d26c4e3fccc754f49ff8eabde416f3f0eab2 adds and closes review-finding hx-eebcb714, but the committed hx-eebcb714 row contains no `notes` at all. `ddx bead show hx-eebcb714 --json` returns only acceptance/description metadata, and `python3 scripts/check_tracker_measure_timestamps.py .ddx/beads.jsonl --id hx-eebcb714` fails with `missing notes`. Under the HELIX measure and report references, a closed execution bead must preserve machine-readable `<measure-results>` and `<report-summary>` evidence so later tracker queries can recover what was verified and which execution closed the bead. As committed, later queries can see that hx-eebcb714 is closed, but they cannot recover any measurement evidence or the closure commit for hx-eebcb714 itself.
Suggested fix: Append a new closed hx-eebcb714 record that preserves the existing acceptance/description while adding a PASS `<measure-results>` block documenting the tracker verification commands and a machine-readable `<report-summary><commit>0097d26c4e3fccc754f49ff8eabde416f3f0eab2</commit>` for the execution that measured and closed hx-eebcb714.

acceptance: The latest closed hx-3f3ded17 record in .ddx/beads.jsonl records rust-cargo gate outcomes that are consistent with commit 95d2d042a68e4f4a4fbc36b8784bdf8588e412ba's verification evidence, and ddx bead show hx-3f3ded17 --json no longer reports cargo gates as 'Not run' if the closing commit claims they were executed.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: .ddx/beads.jsonl:246
Category: drift
Severity: medium
Description: The closing commit 95d2d042a68e4f4a4fbc36b8784bdf8588e412ba advertises `cargo check`, `cargo test`, `cargo clippy -- -D warnings`, `cargo fmt --check`, `cargo deny check`, and `cargo machete crates` in its Verification trailer, but the durable <measure-results> block on hx-3f3ded17 still records each rust-cargo gate as status='partial' with "Not run for scoped verification" details copied from the earlier implementation step. That leaves the governing bead's machine-readable measurement evidence internally inconsistent with the execution evidence attached to the commit that closes it, weakening concern-aware auditability and making later tracker queries misreport what was actually verified for this closure.
Suggested fix: Refresh the closed hx-3f3ded17 tracker record so its rust-cargo gate accounting matches the verification actually performed for commit 95d2d042a68e4f4a4fbc36b8784bdf8588e412ba, or explicitly narrow the commit's recorded verification surface to only the commands that were run.

acceptance: http_update_version_conflict_returns_409 asserts body["detail"]["current_entity"] is non-null with correct id, version, and data fields; cargo test -p axon-server passes
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-server/src/gateway.rs:715-732
Category: integration
Severity: medium
Spec: crates/axon-server/src/gateway.rs
Description: The HTTP gateway tests http_update_version_conflict_returns_409 and http_errors_are_structured_with_field_level_details do not assert that current_entity is present in 409 conflict responses. The gRPC path (service.rs) has two tests verifying current_entity for both Some and None cases, but the HTTP path lacks equivalent coverage. This creates a P6 (API parity) test-coverage gap between transports — a regression in the gateway serialization of current_entity would go undetected.
Suggested fix: Add assertions to http_update_version_conflict_returns_409 verifying body["detail"]["current_entity"] is non-null and contains the expected entity id, version, and data. Optionally add a second test for the null case.
title: integration: HTTP conflict tests missing current_entity assertions

acceptance: The repo and active concern stack converge on one documented policy: either add the pinned toolchain / wrapper path and tighten unsafe_code enforcement to match the concern, or update docs/helix/01-frame/concerns.md with explicit project overrides so the declared concern no longer contradicts implementation and CI.
description:
Discovered from review issue hx-1686cefa under governing bead hx-08e590f6.

Planning evidence:
- /home/erik/Projects/helix/workflows/concerns/rust-cargo/concern.md:10-22 requires a pinned rust-toolchain.toml / wrapper workflow and declares unsafe_code = deny as the concern default.
- docs/helix/01-frame/concerns.md:15-18 only overrides edition and MSRV; it does not override toolchain pinning or the unsafe-code policy.

Implementation evidence:
- The repo has no rust-toolchain.toml at the root.
- Cargo.toml:64-67 sets workspace.lints.rust unsafe_code = warn, not deny.
- CI uses stable toolchain actions directly rather than a repo-pinned wrapper.

Gap:
The active rust-cargo concern and the repo implementation no longer agree on toolchain pinning and unsafe-code enforcement, so the concern stack is partially contradictory.

acceptance: After an /mcp/sse client disconnects, reconnecting without an explicit session-recovery token and without sending resources/subscribe does not receive resource_updated events from the prior connection’s subscriptions.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-server/src/mcp_http.rs:108
Category: integration
Severity: medium
Description: The new HTTP transport never tears down session subscriptions when an SSE client disconnects. connect() only appends a sender to listeners, handle_mcp_sse() never removes the session on stream close, and publish_entity_change() only prunes dead senders opportunistically after a later mutation while leaving the McpServer and its subscriptions vector intact. I reproduced this against a live server on 2026-04-09: subscribe once to axon://tasks/t-001, close both SSE connections, open a fresh GET /mcp/sse connection without sending another subscribe request, then update tasks/t-001 again. The fresh connection immediately received resource_updated, proving the old subscription survived the disconnect and leaked into the next transport session. That contradicts FEAT-016’s edge-case requirement that agent disconnects clean up subscriptions and leaves stale session state growing indefinitely.
Suggested fix: Treat the SSE lifecycle as the lifetime of the HTTP MCP session. Remove the session entry, or at minimum clear its subscriptions, when the last listener disconnects. If durable sessions are required across reconnects, make reconnection explicit with a server-issued session token plus TTL/cleanup policy instead of silently retaining subscriptions forever.
notes:
<measure-results>

  </acceptance>

acceptance: ADR-004 Protocol section commit sequence shows audit entries flushed after commit_tx(), not before. grep for 'Write all audit entries' in the Protocol block returns 0 or the line clearly indicates post-commit timing.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review of commit 5b749c1.
File: docs/helix/02-design/adr/ADR-004-transaction-model.md:53-55
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: ADR-004 Protocol section (lines 53-55) shows the commit sequence as: (1) Apply all entity writes, (2) Write all audit entries, (3) Commit storage transaction. This implies audit entries are written before commit_tx(), inside the storage transaction. However, ADR-004's own Audit Integration section (lines 93-94) correctly states audit entries are buffered and flushed only after commit_tx() succeeds. The code (transaction.rs:152-160) confirms post-commit flush. This is the same class of residual same-transaction audit claim that commit 5b749c1 fixed in ADR-003.
Suggested fix: Update the Protocol commit sequence to show audit entry buffering during execute, storage commit, then post-commit audit flush. E.g.: Apply all entity writes (version + 1) -> Commit storage transaction (atomic via StorageAdapter) -> Flush buffered audit entries (post-commit).
title: correctness: ADR-004 Protocol section describes pre-commit audit writes, contradicting post-commit strategy

acceptance: When `get_entity_markdown` returns `RenderFailed`, `axon --output json entity get ... --render markdown` and `axon --output yaml entity get ... --render markdown` emit parseable structured output containing both the entity payload and the render-failure detail; regression tests cover both formats.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-cli/src/main.rs:891
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The new markdown CLI path now serializes `GetEntityMarkdownResponse` for JSON/YAML only when the handler returns `Rendered`, but the adjacent `RenderFailed` arm still bails immediately. That throws away the structured failure payload even though `AxonHandler::get_entity_markdown()` deliberately preserves the fetched entity in `RenderFailed { entity, detail }` and the HTTP gateway surfaces that payload on markdown render failure. In `--output json` or `--output yaml` mode, scripts and agents therefore still lose the machine-readable entity fallback on the error path, which is exactly when structured output is most valuable.
Suggested fix: In JSON/YAML modes, serialize the full `GetEntityMarkdownResponse` for both `Rendered` and `RenderFailed` outcomes, then return a non-zero exit status if desired after writing the structured payload. Add CLI regression coverage for render-failure JSON and YAML output so the entity payload and detail remain available to machine consumers.
title: correctness: CLI markdown structured output drops render-failure payload

acceptance: On an upgraded Postgres catalog where legacy schema or collection-view rows still store the raw qualified collection string and default namespace columns, `unregister_collection(<qualified>)` removes those rows completely; a regression test seeds that state for both tables and verifies zero matching metadata rows remain afterward.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs:215
File: crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs:227
File: crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs:1184
File: crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs:1210
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The fresh fix deletes raw-qualified legacy metadata rows only inside the resolved namespace (`database_name = prod`, `schema_name = billing`), but the upgrade path that created this class of legacy rows can leave them in `default/default`. `ensure_namespace_catalog_tables()` only backfills namespace columns by joining `schemas.collection = collections.name` and `collection_views.collection = collections.name`. For pre-fix rows whose `collection` value is still the raw qualified identifier like `prod.billing.invoices`, those joins miss and the newly added namespace columns stay at their default values. A later `unregister_collection(prod.billing.invoices)` now deletes the normalized bare rows in `prod.billing`, plus raw-qualified rows in `prod.billing`, but it still does not delete the legacy raw-qualified rows stranded in `default/default`. The new regression test seeds the legacy row directly in the resolved namespace, so it does not cover the actual upgrade state implied by the migration SQL.
Suggested fix: Normalize or clean up raw-qualified legacy rows using the parsed collection identifier rather than only the resolved namespace. Either rewrite legacy `schemas` and `collection_views` rows during upgrade so raw qualified identifiers get their correct `database_name` and `schema_name`, or make `unregister_collection` also delete raw-qualified legacy rows that still sit in `default/default`. Add a regression test that seeds a pre-upgrade raw-qualified schema and collection-view row with default namespace columns and verifies qualified unregister removes them fully.
title: correctness: default-namespaced legacy Postgres metadata survives qualified unregister

acceptance: When buildMcpEnvelopePreview returns a preview with policyVersion=null and the explanation has policyVersion=null, the policy-comparison element either renders match=true or renders an explicit 'unknown' state; new unit test in policy-evaluator.test.ts (or component test) asserts the desired behavior; e2e mcp-envelope-preview bridge test still passes (existing positive case has matching non-null versions)
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review (parent: hx-0f99be1b).
File: ui/src/routes/tenants/[tenant]/databases/[database]/policies/+page.svelte:160-172 (mcpEnvelopeComparison \$derived)
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The newly added mcpEnvelopeComparison computes:
  policyVersionMatches:
    mcpEnvelopePreview.policyVersion !== null &&
    mcpEnvelopePreview.policyVersion === explanation.policyVersion
The leading null-guard means that when both sides return null for policyVersion (e.g., a collection without an active policy where neither the envelope nor the explanation can resolve a version), policyVersionMatches is false and the UI renders 'Policy version differs (MCP: v?; GraphQL: v?)'. Two unknown values are not a mismatch — they are jointly unknown — and rendering them as a mismatch will cause operators to file false drift reports.

Same shape concern (less severe) for outcomeMatches: it is reason-vs-reason; if both are empty strings it would currently match, so OK there.

Suggested fix: drop the null-guard so the comparison is mcpEnvelopePreview.policyVersion === explanation.policyVersion (null === null returns true). Alternatively, introduce a third state 'unknown' rendered as 'Policy version unknown (no active version)' rather than match/mismatch when either side is null.
notes:
<measure-results>
  <timestamp>2026-04-27T08:13:00Z</timestamp>
  <status>PASS</status>
  <acceptance>
    <criterion name='unknown-state for null/null policyVersion' status='pass' evidence='buildMcpEnvelopeComparison returns policyVersionMatch=unknown when both sides are null; UI renders Policy version unknown (no active version)'/>
    <criterion name='unit test in policy-evaluator.test.ts' status='pass' evidence='6 new tests in buildMcpEnvelopeComparison describe block; bun test 87 pass / 0 fail'/>
    <criterion name='e2e bridge test parity preserved' status='pass' evidence='match path emits data-match=match for non-null matching versions; preview rendering unchanged for that case'/>
  </acceptance>
  <gates>
    <gate concern='typescript-bun' command='bun run typecheck' status='pass'/>
    <gate concern='typescript-bun' command='bun run lint (biome check)' status='pass'/>
    <gate concern='typescript-bun' command='bun test src/lib/policy-evaluator.test.ts' status='pass'/>
  </gates>
</measure-results>
title: correctness: GraphQL parity comparison treats null/null policy versions as a mismatch

description:

Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The test plan specifies PROP-004 as verifying concurrent multi-entity transaction serializability. The implemented test only tests a single transaction with correct or incorrect versions. It does not test multiple overlapping transactions or verify serializable outcomes. acceptance: Test: a FilterNode nested deeper than the configured max depth (e.g. 32) returns an AxonError::InvalidArgument. Test: a filter at exactly the max depth succeeds. No stack overflow on deeply nested input.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:664-668 and crates/axon-api/src/request.rs:149-154
Category: security
Severity: medium
Description: FilterNode is a recursive enum (And/Or variants contain Vec of FilterNode). The apply_filter function recurses without a depth limit. A malicious or buggy client can send a deeply nested filter via the HTTP API (thousands of levels of nesting), causing a stack overflow and crashing the server process. The HTTP gateway directly deserializes client-provided JSON into FilterNode with no depth validation.
Suggested fix: Add a depth limit (e.g. max 32 levels) validated at the start of query_entities before filter evaluation.
title: security: no recursion depth limit on FilterNode tree

acceptance: Either: (a) CreateCollectionRequest includes a schema field and create_collection persists it, with a test asserting create without schema returns an error; OR (b) FEAT-001 constraints section is updated to document the create-then-bind pattern
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:499-522, crates/axon-api/src/request.rs:218-221
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: FEAT-001 overview says a collection is a named schema-bound container. US-001 acceptance criteria requires schema validation before creation. FEAT-001 constraints state schema binding is required. However CreateCollectionRequest only has name and actor — no schema field. Collections are created without any schema binding. The bead description says explicit create with schema binding but schema is not part of the create flow.
Suggested fix: Either add schema CollectionSchema to CreateCollectionRequest and validate and persist it during creation, or document the intentional deviation and update FEAT-001 constraints.
title: correctness: create_collection does not bind a schema per FEAT-001

acceptance: ddx bead show hx-daa8528f --json reports a latest closed record whose measure/report statuses are consistent with a review that filed hx-ea882ad7, and whose <report-summary> includes <commit>70c6f226814c91e91c770f96a15e5f24fb65d366</commit>; the refreshed row remains machine-readable and passes python3 scripts/check_tracker_measure_timestamps.py .ddx/beads.jsonl --id hx-daa8528f.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: .ddx/beads.jsonl:529
Category: drift
Severity: medium
Description: Commit 70c6f226814c91e91c770f96a15e5f24fb65d366 adds closed review bead hx-daa8528f for commit b1af1c873d491c9a96a1201eab0396b7b2e6296f, but its durable review evidence is internally inconsistent in two ways. First, <measure-results><status>PASS</status> and <report-summary><status>PASS</status> are recorded even though the same row says One medium drift finding was identified and filed as hx-ea882ad7. The fresh-eyes review actions measure phase requires review outcomes to be recorded as CLEAN or ISSUES_FOUND, and a review that files a follow-on defect is not a clean PASS. Second, the same <report-summary> omits the closing commit reference entirely, so later tracker queries cannot recover which execution created and closed the review bead. Together these fields overstate the review outcome and weaken auditability of the new review record itself.
Suggested fix: Refresh hx-daa8528f in .ddx/beads.jsonl so its machine-readable measurement/report statuses match the review outcome (ISSUES_FOUND and a follow-on style report status, or the repos canonical equivalent for a finding-bearing review), and add <report-summary><commit>70c6f226814c91e91c770f96a15e5f24fb65d366</commit> for the execution that wrote the row.

Conditional on the V1-scope decision, author either a FEAT-011 amendment (new stories US-046..US-04n) or a new FEAT-031 "Policy and Intents Admin UI". The spec must define:
1. Effective-policy / explainPolicy introspection surface in the database workspace (view-only).
2. Pending mutation-intent list route (per-database), with filter by status (pending/approved/rejected/expired/committed) and approver role.
3. Intent detail route showing diff, pre-image versions, policy explanation, operation hash, subject, credential/grant versions, and TTL.
4. Approve and reject actions (reason optional/required per policy), with structured error when the caller lacks the approver role.
5. Stale-intent handling in the UI (re-preview / resubmit flow).
6. Integration with audit route (jump to audit entry for the committed intent and vice versa).
7. Reverse route coverage table and Playwright test mapping.
8. Dependency citation of ADR-019 and (backend beads hx-44bdafd1 / hx-5e34a26c).
9. Explicit OOS list.
This bead blocks UI build decomposition.

acceptance: Deleted-entity rollback returns ConflictingVersion/HTTP 409 when another writer recreates the entity before the rollback write commits, and regression coverage proves the newer entity state is preserved instead of being overwritten
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:1453
File: crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs:479
Category: integration
Severity: high
Description: The new deleted-entity branch in rollback_entity recreates the entity with storage.put(recreated.clone()) instead of an OCC-checked write. The live-entity branch uses compare_and_swap, but the deleted branch performs a blind upsert. In the Postgres adapter, put is implemented as INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE, so a stale deleted-entity rollback can overwrite a concurrent recreate or concurrent rollback and still return success instead of a 409 conflict. FEAT-023 says standard OCC applies to entity rollback, and Principle P4 forbids lost updates, so this branch currently weakens concurrency guarantees precisely for the deleted-entity recovery path this commit added.
Suggested fix: Introduce a tombstone-aware compare-and-swap or create-if-version API for deleted-entity rollback so the recreate only succeeds when the entity is still absent at the expected deleted version, and add regression coverage for a stale/concurrent recreate case on an adapter that can upsert existing rows.
notes:
<measure-results>
  <timestamp>2026-04-09T16:51:42Z</timestamp>
  <status>PASS</status>

acceptance: DELETE /collections/{collection}/template succeeds when the request has Content-Type: application/json and an empty body; a gateway regression test covers that request shape; cargo test -p axon-server http_collection_template_crud_round_trip_uses_public_surface passes with the new regression test also green
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-server/src/gateway.rs:813
File: /home/erik/.cargo/registry/src/index.crates.io-1949cf8c6b5b557f/axum-0.8.8/src/json.rs:123
Category: integration
Severity: medium
Description: The new DELETE template handler switched from a body-less route to Option<Json<DeleteCollectionTemplateBody>>. Under the axum 0.8.8 OptionalFromRequest implementation, Option<Json<_>> is only optional when the Content-Type header is absent; if a client sends Content-Type: application/json, axum always tries to deserialize the body and rejects empty bodies with a JSON syntax error. That means generic HTTP clients that send an empty JSON DELETE now get a 4xx response even though the route previously succeeded and the body is semantically optional.
Suggested fix: Replace the extractor with a tolerant parser that treats an empty JSON body as no actor, or accept raw bytes and parse an actor object only when a non-empty body is present. Add a gateway regression test that sends DELETE /collections/{collection}/template with Content-Type: application/json and an empty body and asserts success.
title: integration: template DELETE rejects empty JSON requests

acceptance: A test covers a failed contract probe followed by a successful retry, and the second helper call re-issues the probe instead of reusing a permanently rejected promise.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: ui/src/lib/graphql.ts:90
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: assertGraphQLHelperContract memoizes the first probe promise in helperContractCheck, but it never clears that cache when the probe fails. A transient fetch error or temporary 5xx from /graphql permanently poisons the helper for the rest of the page lifetime: subsequent calls reuse the same rejected promise and fail immediately even after the backend recovers. Reproduced with a bun eval against ui/src/lib/graphql.ts that throws on the first fetch, succeeds on the second fetch, and still reports the same error twice because the second helper call never retries the probe.
Suggested fix: Clear helperContractCheck when the probe rejects, or cache only successful support checks so later calls can retry after transient failures.
title: correctness: GraphQL helper contract probe caches transient failures forever

acceptance: Validator fails for fixtures containing a top-level self-closing <measure-results/> block even when another valid measure block is present later in the same notes, and regression tests cover both unscoped and --id-scoped runs.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: scripts/check_tracker_measure_timestamps.py:41
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The new parser still ignores a top-level self-closing <measure-results/> tag because handle_startendtag() only records self-closing <timestamp/> tags while top-level block detection lives only in handle_starttag(). As a result, malformed tracker evidence can disappear whenever another valid block is present. Reproduction: run python3 scripts/check_tracker_measure_timestamps.py <fixture.jsonl> against a one-record fixture whose notes are <measure-results/><measure-results><timestamp>2026-04-09T18:59:11Z</timestamp></measure-results> and updated_at is later than the timestamp; the command exits 0 and reports success even though the first top-level measurement block is malformed. The same scoped run with --id also exits 0. This violates the governing requirement that every malformed top-level measurement block fail validation and weakens tracker auditability by letting truncated or self-closed evidence hide behind a later valid block.
Suggested fix: Treat top-level self-closing <measure-results/> tags as malformed measurement blocks, either by tracking them explicitly in handle_startendtag() or by sharing the top-level block-detection path across start-tag forms. Add a regression test covering a self-closing malformed block followed by a valid block and assert both unscoped and scoped validation exit non-zero with a malformed-block error.
notes:
<measure-results>
  <timestamp>2026-04-09T22:00:28Z</timestamp>
  <status>PASS</status>

    <criterion name='scoped --id validation also fails for the malformed self-closing block fixture' status='pass' evidence='python3 tests/test_check_tracker_measure_timestamps.py::self_closing_measure_results_with_later_valid_block_fails_scoped'/>
  </acceptance>
  <gates>
    <gate concern='tracker-governance' command='python3 tests/test_check_tracker_measure_timestamps.py' status='pass'/>
  </gates>
</measure-results>

This tracker refresh preserves the closure evidence that was missing from the original closed row.</summary>

acceptance: filter_depth uses an iterative (non-recursive) algorithm. Test: a FilterNode tree of depth 100_000 returns the correct depth value without stack overflow.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:686-692
Spec: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs
Category: security/correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The filter_depth() function uses recursion to measure FilterNode tree depth. If an attacker constructs a tree deep enough to overflow the stack (e.g. millions of levels via a non-JSON path), the overflow occurs inside filter_depth before the depth check at line 228 can reject it. The commit message for 9d63b5a claims 'safe linear traversal' but the implementation recurses. Practical risk is mitigated by serde_json's default recursion limit (128) for HTTP deserialization, and 1000 levels works in tests. But defense-in-depth is incomplete — the guard itself shares the vulnerability it protects against.
Suggested fix: Rewrite filter_depth as an iterative traversal using an explicit Vec<&FilterNode> stack, eliminating recursive calls.
title: security: filter_depth itself uses unbounded recursion

acceptance: ADR-004 references the correct error variant names matching crates/axon-core/src/error.rs: ConflictingVersion (not Conflict) and SchemaValidation (not SchemaViolation)
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: docs/helix/02-design/adr/ADR-004-transaction-model.md:69,73
File: crates/axon-core/src/error.rs:20,13
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: ADR-004 line 69 says 'Axon returns AxonError::Conflict' but the actual variant is AxonError::ConflictingVersion (error.rs:20). Line 73 says 'AxonError::SchemaViolation with retryable: false' but the actual variant is AxonError::SchemaValidation (error.rs:13) and neither variant has a retryable field. These naming mismatches will confuse implementers who try to match the ADR specification against the code.
Suggested fix: Update ADR-004 to use the correct variant names (ConflictingVersion, SchemaValidation). If the retryable field is intended as a future addition, note it as such or file a separate implementation issue.
title: correctness: ADR-004 references AxonError::Conflict and SchemaViolation but actual variants are ConflictingVersion and SchemaValidation

acceptance: 1. Transaction::commit calls abort_tx() when commit_tx() fails. 2. A test exists that simulates commit_tx failure and verifies abort_tx is called and the adapter is not wedged.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-api/src/transaction.rs:141-143
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Spec: crates/axon-api/src/transaction.rs
Description: In Transaction::commit, when execute() succeeds but commit_tx() fails, no abort_tx() is called. The storage-level transaction is left open with uncommitted writes buffered. For SqliteStorageAdapter, in_tx stays true, wedging the adapter so subsequent begin_tx() calls are rejected. This violates P4 (Transactions Mean Transactions) — a failed commit should leave the system in a clean state.
Suggested fix: Wrap commit_tx() failure to call abort_tx() before propagating the error:
  Ok(written) => match storage.commit_tx() {
      Ok(()) => Ok(written),
      Err(e) => { let _ = storage.abort_tx(); Err(e) }
  }
title: correctness: abort_tx not called on commit_tx failure in Transaction::commit

acceptance: Namespace::parse("billing.invoices") resolves to database=default, schema=billing, collection=invoices in the default-db case; adapter/handler tests cover two-part reads or writes against default.billing.invoices; three-part resolution for prod.billing.invoices still passes.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-core/src/id.rs:51
File: crates/axon-core/src/id.rs:56
File: crates/axon-core/src/id.rs:265
Category: integration
Severity: high
Description: FEAT-014 and ADR-011 define two-part collection names as schema-qualified shorthand using the current database, so billing.invoices must resolve to {current_db}.billing.invoices (in the default case, default.billing.invoices). Namespace::parse still interprets every two-part name as database.collection and returns (billing.default, invoices), and the unit test at parse_two_part_name locks that behavior in. Because resolve_collection_key and all adapter resolve_catalog_key implementations call Namespace::parse, the reviewed OCC fix only preserves identity for three-part names; a two-part update/patch/get path will route to the wrong namespace, and if a database named billing exists it can silently target the wrong tenant.
Suggested fix: Change two-part parsing to mean schema.collection with the current/default database applied, update the affected unit tests, and add handler/storage regression coverage proving billing.invoices resolves to default.billing.invoices in default-db flows while three-part names keep working unchanged.
title: integration: two-part collection names resolve as database.collection instead of schema.collection

acceptance: axon collection template delete <collection> accepts an optional --actor flag; the CLI passes that actor into DeleteCollectionTemplateRequest; a CLI or handler-backed regression test verifies the template.delete audit entry records the provided actor
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-cli/src/main.rs:197
File: crates/axon-cli/src/main.rs:824
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: This commit threads actor through DeleteCollectionTemplateRequest, but the CLI surface still defines axon collection template delete without an --actor flag and hardcodes actor: None when invoking the handler. As a result, template deletions performed through the shipped CLI are always audited as anonymous, even though axon collection template put already supports actor attribution and FEAT-026 requires template changes to be audited with actor provenance.
Suggested fix: Add --actor <id> to CollectionTemplateCmd::Delete, pass it into DeleteCollectionTemplateRequest, and add CLI coverage proving the resulting audit entry records the supplied actor for template deletion.
title: correctness: template delete CLI cannot attribute actor

acceptance: Postgres stores and reads schema/version/view metadata under the same resolved qualified collection identity used for entities; qualified writes followed by bare reads on a uniquely resolvable namespaced collection succeed for schema and view APIs; regression tests cover the affected schema and collection-view methods.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs:820
File: crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs:843
File: crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs:860
File: crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs:863
File: crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs:910
File: crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs:945
File: crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs:967
File: crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs:970
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: Postgres now keys entity rows by (database_name, schema_name, bare collection, id), but the schema and collection-view tables still persist and query the caller's raw collection string. A qualified write such as put_schema(prod.billing.invoices) stores collection = prod.billing.invoices, while a later bare lookup that resolves uniquely to the same namespace queries for collection = invoices and returns nothing. The same mismatch applies to collection views. This leaves metadata behavior out of sync with the entity data path and breaks bare-name access once a unique non-default namespace is involved.
Suggested fix: Normalize Postgres schema and collection-view persistence/lookups to use the resolved bare collection key consistently, matching the entity CRUD path. Add regression tests for qualified writes followed by bare reads on schema/version/view methods for a uniquely resolvable namespaced collection.
title: correctness: postgres schema and view lookups key off the raw collection string

acceptance: When buildMcpStdioProvenance is called with an audit entry whose timestamp_ns is more than 60 minutes before now, the resulting configStatusLabel includes the lastSeen timestamp and does not contain the substring 'No recent stdio activity recorded'; existing test 'marks idle status when last activity is hours old' is extended to assert the stale label content; e2e mcp-envelope-preview MCP stdio provenance test still passes
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review (parent: hx-0f99be1b).
File: ui/src/lib/mcp-provenance.ts:104-123 (classifyConnection / statusLabel)
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: classifyConnection returns 'unknown' both when lastActivityNs is null (genuinely no activity) and when activity exists but is older than 60 minutes. statusLabel then renders 'No recent stdio activity recorded' for both cases, even when the audit row clearly shows the agent connected hours ago. Operators reviewing an older intent see misleading 'no activity' text alongside an audit timeline that shows activity.

Suggested fix: distinguish 'stale' (had activity but >60min ago) from 'unknown' (no audit at all). Either:
(a) introduce a third McpStdioConnectionStatus value 'stale' and render 'Stale (last stdio activity \${lastSeen})', or
(b) keep two states but make statusLabel always include the last-activity timestamp when lastActivityNs is non-null, regardless of status.

acceptance: A scoped validation fixture containing an unclosed top-level <measure-results> block exits non-zero with a malformed-block error, while well-formed top-level measure blocks with a single timestamp still pass.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: scripts/check_tracker_measure_timestamps.py:29
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: The new parser records a top-level <measure-results> block as soon as it sees the opening tag, and it never verifies that a matching closing </measure-results> tag was present. As a result, a truncated notes payload such as <measure-results><timestamp>2026-04-09T18:59:11Z</timestamp> is accepted as valid scoped evidence. Reproduction: run python3 scripts/check_tracker_measure_timestamps.py /tmp/fixture.jsonl --id hx-unclosed-measure against a one-record fixture whose notes are exactly that truncated string; the command exits 0 and prints success even though the measurement block is malformed and not machine-parseable as a real HELIX measure record. The measure workflow defines <measure-results> as a block with structured contents, so accepting an unterminated block weakens tracker auditability and lets corrupted review evidence pass validation.
Suggested fix: Only emit a parsed measure-results block after the parser has seen the matching closing </measure-results> tag, or otherwise track open/closed state so unterminated top-level blocks fail validation. Add a regression test asserting that a requested bead with an unclosed <measure-results> block exits non-zero with a malformed-block error.
notes:

    <criterion name='well_formed_single_timestamp_blocks_still_pass' status='pass' evidence='python3 tests/test_check_tracker_measure_timestamps.py'/>
  </acceptance>
  <gates>
    <gate concern='scoped-python-tests' command='python3 tests/test_check_tracker_measure_timestamps.py' status='pass'/>
    <gate concern='scoped-cli-repro' command='python3 scripts/check_tracker_measure_timestamps.py <tmp> --id hx-unclosed-measure' status='pass'/>

Category: drift
Severity: medium
Description: The reviewed commit adds a strict TypeScript gate, but `allowJs: true` with `checkJs: false` leaves the existing UI application modules outside semantic type checking. The current UI logic remains in JavaScript files such as `ui/src/lib/graphql.js` and plain `<script>` blocks under `ui/src/routes/`, so `bun run typecheck` succeeds without enforcing the `TypeScript throughout` practice declared in `docs/helix/01-frame/concerns.md` and FEAT-011.
Suggested fix: Migrate the UI helper and route scripts to TypeScript (for example `.ts` and `<script lang="ts">`) or enable JS checking until the migration is complete so the typecheck gate actually covers the shipped UI logic.

description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: .ddx/beads.jsonl:497
Category: drift
Severity: medium
Description: The same active closed hx-ae520769 record records only four rust-cargo gates in its PASS <measure-results><gates> block: `cargo check`, `cargo test`, `cargo clippy -- -D warnings`, and `cargo fmt --check`. The active rust-cargo concern in this repo declares a broader quality-gate surface that also includes `cargo deny check` and `cargo machete crates`, and recent tracker-only closure repairs either record those commands as pass or explicitly mark them partial with scope-specific rationale. Here they are omitted entirely, so the bead's durable measurement under-reports the governing concern and leaves the PASS result under-specified relative to the project's declared practices.
Suggested fix: Refresh hx-ae520769 so its latest <measure-results><gates> block records the full rust-cargo concern surface for this repo, including `cargo deny check` and `cargo machete crates`, or explicitly marks them partial/not applicable with scope-specific evidence for this tracker-only change instead of omitting them.

description:

File: .ddx/beads.jsonl:446
Category: drift
Severity: medium
Description: Commit d7c9acbf3245690a425ac685f8f95b9d1966e284 closes review-finding hx-ea0ee002 by flipping its tracker row from status="in_progress" to status="closed", but it leaves the durable notes unchanged. The row's existing <report-summary><commit> still points to 73b85abfc683108a8d4ee5d9d9e4e6d60e380e1b, which was the earlier commit that repaired hx-035ebd2f, not the HEAD commit that actually performed the hx-ea0ee002 closure. Under the HELIX report workflow, the governing bead's closure record must preserve references to the commits produced by that execution step. As committed, later tracker queries can see that hx-ea0ee002 is closed, but they cannot recover which commit actually closed and measured hx-ea0ee002 itself, so the bead remains non-auditable in exactly the way this tracker-fix chain is trying to prevent.
Suggested fix: Append a new closed hx-ea0ee002 record that preserves the current PASS <measure-results> block and adds a machine-readable <report-summary><commit>d7c9acbf3245690a425ac685f8f95b9d1966e284</commit> while retaining the historical 73b85abfc683108a8d4ee5d9d9e4e6d60e380e1b reference as evidence for the earlier hx-035ebd2f repair.

The new report-summary added in HEAD therefore remains audit-incomplete in the same way earlier tracker repairs were: later tracker queries can see hx-1ce79f3b is closed and references artifact .ddx/beads.jsonl:hx-d762919b, but they still cannot recover that commit e4eaa99b34d3aa46cebcb86c399c3355af63b6ec measured and closed hx-1ce79f3b itself. Under /home/erik/Projects/helix/workflows/references/report.md, the governing bead closure summary must preserve references to the commits produced by that execution step.
Suggested fix: Append a new closed hx-1ce79f3b record that preserves the current PASS `<measure-results>` block and artifact reference to .ddx/beads.jsonl:hx-d762919b while updating the machine-readable `<report-summary><commit>` to e4eaa99b34d3aa46cebcb86c399c3355af63b6ec for the execution that actually closed hx-1ce79f3b.

acceptance: Opening a SQLite database created with the pre-fix collection_views schema and then unregistering a collection removes any stored collection view. An automated regression test covers the SQLite upgrade path.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-storage/src/sqlite.rs:53
Category: integration
Severity: medium
Description: The SQLite fix only adds the `collection_views -> collections` foreign key inside `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS`. On databases created before commit f3bfb06, `collection_views` already exists without that constraint, so opening the database with the new code does not backfill the foreign key. `unregister_collection()` still deletes only from `collections`, which leaves stale collection views behind on upgraded databases. I reproduced this by creating the pre-fix schema, running the new init SQL, inserting a collection and view, deleting the collection, and observing `SELECT COUNT(*) FROM collection_views` still returned `1`.
Suggested fix: Add an explicit SQLite migration or fallback cleanup path for upgraded databases. At minimum, `unregister_collection()` should delete from `collection_views` directly; ideally the adapter should also migrate existing SQLite databases so the foreign key and cascade are present after upgrade, and add an upgrade regression test.
title: integration: SQLite upgrades keep stale collection views after unregister

acceptance: The GraphQL helper-contract probe no longer requests the full __schema.types table; a regression test verifies the probe only asks for the specific root fields and named helper types it validates, and the existing helper-contract tests still pass
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: ui/src/lib/graphql.ts:13
Category: performance
Severity: medium
Description: The new helper-contract probe now requests the full __schema.types table for the entire GraphQL schema before the first UI data fetch. That is much broader than the helper needs: the code only validates three root fields plus the shapes of CollectionMeta, EntityConnection, EntityEdge, PageInfo, and the entity return type. On non-trivial schemas this turns every cold helper call into a full-schema introspection round trip, increasing startup latency and response size well beyond the previous targeted probe. It also works against FEAT-015 US-049's introspection latency goal and risks tripping future GraphQL depth or complexity limits once those protections are enabled.
Suggested fix: Replace the full schema type scan with a targeted contract probe. For example, inspect the query root first and then fetch only the named types referenced by the helper fields, or split the validation into smaller __type(name: ...) queries so the client never downloads the entire schema just to decide whether these helpers are safe to use.
notes:

acceptance: A contract test executes the helper queries against a schema that matches FEAT-015 and ADR-012, and the helpers either succeed with query documents that match the specified schema or continue to fail fast without issuing invalid selection sets.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: ui/src/lib/graphql.ts:96
Category: integration
Severity: medium
Description: The new helper-contract gate only checks for the presence of the root fields collections, entities, and entity, plus minimal metadata on CollectionMeta and EntityConnection. But the governing FEAT-015 and ADR-012 spec defines the generic entity(collection, id) and entities(collection, ...) queries as returning untyped JSON, while these helpers still send selection sets that require structured fields like id, version, data, and pageInfo.hasNextPage. Once FEAT-015 lands, assertGraphQLHelperContract can report support even though fetchEntity and fetchEntities still issue invalid queries against the specified schema.
Suggested fix: Validate the actual response shape that the UI queries require, or redesign the helpers to match the FEAT-015 generic-query contract before treating the backend as supported.
title: integration: GraphQL helper gate does not verify FEAT-015 response shape

acceptance: `cargo clippy -p axon-api --all-targets --no-deps -- -D warnings -D clippy::todo -D clippy::unimplemented -D clippy::dbg_macro -D clippy::print_stdout -D clippy::unwrap_used` passes, and the namespaced patch regression test still passes.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:8987
Category: drift
Severity: medium
Description: The reviewed commit adds `assert_eq!(response.gates["complete"].pass, true);` in the new namespaced patch regression test. Under the active rust-cargo concern, `cargo clippy -p axon-api --all-targets --no-deps -- -D warnings -D clippy::todo -D clippy::unimplemented -D clippy::dbg_macro -D clippy::print_stdout -D clippy::unwrap_used` now fails with `clippy::bool_assert_comparison`, so the changed package no longer satisfies the required strict clippy gate.
Suggested fix: Replace the literal-bool comparison with `assert!(response.gates["complete"].pass);` (or an equivalent non-literal assertion) and re-run the strict `axon-api` clippy command.
title: drift: new namespaced patch test trips strict clippy bool assert lint

acceptance: Test: query_entities with an after_id that does not exist in the collection returns an error (not silently reset results). Existing pagination tests continue to pass.
description:
Review finding from fresh-eyes review.
File: crates/axon-api/src/handler.rs:271-278
Category: correctness
Severity: medium
Description: When after_id references an entity not in the filtered result set (deleted between pages, filter changed, or invalid), position().map(|i| i + 1).unwrap_or(0) silently falls back to index 0, restarting pagination from the beginning. Clients cannot distinguish page 1 from an invalid-cursor restart. This causes silent duplicate processing during paginated iteration.
Suggested fix: Return AxonError::InvalidArgument when after_id is provided but not found in the result set, or add a cursor_valid field to the response.
title: correctness: invalid cursor silently resets pagination to start

Category: drift
Severity: medium
Description: The same commit closes review-finding hx-035ebd2f by appending a PASS <measure-results> block and flipping the bead to status=closed, but it does not add a <report-summary> or other durable close summary for the governing bead itself. The commit message claims hx-035ebd2f was closed with durable PASS evidence, yet the tracker record only carries measurement data. Under the HELIX report workflow already enforced elsewhere in this repository, a closed execution bead should preserve what was done, which commit closed it, and whether follow-on work was required. Without that closure note, later tracker queries can see that hx-035ebd2f passed measurement, but they cannot recover the execution summary for the step that actually closed it. This repeats the tracker-governance drift previously flagged for other beads closed with measurement-only evidence.
Suggested fix: Append a new closed hx-035ebd2f record that preserves the current PASS <measure-results> block and adds a <report-summary> naming commit 2a33925e6a372c6bdc425816f6d8337edb1fc742, summarizing that hx-9bf2b57c was refreshed to include rust-cargo gates and that no additional follow-on work was created by the closing step itself.
